html:
How to use this guide

Read the briefing notes below before drilling the plans. They explain how the question list was generated, which characters and themes to rehearse first, what the seven-sentence body paragraph shape looks like, the three pedagogical depths Sophia threads into every Grade-9 answer, and the universal terminology you must memorise.

Overview

AQA GCSE English Literature — Leave Taking — 20 Most Likely Questions to Come Up (2026)

AQA English Literature Paper 2 — Section A (Modern Texts). 30 marks + 4 marks AO4 (SPaG). Recommended writing time: 50 minutes. AOs: AO1 (response + textual reference) / AO2 (writer's methods) / AO3 (context) / AO4 (SPaG). NO extract — questions are essay-stems only.

How this list was generated

This Q-bank was built using Sophicly's AQA-Prediction-Algorithm-the model in its new-spec adapted form (see `AQA-Prediction-Algorithm-the model.md` + `AQA-Prediction-Algorithm-an earlier model iteration.md §5` + `AQA-Prediction-Algorithm-Leave-Taking.md` in this folder). Leave Taking is a new-spec text — first taught from September 2023, first live exam summer 2025 — so only one year of past-paper data exists. The standard the model formula (Frequency × 0.35 + CycleGap × 0.25 + CoverageGap × 0.15 + unexplored-angle signal × 0.15 + core-topic boost × 0.20 − ExactAngleRepeatPenalty × 0.10) cannot run on a one-year dataset, so the algorithm substitutes the an earlier model iteration §5 sparse-data signals: SpecAOWeight × 0.30 + SpecimenPaperMatch × 0.35 + AnalogousTextPattern × 0.15 + SiblingBoardSignal × 0.10 + core-topic boost × 0.10. AQA has not published a verifiable specimen-paper question for Leave Taking, so SpecimenPaperMatch scores zero across the board. Eduqas also introduced Leave Taking in 2025 (replacing A Taste of Honey), so SiblingBoardSignal effectively scores zero as well. The two active signals are therefore the spec's named AOs and the analogous-text patterns from AQA's established Section A modern-text siblings — An Inspector Calls, Blood Brothers, Animal Farm, Lord of the Flies, and Anita and Me. The the model spine boost still applies: three core themes (Caribbean inheritance / Mother-daughter / Belonging-displacement) and three core characters (Enid / Del / Mai) each receive `core-topic boost = 1.0`. The the model fix that mattered for the 2026 Macbeth = Guilt and R&J = Conflict actuals also applies here: ExactAngleRepeatPenalty fires only at the same angle asked in 2025, not at the person or theme as a whole. The 2025 stems were Enid-as-strong-mother and difficult-older-younger relationships, so a fresh-angle Enid Q (Windrush experience rather than matriarch) and a fresh-angle generational Q (cross-generational inheritance rather than older-younger conflict) remain live candidates under the model and are restored to the top set. The Coverage Pack appendix carries the exact 2025 angles for canonical coverage only.

Note on section labels. Section IDs (C1–C10 + T1–T10) are stable character/theme IDs used throughout the cross-reference table at the foot of the document; they are NOT priority order. The priority order under the model (substitute method) is given in the tables below.

Priority order — rehearse in this order

Characters (C1–C10 in priority order):

1 — C1 (Del): MEDIUM-HIGH — Spine character + protagonist coming-of-age. Sibling pattern: AIC-Sheila pattern (Spec + 2019 + 2025) confirms AQA returns to a new text's protagonist in years 1–3. Not asked 2025.

2 — C2 (Enid (Windrush-experience angle — NOT strong-mother)): MEDIUM-HIGH — Spine character. ExactAngleRepeatPenalty avoided by switching from the 2025 strong-mother angle to the Windrush-experience angle. Run at Windrush-experience angle (NOT matriarchy framing — that is the 2025 angle, still valid as secondary).

3 — C3 (Mai): MEDIUM — Spine character + structural-Eva archetype (off-stage-vital mentor never asked). Sibling pattern: Inspector / Old Major / Nanima all rotate in years 2–3 of a new text.

4 — C4 (Brod): MEDIUM — Boxer-2021 archetype (broken working-class male figure). Sibling pattern: AF 2021 directly precedented.

5 — C5 (The female line — Enid, Mai and Del across three generations): MEDIUM — Two core characters + structural female-line argument. Sibling pattern: AIC 2023 generational frame inverted as character study.

6 — C6 (Enid and Brod as the Windrush generation (multi-char)): MEDIUM — Spine character + Windrush pair. Bypasses the 2025 Enid solo recency penalty by pairing. Sibling pattern: AIC 2025 Sheila + Gerald multi-char precedent.

7 — C7 (Mai and Enid as chosen and biological mother (multi-char)): MEDIUM-LOW — Two core characters in pair. Tests the extended-kinship structure of the diasporic household.

8 — C8 (Enid and Del's relationship (multi-char)): MEDIUM-LOW — Two core characters. Recency-bordering: Enid solo at strong-mother asked 2025, but the relationship framing is distinct and Del-anchored.

9 — C9 (Del and Viv as sisters (multi-char)): LOW-MEDIUM — Multi-char Q following AIC-2025 Sheila + Gerald precedent. Two-route response of the second generation.

10 — C10 (Viv): LOW-MEDIUM — Studious-sibling-foil archetype (Eric precedent). Less stage time than Del, harder for examiner to set without an extract.

Themes (T1–T10 in priority order):

1 — T1 (Identity, Britishness and Caribbean inheritance): MEDIUM-HIGH — Spine theme + spec's flagship AO3 anchor + never asked. AQA's Spark Conversation materials name identity as the play's headline theme.

2 — T2 (Belonging and displacement): MEDIUM-HIGH — Spine theme (belonging-displacement) + fresh angle distinct from Identity Q. The play's title-anchored question staged as the territory between two homelands.

3 — T3 (The mother-daughter relationship): MEDIUM — Spine theme + sibling pattern: BB Mothers-and-Sons, Anita Mother-Daughter, AIC 2023 women. Distinct from the 2025 older-younger framing.

4 — T4 (Cultural inheritance): MEDIUM — Spine theme (Caribbean inheritance) at the structural rather than identity-naming angle. Handwritten book / patois / Obeah practice as inheritance forms.

5 — T5 (Coming-of-age and change): MEDIUM — Sibling pattern: AIC-Sheila / Eric change angle asked 5× in 9 years; LotF loss-of-innocence; Anita coming-of-age. Distinct from 2025 generational framing.

6 — T6 (Race and racism in 1980s Britain): MEDIUM — Sibling pattern: Anita 2023 directly precedented. LT has the anchor moments (Gullyman, Viv's workplace, legislative chain).

7 — T7 (Memory, Windrush legacy and leave-taking): MEDIUM-LOW — Title-anchored theme. Strong AO3 hooks (1948 BNA, 1962 CIA, 2018 Windrush scandal). No direct sibling precedent.

8 — T8 (Religion and spiritual inheritance): MEDIUM-LOW — Spec-named, genuinely LT-specific (no sibling precedent reduces AnalogousTextPattern). Clean AO3 anchors (Obeah Act 1898, Caribbean cosmology).

9 — T9 (Family, household and the secrets the host country imposed): LOW-MEDIUM — Combines family/household frame with truth-telling. Sibling pattern: AIC family / paternal-authority gap.

10 — T10 (Generational change and cross-generational inheritance (fresh angle — NOT older-younger conflict)): LOW-MEDIUM — Spine-adjacent + fresh angle on the 2025-asked generational topic. ExactAngleRepeatPenalty avoided by switching from older-younger conflict to cross-generational inheritance.

Coverage Pack (rehearse but not top-20 under the model): Enid at the 2025 strong-mother angle, Generational at the 2025 older-younger conflict angle, Truth-telling as a standalone Q. These angles were asked recently. Rehearse them as canonical coverage and as secondary 2026 predictions — AQA's cycle suggests fresh angles are more likely but recently-asked angles can still come up.

How to use this document

Each question prints a typical AQA stem, a one-paragraph frame, a short technique gloss, and a skeleton plan in Sophicly's locked structure (Introduction 3 sentences / three Body Paragraphs of 7 sentences / Conclusion 4 sentences). If exam time is tight, rehearse the top 5 of each axis first (rows 1–5 in the tables above). For comprehensive coverage, rehearse all 20. Memorise the cross-question scene-reuse table at the end so the same scene-bank answers every Q here.

Pedagogical depth — three non-negotiables for HIGH-confidence picks

Every Grade-9 essay on Leave Taking should thread at least one of these readings into one BP or the conclusion. They are the play's deepest political moves; Sophia teaches them once so students can deploy them across any Q.

1. Enid as strong-mother archetype OR Enid as the Windrush MOTHER'S COST — the play's deepest counter-reading.

The standard reading of Enid celebrates her as the strong-mother archetype the diaspora's first generation requires: two jobs, breadwinner authority, household held together against the host country's racism. The 2025 AQA Q at the strong-mother angle confirms how readily examiners and students settle into this frame. The sharper counter-reading refuses the celebration and reads Enid inside Pinnock's critique. Enid's two jobs, her silence about Mai-in-Jamaica, her broken-speech confession at Mai's bedsit ("I have to be man and woman") name the WINDRUSH MOTHER'S COST, not her strength. The two jobs are extraction, not strength. The silence is wound, not authority. The composure she presents to Del and Viv is the labour of a woman whose own mother has been kept off-stage for eighteen years to protect the household from the truth of what migration extracted from the mother who travelled. Pinnock's deepest move is exposing what migration extracts from mothers, not what mothers contribute. The play's pedagogical pivot is from the celebratory frame (the host country's preferred image of the resilient Windrush mother) to the structural frame (the host country's invisible labour-cost). Read this way, Enid's Scene 6 broken-speech is not strength rendered articulate but cost rendered articulate; the cleaned bedsit in Scene 8 is the cost being inherited rather than the strength being transmitted. The handwritten book Del receives in Scene 8 carries both the inheritance and the record of what was paid for it. Thread this into: C2 Enid / C5 Female line / C6 Enid and Brod / C7 Mai and Enid / T2 Belonging and displacement / T3 Mother-daughter / T7 Memory and Windrush legacy / any conclusion that names the play's authorial position on the diasporic mother.

2. The Caribbean-British diaspora double consciousness — Enid's silence about Mai-in-Jamaica replicating the silence colonial Britain imposed on the Windrush generation.

Enid leaves her mother — Mai-in-Jamaica, the off-stage matriarch whose death by telephone in Scene 3 is the play's structural pivot — and across the eight scenes refuses to articulate the cost of that leaving. The silence is doubled. Privately, Enid has carried for eighteen years a suppressed truth about her own mother's reaction to her departure, the kind of silence that holds a household together at the cost of healing it. Publicly, that silence replicates the silence colonial Britain imposed on the Windrush generation: a Britain that recruited Caribbean labour between 1948 and 1971, then progressively erased the documentation of their right to remain (1962 Commonwealth Immigrants Act, 1971 Immigration Act, 1981 British Nationality Act, culminating in the 2018 Windrush deportation scandal). Del's reception arc — Scene 1 mockery, Scene 7 named gift, Scene 8 cleaned bedsit and palm-reading — is Pinnock's argument that silenced inheritance must be excavated to be claimed. Stuart Hall's Cultural Identity and Diaspora (1990) names diasporic identity as "a matter of 'becoming' as well as of 'being'"; Del's becoming is the play's structural answer to Britain's institutional forgetting. Thread this into: C1 Del / C2 Enid / C5 Female line / T1 Identity / T2 Belonging / T3 Mother-daughter / T7 Memory and Windrush legacy / any essay with a Scene 8 BP.

3. Mai's spiritual practice as alternative epistemology, NOT exotic folklore — recovery as cosmology, not curiosity.

Pinnock stages two cosmologies inside the play with equal naturalistic weight: the pastor's Scene 2 visit to Enid's living room and Mai's Obeah practice across Scenes 1, 5, 6, 7 and 8. She declines to fold one inside the other. The pastor is not framed as primary cosmology against Mai's secondary folklore — they are doubled, and Pinnock's structural refusal to subordinate Mai is itself the argument. Obeah is the Afro-Caribbean spiritual tradition that carries African religious memory through enslavement and migration; criminalised in Jamaica under the Obeah Act of 1898 (the statute remains on the books to this day) and treated by colonial Britain as superstition to be eradicated. Pinnock presents Mai's practice as an alternative epistemology — a complete system of knowing, healing and inheriting that imperial Britain attempted to erase. Mai's clairvoyant reading, Enid's consultation in Scene 6 ("I have to be man and woman"), Mai's recognition of Del's "gift of the Obeah" in Scene 7, and the closing palm-reading in Scene 8 register the epistemology working at the level of the household. The counter-reading exposes the host country's framing of Obeah as exotic spiritual practice as itself the imperial gesture — what colonial Britain attempted to erase by classification, Pinnock restores by structural equivalence with the pastor's visit. Recovering Mai's practice is therefore not the recovery of a relative but the recovery of a cosmology — an alternative system of knowing the British schooling that produced Del and Viv had taught them to refuse. The handwritten book transferred to Del in Scene 8 is body-to-body transmission of a knowledge system the host country had refused to recognise. Thread this into: C3 Mai / C1 Del / T1 Identity / T4 Cultural inheritance / T8 Religion / any essay with a Scene 8 BP.

Why this depth matters pedagogically. AQA's top-band descriptor asks for "a critical, exploratory, conceptualised response". A reading that locates the play's argument at the level of imperial epistemology and post-colonial inheritance — that names the Windrush MOTHER'S COST rather than celebrating the strong-mother archetype — and that traces the formal mechanism (eight-scene cyclical structure, Mai's bedsit at open and close, the handwritten book as material vehicle) is the species of conceptualisation Examiner Reports name as Grade 9. Without it, a Del essay is sympathetic coming-of-age; with it, the essay is a post-colonial argument about inheritance traced to its dramatic device.

A universal critical-angle method. Every text in the AQA spec rewards the same move: identify the standard reading; then identify a counter-reading the same evidence supports; then read the two together as the species of conceptualisation Grade 9 demands. For Leave Taking the dual readings are strong-mother/Windrush MOTHER'S COST (Enid), exotic-folklore/alternative-cosmology (Mai's Obeah practice), individual-becoming/structural-inheritance (Del). Sophia should generate the second reading whenever helping a student plan — the standard reading is what most students will write; the second reading is what carries them past the band-9 line.

The seven-sentence body paragraph shape

1. Topic sentence — concept only, no techniques named.

2. Technique + evidence — one quote with multiple rhetorical techniques visible inside it.

3. Close analysis — grammar, word-class, syntax, and one further observation about the same quote.

4. Effect on audience 1 — what the audience sees, hears, feels.

5. Effect on audience 2 — what the audience recognises, registers, infers.

6. Author's purpose — what Pinnock may be arguing through this scene.

7. Context (AO3) — one period-specific fact that lands the BP back at the controlling concept.

Memorise these once

Leave Taking argues that the Caribbean diaspora's second generation inherits a mother's exile without inheriting her homeland — that silenced Caribbean inheritance must be claimed across three generations or lost — and that the cost of leave-taking is paid not by the generation that left but by the generation that was never asked. Pinnock writes for two audiences at once: the Windrush mothers who must break the silences the host country imposed on them, and the British-born daughters who must claim the inheritance the host country taught them to refuse. The play is set in 1980s London against the recent backdrop of Thatcher Britain's 1981 British Nationality Act and the cumulative hostile-environment legislation (1962 CIA / 1971 IA / 1981 BNA) that progressively eroded the Windrush generation's right to remain. The closing image — Del reading her mother's palm inside Mai's living room — is the controlling concept staged as one quiet physical action: healing across the diasporic mother-daughter line requires the trained daughter to make a room inside which the mother's silenced truths can finally be spoken.

Twelve canonical quotes — memorise verbatim, redeploy across the bank. Each anchors three or more Qs.

Enid (Windrush mother)

• "You come here, you try to fit in. Stick to the rules. England been good to me. I proud a my English girls." (Scene 2, p.29) — assimilationist credo; reuse for C2, C5, C6, C8, T1, T2, T6, T7, CP1.

• "I work two jobs seven days a week to put food in you belly and roof over you head. I wear one dress, one pair a shoes with hole in it so that you can dress like those children who have fathers." (Scene 2, p.33) — the defining sacrifice speech; reuse for C2, C5, C7, C8, T3, T9, CP1.

• "'Mooma,' I say, 'I gone now.' She never turn round, jus' carry on working, chop chop chop, play deaf. In the end I had to give up, walk away. (Slight pause) It wasn't easy to leave." (Scene 4, pp.41–43) — the play's title-image of leave-taking refused; reuse for C2, C5, C7, C8, T3, T7, CP1, CP3.

• "How you going teach you children that they don't exist?…You say I don't see how them treat you out there. I see it. I see it and it make me want to tear the place down. I would chop off my hand if it would help you." (Scene 8, p.72) — the climactic confession; reuse for C2, C5, C8, T1, T3, T6, T9, CP1, CP3.

Del (second-generation daughter)

• "But what you give us that we can use out there? You don't see the police vans hunting us down, or the managers who treat us like we're the lowest of the low. You're too busy bowing and scraping to your beloved England." (Scene 2, p.34) — Del's indictment of assimilation; reuse for C1, C5, C8, C9, T1, T2, T6, CP2.

• "All I did last night was dance… The bass is mad. You wanna see it pounding the walls, like one big pulsing heart. When that bass gets inside you and flings you round the room you can't do nothing to stop it." (Scene 2, p.33) — Del's claim to Black British youth culture; reuse for C1, C9, T1, T2, T5.

• "She hates me. Always has, ever since I was a little kid. She thinks I'm bad." (Scene 6) — Del's anchor on the mother-daughter wound; reuse for C1, C8, T3, CP2.

Brod (Windrush man)

• "All my life I think of meself as a British subject, wave a flag on Empire Day, touch me hat whenever me see a picture a the queen. Then them send me letter say if me don't get me nationality paper in order they going kick me outta the country." (Scene 2, p.27) — Brod's foundational Windrush speech; reuse for C4, C6, T1, T6, T7, T9.

• "These girls ain't English like them newsreader who got English stamp on them like the letters on a stick a rock, right through English. These girls got Caribbean souls." (Scene 2, p.29) — Brod's defining argument for Caribbean heritage; reuse for C4, C6, C10, T1, T4, T10.

• "…you father change from a smiling boy into a hard man… If they won't treat him like a human being outside, him make sure she treat him like a king in him own house… no one care what he want to do to a black woman." (Scene 7, p.62) — Brod's account of how workplace racism deformed Enid's husband into a domestic abuser; reuse for C4, C6, C8, T3, T6, T9, CP3.

Mai (Obeah practitioner, chosen mother)

• "It not easy to turn you back on one country and start fresh in foreign. It mash up you life." (Scene 6, p.56) — Mai's diagnosis of migration's psychological cost; reuse for C3, C7, T1, T2, T7, T8.

• "You have a liking for books, but when you read words run across the page like black ants. The teachers say you slow, so you give up and run with a crowd who make you feel like you belong… but just recently you had a change of heart." (Scene 5, p.47) — Mai's clairvoyant naming of Del's dyslexia; reuse for C1, C3, C7, T4, T5, T8, T10.

Viv (studious-sibling foil)

• "Oh, I knew all the answers. Pat me on the head and they all come tumbling out, say exactly what the examiners want to hear. But no matter how hard I search for myself in them books, I'm never there… Things I feel they haven't got words for. I need another language to express myself." (Scene 5, p.53) — Viv's central rejection of the Euro-centric curriculum; reuse for C9, C10, T1, T4, T5, T6, T10.

Key terms:

• Diaspora — a people dispersed from their homeland; here, the Caribbean migration to Britain after 1948 and the family it produced inside Enid's North London living room.

• Patois — Jamaican English variety with its own grammar and phonology; the speech of the Windrush generation in the play (Enid, Brod, Mai), set against the south-east London speech of Del and Viv.

• Obeah — the Afro-Caribbean spiritual tradition carrying African religious memory through enslavement and migration; criminalised in Jamaica under the Obeah Act of 1898; treated by Pinnock with the same naturalistic seriousness she gives the pastor's visit.

• Naturalism — late-nineteenth-century dramatic tradition (Ibsen, Chekhov) in which character is the product of social conditions and dialogue carries the action; Pinnock extends it for Black British theatre.

• Episodic structure — a play built of multiple discrete scenes (eight here) rather than a tightly continuous three-act arc; allows Del's pregnancy to advance, a grandmother to die off-stage, a household to drift apart at the pace of family life.

• Cultural-inheritance ritual — the heightened device (Mai's Obeah readings, the handwritten book, the closing palm-reading) Pinnock holds inside naturalistic staging so the inheritance is taken seriously rather than folklorised.

Three context factors that travel across all 20 questions (full version at the foot of the document):

1. The Windrush Era + 1948 British Nationality Act. The Windrush generation arrived 1948–1971 carrying British passports under the 1948 BNA. "Most Caribbean migrants arrived on British-subject passports the 1962 Act retrospectively voided — that documentary erasure became the 2018 Windrush scandal." The 1962 Commonwealth Immigrants Act introduced employment vouchers; the 1971 Immigration Act made "patriality" the test for unrestricted entry; the 1981 British Nationality Act stripped the automatic citizenship rights the 1948 Act had granted.

2. 1981 Britain. The 1981 British Nationality Act passed in the same year as the Brixton uprising (10–12 April), the Toxteth riots (July), and the New Cross fire (18 January, in which thirteen young Black people died). The play premiered 11 November 1987 inside the cultural moment those events forced into public visibility — the formation of Black British identity as a distinct political and aesthetic category.

3. Obeah as suppressed Caribbean cosmology. Criminalised under the Jamaican Obeah Act 1898 (imprisonment with hard labour for practitioners; the statute remains on the Jamaican books to this day). The practice survived through clandestine transmission across enslavement and migration. Pinnock has framed Obeah in interview as "traces of African spiritual rituals preserved by clandestine practices" — a religious inheritance carrying the memory of an African heritage that enslavement attempted to erase.

Top 10 Character Questions

Most likely AQA Modern Text character questions, ordered by examiner-prediction confidence. Each carries one ready-to-rehearse plan with seven-sentence body paragraphs and an empty response area to draft against.

C1 — Del

Question

How does Pinnock present Del and her change in Leave Taking?

Write about:

- how Pinnock presents Del at different points in the play

- how Pinnock uses Del's change to convey her ideas.

[30 marks + 4 AO4 = 34 marks]

Frame. Del is Pinnock's most complete dramatisation of the change the diaspora's second generation must undergo — a young woman who travels from defensive mockery, through painful recognition, to trained reception of the inheritance she had refused. The plan tracks three stages — Scene 2 dismissal of her mother's assimilationist credo, Scene 5 Mai's clairvoyant reading naming her dyslexia, Scene 8 cleaned bedsit and palm-reading of her own mother.

Pedagogical depth to thread. The Caribbean-British double-consciousness reading (see top of document). Del's reception arc dramatises the play's argument that silenced inheritance must be excavated to be claimed. Land this in BP3 or the conclusion at minimum.

Technique gloss:

• Stage business — wordless physical action carrying meaning (the stolen charm in Scene 1; the kicked wall in Scene 7; the cleaned bedsit in Scene 8).

• Recurring object — an object that returns across scenes with cumulative meaning (Mai's notebook of Obeah practice).

• Anagnorisis — Aristotelian moment of recognition; Mai's "And now I see it plain plain" (Scene 7) registers Del's inheritance.

• Coming-of-age / Bildungsroman — the protagonist moves from innocence through experience to maturity.

• Cultural-inheritance ritual — the heightened device (Obeah practice) Pinnock holds inside naturalism.

• Cyclical structure — the play's Scene 8 returns to Mai's bedsit (the Scene 1 setting), now cleaned and inherited.

• Hook (historical fact): The Brixton uprising of April 1981 and the New Cross fire of January 1981 — in which thirteen young Black people died — pushed Black British identity into public visibility for the first time, and Winsome Pinnock, writing Leave Taking in 1987 at the age of twenty-three, made that emergent identity the inheritance her British-born protagonist Del has to learn to receive.

• Building sentence (historical context): Born in Islington in 1961 to Jamaican parents who arrived in the Windrush years and trained at the Royal Court Young Writers' Group, Pinnock writes inside the cumulative legislative chain (1962 Commonwealth Immigrants Act, 1971 Immigration Act, 1981 British Nationality Act) that eroded the older generation's right to remain — the political pressure shaping Del's south-east London childhood.

• Thesis: Across her Scene 2 demolition of Enid's assimilationist credo ("You're too busy bowing and scraping to your beloved England"), her Scene 5 acceptance of Mai's clairvoyant reading of her dyslexia ("words run across the page like black ants"), and her Scene 8 cleaning of Mai's bedsit, receipt of the notebook, and palm-reading of her own mother, Pinnock presents Del as the figure through whom the diasporic second generation's coming-of-age becomes possible.

Scene focus: Enid's North London living room; Del returns in her work uniform having stayed out at the sound system and lost her job; the argument with Enid escalates; Del's hospital-party speech ends with Enid's slap.

• Topic sentence: Pinnock first presents Del as a British-born daughter whose anger is the precise instrument that names the cost her mother has been refusing to register, the second generation's voice installed before the journey is staged.

• Technique + evidence: Pinnock deploys direct address inside accumulating imagery — Del's indictment "But what you give us that we can use out there? You don't see the police vans hunting us down, or the managers who treat us like we're the lowest of the low. You're too busy bowing and scraping to your beloved England" — combining direct address (the second-person "you" turning the mother into the speech's defendant) with the police-vans / managers / bowing-and-scraping triad that names institutional racism, workplace racism and assimilationist posture in three concrete images.

• Close analysis: The participles "hunting" and "bowing and scraping" turn institutions into agents and Enid into a posture, the grammar enacting Del's argument that the host country has produced her mother's deference rather than rewarded it.

• Effect on audience 1: The audience hears in the south-east London register Del uses against Enid's patois the play's central translation problem — the second-generation daughter speaks the host country's English at the very mother whose silence the host country has imposed.

• Effect on audience 2: The audience registers the second generation's diagnostic clarity — Del names institutional, workplace and posture-level racism in a single breath, the very analysis Enid has been working too hard to refuse.

• Author's purpose: Pinnock honours Del by giving her the play's first analytical voice, dramatising her conviction that the British-born daughter has registered what the Windrush mother cannot afford to name, and that the daughter's anger is a form of seeing rather than a failure of love.

• Connective: That seeing, however, has not yet been handed an inheritance through which to channel itself — and Scene 5 stages the first moment another woman recognises in Del something Del herself has never been told she carries.

Scene focus: Mai's bedsit, a few weeks later; Del has moved in with Mai, pregnant and refusing Roy; Mai conducts a clairvoyant reading of Del during which she names the dyslexia the British school system failed to diagnose.

• Topic sentence: Where Scene 2 had installed Del's voice as indictment, Scene 5 stages her first reception of being seen — the older Caribbean-born practitioner reading her in vocabulary the British classroom never reached for.

• Technique + evidence: Pinnock deploys extended simile inside the consultation form — Mai's "You have a liking for books, but when you read words run across the page like black ants. The teachers say you slow, so you give up and run with a crowd who make you feel like you belong" — combining the "black ants" simile (visual concrete imagery for the perceptual difficulty no teacher reached) with the diagnostic chain (the liking for books, the visual breakdown, the school's mislabel, the substitute community) that converts a school file into a portrait the daughter recognises.

• Close analysis: The verb "run" gives agency to the words rather than to the failing reader, while "black ants" compresses the smallness, the swarm and the breaking-apart of letters into one specific image the British curriculum had no language for.

• Effect on audience 1: The audience watches Del receive a reading her school never gave her — the practice she had once mocked is the one room in which her perceptual experience is finally rendered legible.

• Effect on audience 2: The audience registers the room's political weight — a Caribbean spiritual practice criminalised under colonial law produces the diagnosis the post-war British state has failed to provide.

• Author's purpose: Pinnock argues here that Caribbean spiritual practice is alternative epistemology, not exotic folklore — a complete system of seeing that names the British-born daughter where the British school had only mislabelled her.

• Connective: Once recognised, Del begins the apprenticeship Scene 7's wall-kick and Mai's "And now I see it plain plain" will confirm, and Scene 8 will complete.

Scene focus: Mai's living room, now cleaned by Del; Mai gives Del her notebook ("I don't have a daughter to pass them on to"); Enid arrives for a reading; Del reads her own mother's palm in the play's closing image.

• Topic sentence: Where Scene 5 had named the inheritance, Scene 8 stages its full reception — Del now trained, the room now cleaned, the notebook now hers, and her mother seated in front of her as the client of a practice the daughter had once dismissed.

• Technique + evidence: Pinnock deploys cyclical structure inside recurring object — the same Mai's bedsit described in the Scene 1 stage direction as "Very messy" now appears cleaned by Del ("First time this place's been cleaned in years"), with Mai handing Del her notebook ("Here, this is for you") and naming the inheritance gap ("I don't have a daughter to pass them on to").

• Close analysis: The unchanged room measures the changed daughter — what looked like a fading practice in Scene 1 has, by Scene 8, gained the practitioner it required — while the notebook insists that the transmission is hand-to-hand rather than abstract, the older generation's writing passing into the younger generation's keeping.

• Effect on audience 1: The audience watches a daughter's hand close on a Caribbean spiritual notebook the British state had criminalised, and registers Pinnock's argument that the inheritance survives by being received rather than preserved.

• Effect on audience 2: The audience receives the closing palm-reading — Enid finally permitted to confess "How you going teach you children that they don't exist?" and "I would chop off my hand if it would help you" — as the healing the trained daughter has earned the right to host.

• Author's purpose: Pinnock makes her clearest argument here — that healing across the diasporic mother-daughter line requires the trained daughter to make a room (Mai's cleaned bedsit, Del's reading hand) inside which the Windrush mother's silenced truths can finally be spoken aloud.

• Connective: Obeah was criminalised in Jamaica from 1760 (the statute remained on the books at the time of writing); Del's receipt of Mai's notebook is one such transmission staged, the protagonist's coming-of-age made structurally identical with the survival of a cosmology the imperial state had tried to erase.

• Restated thesis: In conclusion, Pinnock traces Del's journey from Scene 2 indictment, through Scene 5 reception, to Scene 8 practice — the diasporic second generation's coming-of-age in dramatic miniature.

• Controlling concept: What Scene 2 installed as refused inheritance the daughter could only diagnose from outside, Scene 5 named as inheritance she could begin to receive, Scene 8 confirmed as inheritance she could host — three stages of the same arc, the consistency of the journey across the eight scenes confirming that change in the second generation is real and that the cost of leave-taking can begin to be paid back.

• Author's purpose: Writing for a 1987 audience inside the cultural emergence of Black British identity, Pinnock embodies in Del her conviction that the second generation can claim what the host country taught them to refuse, and that the claiming is the precondition for healing across the female line.

• Ultimate moral: Del's enduring power as a theatrical creation is that her arc is the audience's arc — the daughter who once dismissed Mai's craft as "Mumbo-jumbo nonsense" has, by the closing palm-reading, become the room inside which the Windrush mother can finally speak.

C2 — Enid (Windrush-experience angle, NOT strong-mother)

Question

How does Pinnock use Enid to explore the experience of the Windrush generation in Leave Taking?

Write about:

- how Pinnock presents Enid

- how Pinnock uses Enid to explore the experience of the Windrush generation.

[30 marks + 4 AO4 = 34 marks]

Frame. Enid is Pinnock's portrait of the Windrush experience as cost-not-celebration — the assimilationist credo ("England been good to me") delivered in Scene 2 carries a hidden price; the Scene 4 grief for the homeland mother Enid could not say goodbye to names that price; the Scene 8 "How you going teach you children that they don't exist?" speech finally articulates what eighteen years of leave-taking extracted. The plan tracks three stages of that diagnosis — Scene 2 assimilationist credo + the 1948 BNA / 1962 CIA / 1971 IA / 1981 BNA legislative chain, Scene 4 unfinished farewell to Mooma and the redirected inheritance, Scene 8 broken-speech confession at Mai's bedsit. Required framing. This Q runs Enid at the Windrush-experience angle — NOT at the 2025 strong-mother / matriarch angle, which the ExactAngleRepeatPenalty rules out. The Scene 2 BP1 emphasis is therefore migration cost, NOT maternal authority.

Pedagogical depth to thread. The Windrush MOTHER'S COST reading and the Caribbean-British double-consciousness reading (see top of document). Enid's silence about her own farewell to Mooma replicates colonial Britain's silence about the Windrush generation; Scene 4 stages that doubled silence and Scene 8 begins to crack it. Thread into BP2 or BP3 at minimum.

Technique gloss:

• Jamaican patois — the speech the Windrush generation brought; phoneme-level evidence of an inheritance the host country tried to override.

• Asyndetic list — list without conjunctions ("Roast breadfruit. Callaloo").

• Unfinished sentence as grief-marker — Pinnock's notation of bereaved syntax ("I want… I want to go home"; "someone to gather… gather me up").

• Onomatopoeic repetition — Mooma's "chop chop chop" as the sound of refused leave-taking.

• Motif — food, weariness, the telephone from Jamaica.

• Stage direction — Mai's bedsit as Enid's only confessional space.

• Hook (historical fact): The Empire Windrush docked at Tilbury on 22 June 1948, the same year the National Health Service was founded; Britain recruited Caribbean cleaners and nurses on British-subject passports under the 1948 British Nationality Act, then progressively voided those passports through the 1962 Commonwealth Immigrants Act, the 1971 Immigration Act and the 1981 British Nationality Act — a legislative chain whose furthest extreme became the 2018 Windrush deportation scandal Pinnock's own essay later cited as the echo of Brod's Scene 2 speech.

• Building sentence (historical context): Winsome Pinnock, born 1961 in Islington to Jamaican parents who arrived in those Windrush years, wrote Leave Taking in 1987 at twenty-three inside the Royal Court Young Writers' Group, deliberately making a Windrush hospital cleaner — Enid — the lead of a British play for the first time and dramatising the legislative chain as a single woman's eighteen-year silence.

• Thesis: Across her Scene 2 assimilationist credo ("England been good to me. I proud a my English girls") whose £50 "nationality secure" footnote registers the Windrush price already paid, her Scene 4 grieving recall of Mooma's "chop chop chop" farewell-refusal that names what the leave-taking actually cost, and her Scene 8 broken-speech confession ("How you going teach you children that they don't exist?") at Mai's bedsit, Pinnock presents Enid as the figure through whom the Windrush experience is rendered as cost rather than celebrated as strength.

Scene focus: Enid's North London living room; Brod's Empire Day / "nationality paper" speech; Enid's "England been good to me" defence; the £50 "nationality secure" exchange; Del's homecoming and her demolition of the credo.

• Topic sentence: Pinnock first presents Enid as a Windrush migrant whose loyalty to the host country is the form of a price already paid, the assimilationist credo installed as the position the play's evidence will progressively cost.

• Technique + evidence: Pinnock deploys declarative parallelism inside ironic juxtaposition — Enid's "You come here, you try to fit in. Stick to the rules. England been good to me. I proud a my English girls" set against the £50 footnote "So, we pay the fifty pound and now we nationality secure" — combining the clipped declaratives (the migrant's pared-down credo, each clause a rule the host country has imposed) with the ironic juxtaposition of the credo and the cash transaction (the "English girls" phrase landing alongside the literal price of citizenship).

• Close analysis: The patois grammar of "England been good to me" and "I proud a my English girls" registers Enid as Caribbean even in the act of declaring herself British, while the £50 footnote converts the credo into a receipt — citizenship paid for in cash by people whose British-subject passports the 1962 Act had already devalued.

• Effect on audience 1: The audience hears the credo and the £50 in the same scene, and recognises Pinnock's argument that the host country has extracted the price of citizenship from the very generation it had recruited to fill its post-war labour shortages.

• Effect on audience 2: The audience registers Enid's daughters as the inheritance the credo is meant to secure ("I proud a my English girls") — the host country's reward for the leave-taking the legislative chain has converted into a contingency.

• Author's purpose: Pinnock argues here that the Windrush experience is the experience of a paid-for belonging the host country can revoke, and that Enid's loyalty is therefore not assimilation rewarded but assimilation extracted under the terms the 1962, 1971 and 1981 Acts progressively dictated.

• Connective: Scene 4's grieving recall of Mooma will name the further price that £50 receipt never bought back — the homeland mother whose silence at Enid's leaving has been carried in private for eighteen years.

Scene focus: Enid sits in the dark drinking on the sofa after Cynthia's call has confirmed Mooma's death; Viv listens; Enid recalls the day she left for England; she forces a saved building-society book on Viv; the scene closes on "I want… I want to go home."

• Topic sentence: Where Scene 2 had installed the assimilationist credo, Scene 4 strips it to its underlying cost — the leave-taking the play's title names, recalled in the very vocabulary the homeland mother used to refuse it.

• Technique + evidence: Pinnock deploys onomatopoeic repetition inside unfinished-sentence syntax — Enid's "'Mooma,' I say, 'I gone now.' She never turn round, jus' carry on working, chop chop chop, play deaf. In the end I had to give up, walk away. (Slight pause.) It wasn't easy to leave" — combining onomatopoeia (the cutlass-stroke "chop chop chop" sounding the refusal that the daughter could not get a goodbye through) with unfinished-sentence syntax (the "(Slight pause.)" and the closing scene's "I want… I want to go home" notating bereaved speech).

• Close analysis: The triple "chop" gives Mooma's labour a sound and Enid's leave-taking a percussion, the cutlass speaking instead of the mother; the closing pause-broken "I want… I want to go home" registers a homeland the legislative chain and the funeral she could not attend have placed permanently beyond return.

• Effect on audience 1: The audience hears the play's title-image realised — "leave taking" is not a one-time act in 1959 but a sound that has been repeating in Enid's head for eighteen years, the homeland reduced to the noise of refused farewell.

• Effect on audience 2: The audience watches Enid press a building-society book on Viv — the saved Caribbean inheritance redirected into a London university place — and registers Pinnock's argument that the Windrush inheritance has had to be re-directed because the original homeland inheritance the daughter left behind cannot be returned to.

• Author's purpose: Pinnock argues here that the Windrush experience is the experience of an inheritance the host country forced the migrant to redirect — from Mooma's cutlass and Mooma's chocolate tea to a building-society book and an English exam — and that the redirection is itself the cost of leave-taking the credo of Scene 2 had refused to name.

• Connective: Scene 8 will gather the unfinished sentence of Scene 4 into the bigger broken-speech confession at Mai's bedsit, and will let the daughter Enid handed the money to walk into Black Studies while the daughter she had given up on holds her mother's hand.

Scene focus: Mai's living room, now cleaned by Del; Enid arrives for a reading; Del reads her own mother's palm; Enid's late confession breaks the eighteen-year silence ("Nobody see you, nobody hear you"; "How you going teach you children that they don't exist?"; "I would chop off my hand if it would help you"; "I been fighting too long, Del").

• Topic sentence: Where Scene 4 had revealed the cost of the leave-taking, Scene 8 stages its possible repair — the Windrush mother finally permitted, by her trained daughter, to articulate the suppressed truths the host country had imposed on her.

• Technique + evidence: Pinnock deploys unfinished-sentence syntax inside doubled negation — Enid's "Nobody see you, nobody hear you … All the time you screaming, you screaming inside but nobody come" leading to "How you going teach you children that they don't exist? … I see it. I see it and it make me want to tear the place down. I would chop off my hand if it would help you" — combining the anaphoric doubled negation ("nobody see you, nobody hear you"; "I see it. I see it") with the unfinished-sentence closing of the same speech ("someone to gather… gather me up") so the confession arrives in clauses too painful to complete.

• Close analysis: The doubled "I see it" repairs the doubled "nobody see you" — eighteen years of host-country invisibility answered by a Windrush mother finally testifying that she has been watching her daughter's reality all along — while the conditional "I would chop off my hand if it would help you" converts a Caribbean idiom of total maternal sacrifice into the play's most painful image of the cost-already-paid.

• Effect on audience 1: The audience hears a Windrush mother finally permitted, by her trained daughter, to articulate what eighteen years of host-country suppression had kept unsaid — the speech delivered inside Mai's cleaned bedsit rather than Enid's living room, the cultural-inheritance space rather than the family-assimilation space.

• Effect on audience 2: The audience receives the unfinished closing image "someone to gather… gather me up and touch my cheek like I was a prize, not a curse, and stroke my hair like Mooma never could" as the Windrush mother asking, for the first time in the play, to be mothered.

• Author's purpose: Pinnock argues here that the Windrush experience produced a silence the legislative chain and the host country's invisibility-of-Black-women combined to enforce, and that the silence can crack only inside a room the older Caribbean generation built and the younger British-born generation has been trained to host.

• Connective: Pinnock's 2018 essay later named the Windrush deportation scandal as the historical echo of Brod's Scene 2 nationality speech; Enid's Scene 8 "Nobody see you, nobody hear you" is the female-line counterpart, the host country's invisibility of the migrant mother registered thirty years before the scandal made it news.

• Restated thesis: In conclusion, Pinnock constructs Enid as a comprehensive portrait of the Windrush experience as cost rather than celebration — Scene 2 assimilationist credo with the £50 receipt attached, Scene 4 onomatopoeic "chop chop chop" farewell-refusal and redirected inheritance, Scene 8 broken-speech confession that finally names the host country's invisibility.

• Controlling concept: What Scene 2 installed as a paid-for belonging, Scene 4 named as an unfinished mourning of a homeland that could not be returned to, Scene 8 began to repair through the daughter's trained reception — three stages of the same diagnosis, the legislative chain (1948 / 1962 / 1971 / 1981) made visible at the level of one woman's eighteen-year silence.

• Author's purpose: Writing in 1987 inside the Royal Court Young Writers' Group, Pinnock honoured a Windrush hospital cleaner who had never been the lead in a British play before, refusing both saint and martyr and giving the audience an ordinary woman whose two jobs, redirected inheritance, and finally-spoken sentences became the indictment.

• Ultimate moral: Enid's enduring power as a theatrical creation is that the closing image — the unfinished "gather me up" lifted by Del's reading hand — names the Windrush experience as a leave-taking the female line is now beginning to take back.

C3 — Mai

Question

How does Pinnock use the character of Mai to explore ideas about cultural inheritance in Leave Taking?

Write about:

- how Pinnock presents Mai

- how Pinnock uses Mai to explore ideas about cultural inheritance.

[30 marks + 4 AO4 = 34 marks]

Frame. Mai is Pinnock's mentor-figure and the play's living emblem of Afro-Caribbean spiritual inheritance — an older Jamaican woman whose Deptford bedsit, weary body, and small client list dramatise how narrowly the practice has had to survive in 1980s Britain, and whose notebook of Obeah practice is the inheritance the play argues must be passed on. The plan tracks three stages — Scene 1 weary practitioner introducing herself as a reader of the soul, Scene 5 clairvoyant reading naming Del's dyslexia, Scene 8 handover of the notebook with "You passed with flying colours. You're a natural."

Pedagogical depth to thread. The Mai-as-alternative-epistemology reading (see top of document). Mai's practice is staged as a complete system of knowing, NOT as exotic folklore; recovering Mai is recovering the Caribbean cosmology imperial Britain attempted to erase. Thread into BP1 or BP3.

Technique gloss:

• Naturalism with cultural-inheritance ritual — Mai's Obeah practice held inside naturalistic staging so the inheritance is dignified rather than folklorised.

• Mentor / catalyst figure — Mai conducts the readings, sets the apprenticeship, hands the notebook.

• Recurring object — Mai's notebook of Obeah practice as the inheritance in material form.

• Stage direction — Mai's "Very messy" bedsit (Scene 1) and its cleaned state (Scene 8) as silent characterisation.

• Extended simile — Mai's "black ants" simile (Scene 5) reading Del's dyslexia.

• Patois aphorism — Mai's diagnostic compressions ("It mash up you life"; "Is people I deal in").

• Hook (historical fact): Obeah was criminalised in Jamaica from 1760 — originally punishable by death, later by flogging and imprisonment — and remained illegal there when Leave Taking premiered in 1987; Winsome Pinnock's staging of an older Caribbean-born practitioner in a Deptford bedsit is the literary answer to more than two centuries of colonial suppression.

• Building sentence (historical context): Born 1961 in Islington to Jamaican parents who arrived in the Windrush years and trained at the Royal Court Young Writers' Group, Pinnock places Mai inside the institutional void left by 1980s NHS services that pathologised Caribbean-British women's suffering, refusing to fold Obeah inside the pastor's Christian register and staging the two cosmologies side by side with equal weight.

• Thesis: Across her Scene 1 self-introduction "Mai see right through to your soul" in the "Very messy" Deptford bedsit, her Scene 5 clairvoyant reading of Del ("words run across the page like black ants") that names the dyslexia the British classroom missed, and her Scene 8 handover of the notebook ("I don't have a daughter to pass them on to") followed by "You passed with flying colours. You're a natural", Pinnock presents Mai as the figure through whom Afro-Caribbean spiritual inheritance survives the colonial criminalisation, the migration, and the host country's classifications that combined to threaten it.

Scene focus: Mai's Deptford bedsit — "Very messy. The table centre stage is covered in papers, playing cards scattered all over"; Mai slumped in the armchair drinking stout; Enid arrives with Del and Viv for a reading; Mai introduces herself.

• Topic sentence: Pinnock first presents Mai as a practitioner whose authority is the form of her sight, the older Caribbean woman installing the practice as a complete system of knowing before the play has staged a single client.

• Technique + evidence: Pinnock deploys patois aphorism inside stage-direction setting — Mai's "Unno scared a me? Is so young people does scared a obeah woman. Can't keep any secret from Mai. Mai see right through to your soul" spoken inside the "Very messy" bedsit whose table holds "papers, playing cards scattered all over, a glass of water and the remains of a half-burnt white candle" — combining the patois aphorism (the third-person "Mai see" declarative converting the practitioner's name into the seeing-instrument itself) with the stage-direction setting (the papers, cards and half-burnt candle staging Obeah as accumulated working knowledge, not as theatrical prop).

• Close analysis: The third-person self-naming "Mai see right through to your soul" registers the practitioner as the instrument of the practice — the patois grammar refusing the host country's "I see" — while the "half-burnt" candle places the room inside a working continuity rather than a tableau set for the audience.

• Effect on audience 1: The audience hears Mai's self-naming as the older Caribbean woman's claim on a vocabulary the host country had criminalised — "obeah woman" spoken without apology, the word the law had punished installed as the name of the practitioner.

• Effect on audience 2: The audience registers the practice's working condition through the room — the papers and scattered cards a record of the clientele the practice has carried, the "half-burnt" candle a sign that the working is continuous rather than ceremonial.

• Author's purpose: Pinnock argues here that Caribbean spiritual practice is alternative epistemology — a complete system of seeing the host country had attempted to erase by classification — and that Mai's self-introduction is the cosmology naming itself before any client has reached the chair.

• Connective: Scene 5 will stage the system at work — the practitioner reading the British-born daughter in vocabulary the British school never reached for.

Scene focus: Mai's bedsit a few weeks later; Del has moved in; Mai takes Del's hand and conducts a clairvoyant reading; Mai names the dyslexia Del's school had labelled as "slow".

• Topic sentence: Where Scene 1 had installed the practitioner as the instrument of seeing, Scene 5 stages the practice doing the institutional work the British state had failed to provide.

• Technique + evidence: Pinnock deploys extended simile inside the consultation form — Mai's "You have a liking for books, but when you read words run across the page like black ants. The teachers say you slow, so you give up and run with a crowd who make you feel like you belong. At least that's what you used to think, but just recently you have had a change of heart" — combining the "black ants" simile (the visual concrete imagery for letters that move and swarm, naming dyslexia in vocabulary the post-war British school never owned) with the diagnostic chain (the liking for books, the perceptual difficulty, the school's mislabel, the substitute community, the recent turn) that builds a portrait the daughter recognises.

• Close analysis: The verb "run" gives the words agency rather than the failing reader, while "black ants" compresses smallness, swarm and breaking-apart into one image, the host country's classification "slow" exposed as misreading; the "change of heart" clause then turns the diagnosis into the start of an apprenticeship rather than a label.

• Effect on audience 1: The audience watches Caribbean spiritual practice produce the diagnosis post-war British education had refused to provide — Mai's hand on Del's hand reading what no teacher's marker had reached.

• Effect on audience 2: The audience registers Mai's gift as the older Caribbean woman holding a complete system of knowing the host country had reclassified as superstition, the practice doing institutional labour the host country had abandoned to it.

• Author's purpose: Pinnock argues here that recovery of Mai's practice is recovery of cosmology — the room in which the British-born daughter can finally be seen is the room the colonial state had spent two centuries trying to close.

• Connective: Scene 7 will see the gift register again ("And now I see it plain plain") and Scene 8 will stage the inheritance handed over.

Scene focus: Mai's cleaned bedsit; Mai puts Del through her "Clairvoyance and Spiritual Aptitude" exam ("You passed with flying colours. You're a natural"); Mai finds her notebook under a cushion and gives it to Del ("Here, this is for you"; "I don't have a daughter to pass them on to"); Enid arrives for a reading.

• Topic sentence: Where Scene 5 had named Del's gift, Scene 8 stages the inheritance's full transfer — Mai handing the working notebook to her trained successor at the moment the daughter is ready to read on her own.

• Technique + evidence: Pinnock deploys recurring object inside the dialogue-paired stage direction — "MAI hands DEL the notebook. … I don't have a daughter to pass them on to. And my son... well, he isn't interested" — combining recurring object (the notebook as the inheritance in material form, the hand-written record of a practice the colonial state had criminalised) with the candid disclosure (the absent son, the absent daughter) that registers the inheritance as a chosen-family transmission rather than a biological one.

• Close analysis: The verb "hands" in the stage direction makes the inheritance hand-to-hand and present-tense, while "I don't have a daughter to pass them on to" names the gap the practice has been waiting for the host country to leave open; Pinnock fills the gap with Del.

• Effect on audience 1: The audience watches a Caribbean spiritual notebook the British state had criminalised pass from the hand that has carried it for decades to the hand of the British-born daughter the host country had taught to refuse it.

• Effect on audience 2: The audience receives Mai's "You passed with flying colours. You're a natural" not as flattery but as the formal naming of an apprenticeship completed; the practitioner has tested, recognised, and now hands.

• Author's purpose: Pinnock argues here that cultural inheritance survives by being received rather than preserved, and that Mai's act of giving the notebook is the dignified form of trust the moment of transfer requires — chosen-family transmission keeping alive the cosmology that biological transmission could not.

• Connective: Leave Taking premiered on 11 November 1987 at the Liverpool Playhouse Studio and was revived at the Bush Theatre in 2018 — the revival opening weeks after the Guardian's reporting on the Windrush deportation scandal, Mai's role registering anew as the keeper of an inheritance the host country was actively threatening.

• Restated thesis: In conclusion, Pinnock constructs Mai as the play's living emblem of Afro-Caribbean spiritual inheritance — Scene 1 practitioner installing herself as the instrument of sight, Scene 5 clairvoyant reading naming Del's dyslexia, Scene 8 notebook handed over with "You passed with flying colours. You're a natural" — making visible the conditions under which the inheritance has had to survive and the conditions under which it can be passed on.

• Controlling concept: What Scene 1 installed as a working cosmology, Scene 5 demonstrated as alternative epistemology doing the institutional labour the British state had abandoned, Scene 8 confirmed as a completed transfer to the next practitioner — three stages of the same inheritance, the cleaned bedsit at the close measuring the distance the practice has travelled across the eight scenes.

• Author's purpose: Writing inside a 1987 cultural moment in which Black British identity was being formed against a host country that had criminalised the practices the diaspora carried, Pinnock makes Mai the figure through whom the Afro-Caribbean spiritual inheritance is given its dignity on a British stage.

• Ultimate moral: Mai's enduring power as a theatrical creation is that the inheritance she carries survives not by assertion but by the small choreography of the transfer — the practitioner finds her notebook under a cushion, hands it to the British-born daughter, and quietly trusts her to read.

C4 — Brod

Question

How does Pinnock use the character of Brod to explore the experience of Caribbean-British men in Leave Taking?

Write about:

- how Pinnock presents Brod

- how Pinnock uses Brod to explore the experience of Caribbean-British men.

[30 marks + 4 AO4 = 34 marks]

Frame. Brod is the play's male Windrush figure — Enid's childhood friend from Jamaica, unmarried, lonely, increasingly dependent on rum, a frequent visitor to her living room — and Pinnock uses him to register what happened to Caribbean-British men whom the host country failed to make space for. The plan tracks three stages — Scene 2 Empire-Day-loyalty turned nationality-letter ambush + Gullyman parable, Scene 7 disclosure-inside-disorder to Del about her own father at Smithfield, Scene 8 structural absence (Brod is not in Scene 8) reading as the moment the inheritance the male line could not carry passes to the female line.

Technique gloss:

• Mirror character — Brod as Enid's mirror (same generation, opposite outcome) and Del's shadow (the unreceived-inheritance path).

• Disclosure-inside-disorder — Brod delivers the play's hardest truth while drunk and turned out of Enid's house.

• Off-stage character — Gullyman (broken by "wogs out" abuse), Viv's father (broken by Smithfield) framing Brod's path.

• Stage direction — Brod's old suit and loose tie (Scene 2), his rum-flask and "swig" (Scene 3) as silent characterisation.

• Structural absence — Brod's exit before Scene 8 reads as the male line stepping aside.

• Compound naming — Brod's "smiling boy into a hard man"; "lump a dead meat".

• Hook (historical fact): Between 1948 and 1971 Britain recruited Caribbean men on British-subject passports under the 1948 British Nationality Act, then progressively voided those passports through the 1962 Commonwealth Immigrants Act, the 1971 Immigration Act and the 1981 British Nationality Act — the same legislative chain whose furthest extreme became the 2018 Windrush deportation scandal Pinnock's own 2018 essay identified as the historical echo of Brod's Scene 2 speech.

• Building sentence (historical context): Winsome Pinnock, born 1961 in Islington to Jamaican parents who arrived in those Windrush years, wrote Leave Taking in 1987 at twenty-three at the Royal Court Young Writers' Group, deliberately staging an unmarried Caribbean-British man in his fifties — Brod — at a time when the host country's workplace racism, housing colour-bar and SUS-law policing pressure had already broken a generation.

• Thesis: Across his Scene 2 ambushed-citizenship speech ("All my life I think of meself as a British subject … Then them send me letter say if me don't get me nationality paper in order they going kick me outta the country") and Gullyman parable ("wogs out"), his Scene 7 disclosure-inside-disorder of the truth about Del's father at Smithfield ("You father change from a smiling boy into a hard man"), and his Scene 8 structural absence in which his exit clears the room for the female-line transfer, Pinnock presents Brod as the figure through whom the male Caribbean-British experience is rendered with seriousness rather than caricature.

Scene focus: Enid's living room; Brod arrives in an old suit with his tie loose; he tells Viv the Gullyman parable; he names the Home Office demand on his nationality paper; the £50 "nationality secure" exchange with Enid follows.

• Topic sentence: Pinnock first presents Brod as a male Windrush figure whose decades of loyalty to the host country have been ambushed by the very state that recruited him, the male migrant rendered as the legislative chain's diagnostic case.

• Technique + evidence: Pinnock deploys autobiographical declarative inside ironic reversal — Brod's "All my life I think of meself as a British subject, wave a flag on Empire Day, touch me hat whenever me see a picture a the queen. Then them send me letter say if me don't get me nationality paper in order they going kick me outta the country" — combining the autobiographical declarative (the lifelong-loyalty list of Empire Day, raised hat, royal portrait) with the ironic reversal (the "Then them send me letter" turn converting the loyalty into the letter that revokes it).

• Close analysis: The triadic loyalty list ("think of meself as a British subject, wave a flag on Empire Day, touch me hat") accumulates a colonial education's full curriculum, while the threshold "Then" converts a lifetime into a sentence's grammatical pivot — the host country revoking the very subjecthood it had instructed.

• Effect on audience 1: The audience hears the Windrush generation's loyalty in the very accent the host country had taught the migrant to refine away — patois grammar carrying Empire Day, the contradiction the play installs as Brod's wound.

• Effect on audience 2: The audience watches the Gullyman parable ("One mornin Gullyman wake up to find him lovely car covered in shit an a message on him door read 'wogs out'. Gullyman heart brock, him mind crack, and now he can hardly talk broken English") extend the same diagnosis from documents to the body — the off-stage friend whose racist abuse has converted Caribbean migrant labour into broken speech.

• Author's purpose: Pinnock argues here that the male Windrush experience is the experience the host country has been most successful at ambushing — and that the Gullyman parable supplies the off-stage extreme of Brod's on-stage trajectory.

• Connective: Scene 7 will move the same diagnosis indoors, into the disclosure about the workplace racism at Smithfield that broke Del's father into a man Del has spent her childhood misreading.

Scene focus: Mai's bedsit, the morning after Enid has thrown Brod out ("All the years I know her … she kick me out into the street"); Brod arrives drunk and entertains Del; under the cover of the disorder he discloses what happened to Del's father at Smithfield meat market.

• Topic sentence: Where Scene 2 had installed Brod's ambushed citizenship, Scene 7 stages his disclosure-inside-disorder — the drunken visitor whose disordered register is the only one inside which the play's hardest family truth can finally travel.

• Technique + evidence: Pinnock deploys compound naming inside the workplace-racism diagnosis — Brod's "It wasn't till we get job a Smithfield meat market that him start to change. Hear the other men, yah, 'Show us yer tail, yer black monkey'. Keep our mouth shut, keep our anger inside. But you father change from a smiling boy into a hard man. Even him face change to favour a lump a dead meat. … no one care what he want to do to a black woman" — combining the compound nouns ("smiling boy" / "hard man" / "lump a dead meat") with the precise institutional location (Smithfield meat market, 1950s–1960s) and the analytical claim ("coming here speeden things up") that names workplace racism as the accelerant of domestic violence.

• Close analysis: The compound "smiling boy into a hard man" sets a parallel-pair against itself — the same man before and after the meat market — while "a lump a dead meat" converts the metaphor of his work into the metaphor of his face; the host country's racist taunts ("yer black monkey") supply the missing causal step.

• Effect on audience 1: The audience hears Pinnock's argument that the male Windrush experience cannot be diagnosed by the documents alone — Smithfield, the meat hooks, the taunts in the dressing room belong inside the same legislative chain as Brod's Home Office letter.

• Effect on audience 2: The audience receives the disclosure as the eighteen-year answer to Del's question — the absent father at the centre of her childhood now explained as a man broken by Britain rather than as a man Enid drove away.

• Author's purpose: Pinnock argues here that the male Windrush experience produced outcomes — Viv's father's violence, Brod's drinking, Gullyman's breakdown — that broke the family roles the host country had asked Caribbean-British men to fulfil, and that the daughters of those families have grown up with explanations they have never been given.

• Connective: Brod's last on-stage line "I might just try to serenade Enid under she window" completes the disclosure; he exits, and Scene 8 will register the male line's structural absence as the room the female line can now occupy.

Scene focus: Scene 8 opens with Mai, Del and the "Clairvoyance and Spiritual Aptitude" exam, then the notebook handover, then Enid as Del's first client; Brod does NOT appear. His last on-stage moment was his Scene 7 exit ("I might just try to serenade Enid under she window. BROD goes."). The closing image is Del reading Enid's palm in Mai's cleaned bedsit.

• Topic sentence: Where Scene 7 had stripped Brod to the disclosure-inside-disorder, Scene 8 confirms his structural place — the male Windrush figure absent from the closing image so the female line can hold the inheritance the male line has been broken too far to carry.

• Technique + evidence: Pinnock deploys structural absence inside the cleaned-bedsit setting — Brod's Scene 7 exit-line "I might just try to serenade Enid under she window" followed by the stage direction "BROD goes", after which Scene 8 stages Mai's notebook transfer to Del and Enid's late confession in Mai's room without any further appearance by Brod — combining the structural absence (the play's most constant male visitor removed from the closing image) with the off-stage trio of broken male figures (Gullyman's "wogs out" breakdown, Del's father's Smithfield disintegration, and Brod's own drinking and homelessness inside Scene 7) that the absence ratifies.

• Close analysis: The stage direction "BROD goes" is the play's most laconic line and its quietest argument — the male Windrush figure's withdrawal is not a failure of the character but the structural condition under which the inheritance reaches the female line; his Scene 7 last word "serenade" even attempts reconciliation, but the play's closing image holds three women in one room, not four.

• Effect on audience 1: The audience registers Brod's absence as quietly poignant — the character who has been the play's most constant visitor receding from the room at the moment the room finally heals.

• Effect on audience 2: The audience recognises Brod as Del's shadow — the second-generation outcome the play warns against — and registers that Mai's "And now I see it plain plain" in Scene 7 was the moment that tipped Del away from Brod's path.

• Author's purpose: Pinnock argues here that the diasporic inheritance, in 1980s Britain, has had to be carried by the female line because the male Windrush figures the host country produced have, in too many cases, been broken before they could carry it themselves — and that Brod's structural absence from Scene 8 is the play's quietest indictment of the workplace racism, housing colour-bar and policing pressure that broke them.

• Connective: The off-stage trio (Gullyman's "wogs out" breakdown, Del's father's Smithfield deformation, and Brod's own diminution into rum and homelessness) bracket the play with the male Windrush outcome the host country produced, and the closing female-line image is what the play offers in its place.

• Restated thesis: In conclusion, Pinnock constructs Brod as the male Windrush figure whose loyalty, disclosure, and absence together stage what the host country cost Caribbean-British men — Scene 2 ambushed-citizenship speech and Gullyman parable, Scene 7 disclosure-inside-disorder about Smithfield, Scene 8 structural absence as the male line steps aside.

• Controlling concept: What Scene 2 installed as the body of the ambushed Windrush man, Scene 7 named through the disclosure of Smithfield's "smiling boy into a hard man", Scene 8 confirmed by the absence that lets the female line carry the inheritance — three stages of the same diagnosis, the male path the play warns against and the female path the play hopes for.

• Author's purpose: Pinnock honours Brod by giving him the play's hardest truth to deliver and then letting him withdraw — refusing both the saint and the cautionary tale, treating Brod as a man whose body has carried what the host country handed him and whose disclosure has earned him the right to recede.

• Ultimate moral: Brod's enduring power as a theatrical creation is the play's quietest stage direction — "BROD goes" — the male Windrush figure's exit clearing the room for the three women whose female line the play closes on.

C5 — The female line: Enid, Mai and Del across three generations

Question

How does Pinnock present the three generations of women in Leave Taking?

Write about:

- how Pinnock presents Enid, Mai and Del at different points in the play

- how Pinnock uses the three generations of women to convey her ideas.

[30 marks + 4 AO4 = 34 marks]

Frame. Pinnock builds the play around three on-stage Caribbean-British women — Mai (older mentor and chosen mother), Enid (Windrush biological mother), and Del (British-born daughter) — and Mooma off-stage in Jamaica forming the fourth-generation anchor. The plan tracks three stages — Scene 1 the three on-stage women in one room but in three registers (Mai practising, Enid supplicating, Del refusing), Scene 4 Enid recalling Mooma to Viv and forcing the building-society book on her (the female line registered in one Enid speech across four generations), Scene 8 the cleaned bedsit converging Mai, Enid and Del on one horizon while Viv leaves for Black Studies.

Pedagogical depth to thread. The Windrush MOTHER'S COST reading + the Mai-as-alternative-epistemology reading + the Caribbean-British double-consciousness reading. The female line carries what the male line cannot; the four generations across two rooms answer the silence the host country imposed. Thread into BP3 or the conclusion.

Technique gloss:

• Female-line structure — Mooma (off-stage), Mai (chosen mother), Enid (Windrush biological), Del (British-born) as the play's structural device.

• Foil-pairing — Mai and Enid (mentor vs biological), Enid and Del (Windrush mother vs British-born daughter), Mai and Del (mentor vs successor).

• Cultural-inheritance ritual — Mai's practice as the room inside which the three on-stage generations finally meet.

• Stage direction — the same Mai's bedsit at Scene 1 "Very messy" and Scene 8 cleaned, the three women in shifting configurations across the eight scenes.

• Doubled coming-of-age — both Enid's truth-telling arc and Del's reception arc must move.

• Recurring object — Mai's notebook of Obeah practice as the female line's material inheritance; Enid's building-society book as its host-country counterpart.

• Hook (historical fact): The Empire Windrush docked at Tilbury on 22 June 1948 carrying mainly men; women followed across the next two decades, and by the 1980s — under the cumulative pressure of the 1962 Commonwealth Immigrants Act, the 1971 Immigration Act, the 1981 British Nationality Act, the Brixton uprising and the New Cross fire — Caribbean-British households had been progressively reduced to female-line transmission as the workplace racism, housing colour-bar and SUS-law policing pressure broke the men around them.

• Building sentence (historical context): Winsome Pinnock, born 1961 in Islington to Jamaican parents who arrived in those Windrush years and trained at the Royal Court Young Writers' Group, wrote Leave Taking in 1987 at twenty-three; her own mother had migrated in 1959 following a husband-to-be who later left her a single parent to four children — the female-line household Pinnock places at the centre of her three-generation on-stage cast.

• Thesis: Across Scene 1 in which the three on-stage women appear in the same room but in three separate registers (Mai's "Mai see right through to your soul"; Enid's anxious consultation; Del's "Mumbo-jumbo nonsense"), Scene 4 in which Enid's recall of Mooma's "chop chop chop" farewell-refusal and her gift of the building-society book to Viv registers the female line in one speech across four generations, and Scene 8 in which the cleaned bedsit converges Mai, Enid and Del on one horizon, Pinnock presents the female line as the structural device through which the diasporic inheritance has had to travel because the male line could not carry it.

Scene focus: Mai's Deptford bedsit; Mai conducts a clairvoyant reading for Enid; Del watches with hostile resistance and palms a charm from the table; the three women are physically together but emotionally separate.

• Topic sentence: Pinnock first places the three on-stage women in the same room but in three separate registers — the older woman practising the inheritance, the middle generation consulting it, the younger generation refusing it — the female line installed before the play has named it.

• Technique + evidence: Pinnock deploys foil-pairing inside contrasting register — Mai's "Mai see right through to your soul" set against Enid's "She getting outta hand. She don't come home after work" and Del's "Mumbo-jumbo nonsense" — combining the foil-pairing (Mai's third-person self-naming and assured patois; Enid's anxious clipped declaratives in patois; Del's south-east London-English dismissal) with the consultation form (the older woman holding the chair, the middle generation occupying the supplicant register, the daughter standing apart) that registers three positions in one opening setting.

• Close analysis: The simultaneity is the argument — Mai's "see" lands as the practitioner's working verb, Enid's "getting outta hand" registers a mother whose authority has slipped, Del's "Mumbo-jumbo nonsense" refuses both in vocabulary the older women never reached for; three registers operating in one room.

• Effect on audience 1: The audience sees the female line as already three rooms inside one room — the practice, the supplication, the refusal each operating in its own register inside Mai's bedsit.

• Effect on audience 2: The audience registers that the play's argument will depend on whether the three registers can be brought into the same room as a chain — the female line existing as transmission only when each position has been filled by the woman trained to fill it.

• Author's purpose: Pinnock argues here that the female line in the diaspora has not been a continuous transmission — it has been three separate registers carrying their own piece of the inheritance, and the play's work will be bringing them together inside the very room they have entered separately.

• Connective: Scene 4 will register the fourth-generation anchor — Mooma off-stage in Jamaica — through Enid's recall to Viv, and will place the building-society book on the table as the host-country counterpart of Mai's notebook.

Scene focus: Enid's living room, dark, late evening after Cynthia has phoned with news of Mooma's death; Viv listens; Enid recalls the day she left Jamaica; she gives Viv the building-society book; the scene closes on "I want… I want to go home."

• Topic sentence: Where Scene 1 had installed the three on-stage registers, Scene 4 registers the fourth-generation anchor — Mooma off-stage in Jamaica — through Enid's recall to Viv, the female line gathered in one speech across four generations.

• Technique + evidence: Pinnock deploys onomatopoeic repetition inside an inheritance-redirection — Enid's "'Mooma,' I say, 'I gone now.' She never turn round, jus' carry on working, chop chop chop, play deaf. In the end I had to give up, walk away. (Slight pause.)
