It wasn't easy to leave" followed by the stage direction "She hands the building society account book to VIV" — combining the onomatopoeia (the cutlass-stroke "chop chop chop" sounding the homeland mother's refusal) with the inheritance-redirection (the homeland farewell denied, the host-country savings book then handed across) inside the same Scene 4 sequence.

• Close analysis: The triple "chop" gives Mooma's labour a sound and Enid's leave-taking a percussion, while the stage-direction handover of the building-society book registers Pinnock's argument that the female line has had to redirect inheritance — Mooma's cutlass and chocolate tea on one side, Viv's London university place on the other.

• Effect on audience 1: The audience hears four generations in one Enid speech — Mooma's labour in the cutlass-sound, Enid's grief in the unfinished close "I want… I want to go home", Viv's future opening as the building-society book lands in her hand, and Del's absence shadowing the scene.

• Effect on audience 2: The audience registers Viv's response ("I can't live your life for you. I don't know what you want. What do you want?") as the female line's third-generation refusal of the redirected inheritance, the British-born daughter declining to fulfil the host-country future the Windrush mother's leave-taking has paid for.

• Author's purpose: Pinnock argues here that the female line in the diaspora is held together by the older generation's recall — Mooma reached through Enid's voice, the homeland inheritance carried in the cutlass-sound — and that the redirected building-society book is the form the host country forced the inheritance to take.

• Connective: Scene 8 will gather the redirected inheritance and the recalled homeland into a third room — Mai's cleaned bedsit — and stage the three on-stage women in one closing horizon.

Scene focus: Mai's cleaned bedsit; Mai gives Del her notebook ("I don't have a daughter to pass them on to"); Enid arrives as Del's first client; Enid's confession "Nobody see you, nobody hear you" and "How you going teach you children that they don't exist?" breaks the eighteen-year silence; the play closes on Del reading her own mother's palm.

• Topic sentence: Where Scene 4 had registered four generations in one Enid speech, Scene 8 stages the three on-stage generations on one horizon — Mai's notebook passing into Del's hand, Enid's silence cracking inside the room the older Caribbean woman built.

• Technique + evidence: Pinnock deploys cyclical structure inside doubled coming-of-age — the same Mai's bedsit the Scene 1 stage direction described as "Very messy" now appears cleaned ("First time this place's been cleaned in years"), with Mai handing Del the notebook ("Here, this is for you … I don't have a daughter to pass them on to") and Enid finally articulating "Nobody see you, nobody hear you … How you going teach you children that they don't exist? … I would chop off my hand if it would help you" — combining the cyclical structure (the unchanged room measuring the changed female line) with doubled coming-of-age (Enid's truth-telling arc and Del's reception arc completing in the same scene).

• Close analysis: The choreography is the argument — Mai withdraws after handing the notebook, Del takes the practitioner's chair, Enid sits as client — the female line registered for the first time as a chain in which each on-stage position has been filled by the woman trained to fill it; the absent Mooma anchors the chain at the homeland end, while Viv's departure for Black Studies ("Black Studies. You ever hear of such a thing?") anchors it at the British end.

• Effect on audience 1: The audience watches the closing image as the three on-stage generations finally arranged as a continuous female line — practitioner, Windrush mother, British-born daughter, each holding her place inside the inherited practice.

• Effect on audience 2: The audience receives Enid's unfinished "someone to gather… gather me up and touch my cheek like I was a prize, not a curse, and stroke my hair like Mooma never could" as the line in which the female line finally folds back on itself — the homeland mother named in the closing breath of the Windrush mother, the British-born daughter the one to hear it.

• Author's purpose: Pinnock argues here that the diasporic inheritance has travelled through the women because the men were broken before they could carry it, and that the female line can claim the inheritance only when the three on-stage generations occupy their three positions in the same room while the off-stage Mooma is finally spoken aloud.

• Connective: Lynette Goddard's Staging Black Feminisms (2007) reads Pinnock's generation as the first to give Black British women's lives full theatrical representation; Scene 8's closing image is exactly that representation, three Caribbean-British women's positions held inside one British stage image.

• Restated thesis: In conclusion, Pinnock dramatises the female line as the structural device through which the diasporic inheritance has had to travel — Scene 1 three women in three separate registers, Scene 4 four generations registered in one Enid speech via the "chop chop chop" farewell and the building-society book handover, Scene 8 three on-stage generations on one horizon in the cleaned bedsit.

• Controlling concept: What Scene 1 installed as the female line's three separate registers, Scene 4 named as the four-generation chain held together by the older generation's recall, Scene 8 confirmed as the three on-stage positions finally coherent — three stages of the same chain, the female line claiming what the male line had been broken too far to carry.

• Author's purpose: Writing inside the Royal Court Young Writers' Group and drawing on her own three-generation Caribbean-British household, Pinnock honours the female line as the structural device through which the inheritance has travelled, and through which it can finally be received.

• Ultimate moral: The closing image — Del's hand on Enid's hand inside Mai's cleaned bedsit, Mooma named in the closing breath, Viv leaving for Black Studies — is the female line's four positions held inside one staged Caribbean-British inheritance the host country had spent four decades trying to break.

C6 — Enid and Brod as the Windrush generation

Question

How does Pinnock present Enid and Brod as members of the Windrush generation in Leave Taking?

Write about:

- how Pinnock presents Enid and Brod

- how Pinnock uses Enid and Brod to explore the experience of the Windrush generation.

[30 marks + 4 AO4 = 34 marks]

Frame. Enid and Brod are Pinnock's two on-stage Windrush figures — childhood friends from Jamaica whose 1980s London lives register the doubled outcome the migration produced for the female and male lines. The plan tracks three stages — Scene 2 the two Windrush positions installed back-to-back (Brod's nationality-letter speech and Empire-Day biography paired with Enid's "England been good to me" credo and £50 "nationality secure" footnote), Scene 7 Brod's disclosure of Smithfield racism and the cost it extracted from Enid's marriage, Scene 8 Enid's broken-speech confession landing what Brod has been carrying alone while Brod himself withdraws from the closing image.

Technique gloss:

• Foil-pairing — Enid (female Windrush outcome) and Brod (male Windrush outcome) as the same generation's two paths.

• Autobiographical declarative — Brod's "All my life I think of meself as a British subject" lifelong-loyalty list.

• Ironic juxtaposition — Brod's nationality-letter ambush set against Enid's £50 "nationality secure" receipt.

• Patois aphorism — the shared homeland register the two characters operate inside ("England been good to me"; "Then them send me letter").

• Compound naming — Brod's "smiling boy into a hard man" registering racism's deformation of Enid's husband.

• Structural absence — Brod absent from the Scene 8 cleaned bedsit; the female-line image carries the inheritance the male line was broken too far to carry.

• Hook (historical fact): The Empire Windrush docked at Tilbury on 22 June 1948 carrying mainly men recruited on British-subject passports under the 1948 British Nationality Act; the cumulative legislative chain of the 1962 Commonwealth Immigrants Act, the 1971 Immigration Act and the 1981 British Nationality Act progressively voided those passports, and the furthest extreme of that erosion later surfaced as the 2018 Windrush deportation scandal — the same documentary erasure Pinnock had dramatised inside her two paired Windrush figures, Enid and Brod, more than thirty years before the Guardian made it news.

• Building sentence (historical context): Born 1961 in Islington to Jamaican parents who arrived in those Windrush years, Pinnock wrote Leave Taking in 1987 at twenty-three inside the Royal Court Young Writers' Group, deliberately staging her childhood-friend pair — the hospital cleaner who held the household and the unmarried male visitor whom the host country broke around her — inside the same 1981-Britain cultural emergence (the New Cross fire, the Brixton uprising, the 1981 BNA) that had only just named Black British identity as a distinct category.

• Thesis: Across Scene 2 in which Brod's autobiographical "All my life I think of meself as a British subject" speech and Enid's "England been good to me" credo install the two Windrush positions back-to-back, Scene 7 in which Brod's "smiling boy into a hard man" disclosure exposes what workplace racism did to Enid's husband and to Enid's marriage, and Scene 8 in which Enid's "I see it. I see it" confession lands what Brod has been carrying alone while Brod is absent from the cleaned bedsit, Pinnock presents Enid and Brod as the same Windrush generation's two paths through 1980s Britain.

Scene focus: Enid's North London living room; the pastor's visit; Brod arrives in an old suit, tie loose; Brod's nationality-letter speech and Empire-Day biography; Enid's "England been good to me" defence and the £50 "nationality secure" exchange.

• Topic sentence: Pinnock first places her two Windrush figures inside the same family room and lets them speak their two positions in immediate sequence — the male migrant's ambushed-citizenship speech answered by the female migrant's assimilationist credo, the same generation's two responses to the same legislative chain installed before any household crisis has yet broken either of them.

• Technique + evidence: Pinnock deploys autobiographical declarative inside ironic juxtaposition — Brod's "All my life I think of meself as a British subject, wave a flag on Empire Day, touch me hat whenever me see a picture a the queen. Then them send me letter say if me don't get me nationality paper in order they going kick me outta the country" set immediately against Enid's "You come here, you try to fit in. Stick to the rules. England been good to me. I proud a my English girls" and her "So, we pay the fifty pound and now we nationality secure" — combining the autobiographical declarative (Brod's triadic loyalty-list of "British subject", "Empire Day" flag-wave and raised hat to the queen's picture) with ironic juxtaposition (the £50 receipt for the citizenship the 1962 Act had already devalued).

• Close analysis: The patois grammar of both speeches registers Caribbean inheritance even in the act of declaring British loyalty — Brod's "meself as a British subject" and Enid's "I proud a my English girls" each delivered in the speech the host country tried to override — while the threshold "Then" in Brod's speech and the cash transaction in Enid's convert lifelong loyalty into a single ambushed sentence and a paid-for credo.

• Effect on audience 1: The audience hears the two Windrush positions in the same scene and registers Pinnock's argument that the host country has extracted from the same generation two different but complementary prices — the male migrant ambushed by the letter, the female migrant filing the credo that the £50 has bought.

• Effect on audience 2: The audience recognises Enid and Brod as the play's two on-stage Windrush figures, and registers that Pinnock has placed them inside the same room precisely because the friendship is the diasporic version of belonging the host country has left available to them.

• Author's purpose: Pinnock argues here that the Windrush experience produced two paths through the same legislative chain — Brod's loyalty turned letter-ambush and Enid's loyalty turned £50 footnote — and that both paths surface inside the same household because the migration has compressed the two outcomes into one diasporic friendship.

• Connective: Scene 7 will move the same diagnosis indoors — into the bedsit — and let the male Windrush figure deliver the disclosure that converts Enid's living-room credo into the eighteen-year silence it has been concealing.

Scene focus: Mai's bedsit the morning after Enid has thrown Brod out; Brod arrives drunk and entertains Del; under the cover of the disorder he discloses what happened to Del's father at Smithfield meat market.

• Topic sentence: Where Scene 2 had installed the two Windrush positions back-to-back, Scene 7 stages the disclosure that converts Enid's credo into the eighteen-year silence it has been concealing — the male Windrush figure delivering, inside drunken disorder, the workplace-racism diagnosis that exposes what the female Windrush figure's marriage has actually cost.

• Technique + evidence: Pinnock deploys compound naming inside the workplace-racism account — Brod's "It wasn't till we get job a Smithfield meat market that him start to change. Hear the other men, yah, 'Show us yer tail, yer black monkey'. Keep our mouth shut, keep our anger inside. But you father change from a smiling boy into a hard man. … If they won't treat him like a human being outside, him make sure she treat him like a king in him own house … no one care what he want to do to a black woman" — combining compound naming ("smiling boy into a hard man"; "king in him own house") with the precise institutional location (Smithfield meat market, 1950s–60s) and the doubled racist taunt ("Show us yer tail, yer black monkey") that supplies the causal chain.

• Close analysis: The compound "smiling boy into a hard man" sets the same migrant against himself before and after the meat market, while "king in him own house … no one care what he want to do to a black woman" names workplace racism as the accelerant of domestic violence — the host country's invisibility of Black women converting the male Windrush worker's humiliation outside into the female Windrush wife's invisibility inside.

• Effect on audience 1: The audience hears the male Windrush figure delivering, inside trickster disorder, the diagnosis the female Windrush figure has not been able to articulate — Smithfield's "black monkey" converting "England been good to me" into the credo it has been protecting.

• Effect on audience 2: The audience recognises that the two Windrush positions cannot be diagnosed apart — Enid's living-room credo and Brod's bedsit disclosure are the same legislative chain inflected through male labour and through female marriage, the same generation's two outcomes the play has been waiting to bring into one diagnosis.

• Author's purpose: Pinnock argues here that the male Windrush experience and the female Windrush experience are bound by the same workplace racism — what the host country did to Brod and to Del's father inside Smithfield is precisely what produced Enid's invisibility inside her own household — and that the two outcomes must be heard together for either to be understood.

• Connective: Scene 8 will gather what Brod has disclosed in Scene 7 into Enid's own voice — the male Windrush figure withdrawn from the cleaned bedsit, the female Windrush figure finally permitted to name aloud what eighteen years of host-country invisibility has imposed.

Scene focus: Mai's cleaned bedsit; Brod absent (his Scene 7 exit "BROD goes" is his last on-stage moment); Enid arrives as Del's first client; the eighteen-year silence cracks in broken-speech confession.

• Topic sentence: Where Scene 7 had let Brod disclose what the host country did to the male Windrush figure, Scene 8 stages the female Windrush figure finally articulating what eighteen years of host-country invisibility had imposed on her — Brod absent from the closing image because the male line has been broken too far to host the room the female line can.

• Technique + evidence: Pinnock deploys broken-speech confession inside structural absence — Enid's "Nobody see you, nobody hear you … All the time you screaming, you screaming inside but nobody come" leading to "How you going teach you children that they don't exist?…You say I don't see how them treat you out there. I see it. I see it and it make me want to tear the place down. I would chop off my hand if it would help you", delivered inside Mai's cleaned bedsit while Brod, last seen exiting in Scene 7 with "BROD goes", is absent from the closing image — combining broken-speech confession (the doubled negation "nobody see you, nobody hear you" answered by the doubled affirmation "I see it. I see it") with structural absence (Brod's withdrawal as the male line's quietest indictment).

• Close analysis: The doubled "I see it" repairs the doubled "nobody see you" — eighteen years of host-country invisibility answered by a Windrush mother finally testifying that she has been watching her daughter's reality all along — and the same diagnosis Brod delivered in Scene 7 in the patois of disorder now arrives in Enid's voice in the patois of confession; the conditional "I would chop off my hand if it would help you" converts a Caribbean idiom of maternal sacrifice into the play's most painful image of the cost the same Windrush legislation extracted from female and male alike.

• Effect on audience 1: The audience hears Brod's Scene 7 diagnosis returning in Enid's voice — the male Windrush figure's disclosure landing as the female Windrush figure's confession, the eighteen-year silence cracking inside the same Caribbean spiritual room the older generation has been keeping.

• Effect on audience 2: The audience receives Brod's Scene 8 absence as the play's quietest indictment of the male Windrush outcome — "BROD goes" is the play's most laconic stage direction, the male migrant's withdrawal clearing the room so the female line can hold what the male line was broken before it could carry.

• Author's purpose: Pinnock argues here that the Windrush generation's two outcomes have had to be carried into the closing image by the female line alone, because the host country's workplace racism, housing colour-bar and policing pressure produced male outcomes too broken to host the diasporic inheritance — Brod's absence and Enid's confession together register the same legislative chain at its female and male extremes.

• Connective: Pinnock's 2018 essay later named the Windrush deportation scandal as the historical echo of Brod's Scene 2 nationality speech; Enid's Scene 8 "Nobody see you, nobody hear you" is the female-line echo of the same erasure, registered thirty years before the scandal made it news.

• Restated thesis: In conclusion, Pinnock pairs Enid and Brod as the same Windrush generation's two paths through 1980s Britain — Scene 2 two positions installed back-to-back, Scene 7 Brod's "smiling boy into a hard man" disclosure exposing the cost Enid has been carrying, Scene 8 Enid's "I see it. I see it" confession landing what Brod has been carrying alone while Brod withdraws.

• Controlling concept: What Scene 2 installed as the two Windrush positions in immediate sequence, Scene 7 named through Brod's disclosure of Smithfield's "black monkey" taunt and its deformation of Enid's husband, Scene 8 confirmed in Enid's broken-speech confession inside the cleaned bedsit — three stages of the same legislative chain inflected through male labour and through female marriage.

• Author's purpose: Writing in 1987 inside the Royal Court Young Writers' Group and the 1981-Britain cultural emergence, Pinnock pairs Enid and Brod to argue that the Windrush experience must be narrated in two voices, and that the doubled diagnosis is the precondition for any second-generation reception of what the migration cost.

• Ultimate moral: The closing image — Brod absent, Enid seated as Del's first client, the cleaned bedsit holding three Caribbean-British women on one horizon — is the Windrush generation's two paths registered as one full diagnosis, the female line carrying inside one staged inheritance what the male line was broken too far to carry alone.

C7 — Mai and Enid as chosen and biological mother

Question

How does Pinnock present Mai and Enid as two kinds of mother in Leave Taking?

Write about:

- how Pinnock presents Mai

- how Pinnock presents Enid

- how Pinnock uses Mai and Enid to convey her ideas about motherhood.

[30 marks + 4 AO4 = 34 marks]

Frame. Pinnock distributes the maternal role across two women — Mai as the older Caribbean-born practitioner who becomes Del's chosen mother inside the room where the spiritual inheritance lives, and Enid as the Windrush biological mother whose two-jobs sacrifice and patois discipline have held the household together unhealed. The plan tracks three stages — Scene 1 Enid bringing her daughters to Mai (the chosen-mother dynamic installed alongside the biological-mother authority "I bring up those two girls on me own"), Scene 6 Enid returning alone to Mai for grief-counsel (the intimate consultation "What they know about a black woman soul?" answered by Mai's "It mash up you life" diagnosis and the "I was hard on my boy" mirror), Scene 8 Mai's "I don't have a daughter to pass them on to" notebook handover paired with Enid's bedsit confession (the chosen mother handing the inheritance and the biological mother finally received inside the chosen-maternal room).

Technique gloss:

• Doubled maternal role — biological and chosen mother distributed across two characters.

• Foil-pairing — Mai's calm practitioner register set against Enid's exhausted disciplinarian register.

• Cultural-inheritance ritual — Mai's practice as the chosen-maternal space the biological-maternal space could not provide.

• Patois aphorism — Mai's "It mash up you life" compression; Enid's patois supplication.

• Mirror disclosure — Mai's "I was hard on my boy" reflecting Enid's own maternal regret.

• Recurring object — Mai's notebook as the chosen-maternal inheritance handed across the closing scene.

• Hook (historical fact): Obeah was criminalised in Jamaica from 1760 — originally punishable by death, later by imprisonment with hard labour, and the statute remains on the Jamaican books — while 1980s NHS services routinely pathologised Caribbean-British women's distress, leaving Pinnock's older Jamaican-born women caretakers of an inheritance two states had tried to erase, and Pinnock's distribution of the maternal role across Mai and Enid is the literary answer to that institutional void.

C8 — Enid and Del's relationship

Question

How does Pinnock present the relationship between Enid and Del in Leave Taking?

Write about:

- how Pinnock presents Enid and Del at different points in the play

- how Pinnock uses the relationship between Enid and Del to convey her ideas.

[30 marks + 4 AO4 = 34 marks]

Frame. The Enid–Del relationship is the play's central diasporic mother-daughter axis — Windrush mother and British-born daughter across eighteen years of patois sacrifice, south-east London-English indictment, and a closing palm-reading in which the trained daughter finally receives the mother she had spent the first scenes refusing. The plan tracks three stages — Scene 2 the rupture installed (Enid's "I work two jobs seven days a week" sacrifice speech and "I proud a my English girls" credo answered by Del's "You're too busy bowing and scraping to your beloved England" indictment and the slap), Scene 6 the daughter hiding while the mother confesses to Mai (Del overhears Enid's "howl of pain" and the "black woman soul" grief she has never been shown), Scene 8 the palm-reading reconciliation (Del cleans Mai's bedsit and reads Enid's palm; Enid's "I see it. I see it" breaks the eighteen-year silence).

Technique gloss:

• Patois vs south-east London speech — the relationship staged in two registers.

• Tricolon of sacrifice — Enid's "food in you belly and roof over you head" / "one dress, one pair a shoes with hole in it" household ledger.

• Direct address as indictment — Del's "You're too busy bowing and scraping to your beloved England".

• Stage business — the slap (Scene 2), Del hiding (Scene 6), the cleaned bedsit (Scene 8) as the relationship's three wordless beats.

• Doubled coming-of-age — both Enid's truth-telling arc and Del's reception arc must move.

• Cultural-inheritance ritual — the closing palm-reading as the third register the relationship has had to invent.

• Hook (historical fact): Winsome Pinnock, born 1961 in Islington to Jamaican parents who arrived in the Windrush years, wrote Leave Taking in 1987 at twenty-three inside the Royal Court Young Writers' Group, drawing on the North London Jamaican household she had been raised inside — and her construction of the Enid–Del axis converts eighteen years of Windrush-mother / British-born-daughter friction into the play's most carefully timed structural arc.

• Building sentence (historical context): Pinnock anchors the relationship inside the cumulative legislative chain (1962 Commonwealth Immigrants Act, 1971 Immigration Act, 1981 British Nationality Act) that progressively voided the British-subject passports the Windrush generation had arrived under, the same legal erosion that the 1981 cluster (Brixton uprising; New Cross fire) made publicly visible just six years before the play's premiere.

• Thesis: Across Scene 2 in which Enid's "I work two jobs seven days a week" sacrifice speech and Del's "You're too busy bowing and scraping to your beloved England" indictment culminate in the slap, Scene 6 in which Del hides while Enid confesses to Mai and the daughter overhears the mother's "howl of pain" she has never been shown, and Scene 8 in which Del cleans Mai's bedsit, receives Mai's notebook, and reads her own mother's palm as Enid's "I see it. I see it" breaks the eighteen-year silence, Pinnock dramatises the Enid–Del relationship as the structural axis through which the diasporic mother-daughter healing has to travel.

Scene focus: Enid's North London living room; Del returns in her work uniform having stayed out at the sound system and lost her job; Enid catches the lie; the argument escalates through Enid's sacrifice speech and Del's hospital-party demolition; the scene ends with the slap.

• Topic sentence: Pinnock first installs the Enid–Del relationship as a rupture between two registers — the Windrush mother's patois ledger of sacrifice answered by the British-born daughter's south-east London-English indictment — the eighteen years of household life compressed into one escalating exchange that ends in a physical strike.

• Technique + evidence: Pinnock deploys tricolon-of-sacrifice inside direct-address indictment — Enid's "I work two jobs seven days a week to put food in you belly and roof over you head. I wear one dress, one pair a shoes with hole in it so that you can dress like those children who have fathers" answered by Del's "But what you give us that we can use out there? You don't see the police vans hunting us down, or the managers who treat us like we're the lowest of the low. You're too busy bowing and scraping to your beloved England" and the stage direction "Enid slaps Del. Pause. For a moment it looks as though Del might hit her back" — combining tricolon-of-sacrifice (the "food in you belly and roof over you head" / "one dress, one pair a shoes with hole in it" household ledger Enid believes her daughters owe her) with direct-address indictment (Del's second-person "you" converting the mother into the speech's defendant).

• Close analysis: Enid's patois itemises a ledger the host country has made her keep — two jobs, one dress, one pair of shoes — while Del's participles "hunting" and "bowing and scraping" turn institutions into agents and Enid herself into a posture; the slap registers in stage business what the two speeches cannot reconcile in language, the relationship's rupture audible in two registers and visible in one strike.

• Effect on audience 1: The audience hears the relationship's friction in the sound of the two registers — the mother's patois grammar of sacrifice cannot be answered by the daughter's south-east London-English grammar of indictment, and the slap is the play's first physical sign that the two registers have nowhere to meet.

• Effect on audience 2: The audience watches Del exit on "That's the last time" and registers that the relationship has reached the point at which the daughter has run out of words inside the mother's register and the mother has run out of patience inside the daughter's.

• Author's purpose: Pinnock argues here that the diasporic mother-daughter relationship cannot be repaired inside the household's existing registers — patois and south-east London speech cannot meet without a third room, and the play's work will be inventing that room across the eight scenes.

• Connective: Scene 6 will return the two women to the same conversation only by placing Del out of sight — the daughter hiding while the mother confesses to the older Caribbean woman the daughter has never seen the mother address.

Scene focus: Mai's living room; Enid arrives alone for a reading, not knowing Del is hiding in another room; Mai reads Enid's palm and predicts a "long journey"; Enid's grief erupts; Mai answers with "It mash up you life" and her own maternal mirror "I was hard on my boy"; after Enid leaves, Mai tells Del "You should be helping each other to survive. Don't you feel for her?" and Del replies "She hates me. Always has, ever since I was a little kid. She thinks I'm bad".

• Topic sentence: Where Scene 2 had installed the rupture in two opposing registers, Scene 6 stages the daughter overhearing the mother in a register the household had not made available — Del hidden in the next room while Enid is finally received as a grieving daughter herself, the relationship's silent reordering installed before either woman can yet speak it to the other.

• Technique + evidence: Pinnock deploys overheard confession inside stage business — Enid's "What doctor know about our illness? Just give you pills to sick you stomach and a doctor certificate. What they know about a black woman soul?" followed by the stage direction "Standing, Enid stares straight ahead, then her face contorts and her mouth opens in a soundless scream. Then the sound comes – a howl of pain…" — combining overheard confession (the chosen mother's room the daughter has been smuggled into as the only space inside which the biological mother's most exposed register can be heard) with stage business (Enid's "howl of pain" registered in the body, the eighteen-year silence cracking inside a room where Del is present but not visible).

• Close analysis: The stage direction converts Enid into a body that screams before it can speak — the "soundless scream" followed by the "howl of pain" registering the eighteen years the patois ledger of Scene 2 had compressed into bearable form — while Del's later answer "She hates me. Always has, ever since I was a little kid. She thinks I'm bad" registers the daughter's eighteen-year misreading of the same household; the third party (Mai's chosen-maternal room) lets each woman articulate what the relationship's own registers had refused to carry.

• Effect on audience 1: The audience watches the daughter overhear what the household had never offered her — the Windrush mother grieving for her own homeland-mother and articulating "black woman soul" the British surgery had not been equipped to receive — and registers that the relationship's first stage of repair is the daughter's silent witnessing inside a room she has not yet been trained to host.

• Effect on audience 2: The audience hears Del's "She hates me" in immediate sequence with Enid's "howl of pain" and recognises the relationship's tragic asymmetry — each woman has spent eighteen years reading the other through the wrong register, the daughter assuming hatred where the mother carries grief, the mother carrying grief in a register the daughter has not been given the means to recognise.

• Author's purpose: Pinnock argues here that the diasporic mother-daughter relationship cannot be repaired by either woman alone — the chosen mother's room is required as the institutional space inside which the biological mother can be received and the daughter can begin to overhear what the household has spent eighteen years not saying.

• Connective: Scene 8 will bring the two women into the same room inside the same register — Del trained by Mai's chosen-maternal practice, Enid seated as the daughter's client, the relationship arriving inside the third register the older generation has been keeping.

Scene focus: Mai's cleaned bedsit; Del has been trained by Mai ("You passed with flying colours. You're a natural") and now holds Mai's notebook; Enid arrives as Del's first client; the eighteen-year silence breaks in Enid's broken-speech confession and the play closes on Del reading her mother's palm.

• Topic sentence: Where Scene 6 had let the daughter overhear the mother through stage business, Scene 8 stages the two women inside the same register — the trained daughter making the room and reading the mother's palm, the Windrush mother finally permitted to articulate what eighteen years of host-country invisibility had imposed on her.

• Technique + evidence: Pinnock deploys broken-speech confession inside cultural-inheritance ritual — Enid's "Nobody see you, nobody hear you … All the time you screaming, you screaming inside but nobody come" leading to "How you going teach you children that they don't exist?…You say I don't see how them treat you out there. I see it. I see it and it make me want to tear the place down. I would chop off my hand if it would help you", delivered inside the cleaned bedsit as Del takes the practitioner's chair and reads Enid's palm — combining broken-speech confession (the doubled negation "nobody see you, nobody hear you" answered by the doubled affirmation "I see it. I see it") with cultural-inheritance ritual (the palm-reading as the third register the relationship has had to invent across the eight scenes).

• Close analysis: The doubled "I see it" repairs the doubled "nobody see you" — eighteen years of host-country invisibility answered by a Windrush mother finally testifying that she has been watching her daughter's reality all along — while the conditional "I would chop off my hand if it would help you" converts a Caribbean idiom of maternal sacrifice into the play's most painful image of the cost the Scene 2 ledger had been concealing; the trained daughter's hand on the mother's palm is the third register the household had not been able to invent in patois or in south-east London speech alone.

• Effect on audience 1: The audience watches the daughter who once landed "You're too busy bowing and scraping to your beloved England" now make a room inside which the mother can say "I see it. I see it", and registers Scene 8 as the play's quietest argument that diasporic mother-daughter healing requires the trained daughter to host the silenced mother.

• Effect on audience 2: The audience receives the closing image — Del's hand on Enid's palm, Mai's notebook on the table, the eighteen-year silence finally articulated — as the relationship's third register staged in body-to-body practice, the inherited Caribbean spiritual room hosting what neither homeland patois nor host-country English had been able to.

• Author's purpose: Pinnock argues here that the diasporic mother-daughter relationship cannot be repaired inside the household's two given registers — and that the trained daughter has to make a third room, the chosen-maternal room, inside which the Windrush mother's silenced truths can finally be received.

• Connective: Stuart Hall's Cultural Identity and Diaspora (1990) names diasporic identity as "a matter of 'becoming' as well as of 'being'" — and the Enid–Del Scene 8 palm-reading is exactly that becoming, the relationship registered not as a state either woman had reached but as a practice the trained daughter has begun.

• Restated thesis: In conclusion, Pinnock dramatises the Enid–Del relationship through three stages — Scene 2 sacrifice ledger meeting south-east London-English indictment and ending in the slap, Scene 6 daughter hiding while mother confesses through Mai's chosen-maternal room, Scene 8 broken-speech confession and palm-reading inside the third register the play has had to invent.

• Controlling concept: What Scene 2 installed as the relationship's two-register rupture, Scene 6 reordered through the daughter's silent overhearing of the mother's "howl of pain", Scene 8 reconciled through the trained daughter's reception of the mother's "I see it. I see it" — three stages of the same diasporic mother-daughter axis arriving at one room.

• Author's purpose: Writing in 1987 inside the Royal Court Young Writers' Group and the formation of Black British identity, Pinnock honours the diasporic mother-daughter relationship as the structural axis through which the female-line healing has to travel.

• Ultimate moral: The closing image — Del's hand on Enid's palm inside Mai's cleaned bedsit, the eighteen-year silence broken in "I see it. I see it" — is the relationship arriving at the same room only inside the third register the older Caribbean generation kept for it.

C9 — Del and Viv as sisters

Question

How does Pinnock present the relationship between Del and Viv in Leave Taking?

Write about:

- how Pinnock presents Del and Viv at different points in the play

- how Pinnock uses the relationship between Del and Viv to convey her ideas.

[30 marks + 4 AO4 = 34 marks]

Frame. Del and Viv are Pinnock's two routes into the diasporic inheritance — the rebellious elder daughter who refuses then receives the body-to-body practice, and the studious younger sister whose curriculum-self in Scene 2 converts by Scene 8 into the formal-educational claiming of Black Studies. The plan tracks three stages — Scene 2 the two routes installed back-to-back (Viv recites Rupert Brooke's "A dust whom England bore, shaped, made aware" on Enid's prompt while Del speaks the sound-system in "All I did last night was dance… The bass is mad"), Scene 5 protective eviction (Del's "Go back to school and sit that exam" sending Viv back to the English Literature paper she has walked out of, the elder sister protecting the younger sister's route), Scene 8 convergence (Viv announces Black Studies at university while Del receives Mai's notebook and reads Enid's palm — both routes complete on the same horizon).

• Foil character / sibling pair — Del and Viv as the second generation's two-route response to the same household pressure.

• Recitation as curriculum-self — Viv's Rupert Brooke recitation as the host country's syllabus installed inside the daughter's voice.

• Concrete-sensory imagery — Del's "The bass is mad" sound-system speech as Black British youth culture installed as her belonging.

• Off-stage event — the missed English Literature exam in Scene 5.

• Protective imperative — Del's "Go back to school and sit that exam" as elder-sister care delivered through redirection.

• Structural convergence — Scene 8 staging the academic and body-to-body routes meeting at the close.

• Building sentence (historical context): Born 1961 in Islington to Jamaican parents who arrived in the Windrush years and educated through the 1970s comprehensive system, Pinnock trained at the Royal Court Young Writers' Group and later joined the Black British women writers (Buchi Emecheta, Joan Riley, Andrea Levy) contesting the canon's absences — the same canon Viv recites from in Scene 2 and converts into Black Studies in Scene 8, the same alternative route the older Caribbean generation has been carrying as Del finally receives it.

• Thesis: Across Scene 2 in which Viv's recitation of Rupert Brooke and Del's "The bass is mad" sound-system speech install the two routes back-to-back, Scene 5 in which Del's protective "Go back to school and sit that exam" sends Viv back to the missed English Literature paper and the two routes diverge under household crisis, and Scene 8 in which Viv's announcement of Black Studies converges with Del's receipt of Mai's notebook on the same closing horizon, Pinnock presents the Del-Viv relationship as the female line's structural argument that the diasporic inheritance can be claimed in more than one register at once.

• Author's purpose: Pinnock argues here that the diasporic second generation has had to invent more than one route home — the canon-recitation route and the youth-culture route — and that the play will need both daughters' speech-acts to make its closing argument that the inheritance can be received in more than one register.

• Connective: Scene 5 will pressure-test the two routes inside crisis — the elder sister insisting from inside the older Caribbean woman's bedsit that the younger sister's curriculum route must be protected even at the cost of her own.

Scene focus: Mai's living room; Del has moved in with Mai, in the early stages of pregnancy; Viv arrives with the saved university money Enid forced on her in Scene 4, telling Del she has walked out of her English Literature exam; Del refuses the money and orders Viv back to the exam.

• Topic sentence: Where Scene 2 had installed the two routes back-to-back inside the family room, Scene 5 stages their divergence under crisis — the elder sister, by her own choice no longer on the curriculum route, refusing to let the younger sister abandon it and ordering her back into the exam the host country has been examining her on.

• Effect on audience 1: The audience hears Viv's "I need another language to express myself" and Del's "Go back to school" in immediate sequence and recognises the play's argument that the curriculum route, even when it does not yet hold the daughter's inheritance, has to be protected because it remains the family's only contracted route into the host country's institutions.

• Effect on audience 2: The audience watches Del refuse the saved university money Enid forced on Viv in Scene 4 and order Viv back to the exam, and registers the elder-sister relationship as care delivered through structural separation — the female line's responsibility for the next generation taking the form of redirection rather than shared shelter.

• Author's purpose: Pinnock argues here that the diasporic sibling relationship in 1980s Britain has had to operate under a household pressure that produced costly divergence — the elder sister's care for the younger taking the form of protecting the very institution that the younger sister has just exposed as inadequate, because no second route has yet been offered.

• Connective: Scene 8 will deliver the second route — Viv's announcement of Black Studies converting the inadequate curriculum into a reclamation route, the same horizon on which Del receives Mai's notebook.

Scene focus: Mai's cleaned bedsit; Mai puts Del through the "Clairvoyance and Spiritual Aptitude" exam and hands over her notebook ("You passed with flying colours. You're a natural"; "Is time. You ready"; "I don't have a daughter to pass them on to"); Viv arrives announcing she is leaving for university to read Black Studies; both routes complete on the same closing horizon.

• Topic sentence: Where Scene 5 had pressure-tested the two routes through protective divergence, Scene 8 stages their convergence — Viv's choice to read Black Studies at university paired with Del's receipt of Mai's notebook, the academic-formal route and the body-to-body route arriving at the same inheritance the older generation has been carrying.

• Technique + evidence: Pinnock deploys structural convergence inside recurring object — Viv announcing her departure for university to read Black Studies paired with Mai's "I don't have a daughter to pass them on to" and "You passed with flying colours. You're a natural" as Mai's notebook passes to Del — combining structural convergence (the academic route and the cultural-inheritance route arriving as complementary rather than competing forms of claiming) with recurring object (Mai's hand-written notebook as the inheritance in material form, the chosen-maternal transmission running parallel to Viv's university choice).

• Close analysis: Viv's announced "Black Studies" converts the Scene 2 Brooke recitation into a reclamation route — the same canon that had refused to hold the daughter now answered by a discipline the daughter herself will study — while Mai's notebook in Del's hand converts the Scene 1 hostile theft of the charm into legitimate transmission of the chosen-maternal practice; the two routes the female line invented arrive at the same inheritance in two different registers inside the same closing minutes.

• Effect on audience 1: The audience hears Viv's "Black Studies" announcement and Mai's "Is time. You ready" in the same scene and recognises Pinnock's argument that the diasporic inheritance can be received in more than one form — the formal-educational route reclaiming what the canon had refused, the body-to-body route receiving what the practitioner had been holding for the trained successor.

• Effect on audience 2: The audience watches the closing image — Del reading Enid's palm with the notebook beside her, Viv leaving for the academy — and registers the sibling relationship as the female line's structural argument that the second generation needed both routes to claim the inheritance the migration had tried to break.

• Author's purpose: Pinnock argues here that the diasporic sibling relationship is the female line's invention of two complementary routes — the academic and the body-to-body, the formal and the inherited — and that the closing convergence is her clearest argument that neither route is sufficient alone and that both are needed to claim what the host country had taught the second generation to refuse.

• Connective: The first British academic Centre for the Study of Race and Ethnicity emerged inside the 1980s, and the Black British literary and cultural studies movement Pinnock joined gave Caribbean-British students the formal-educational route Viv announces — the route running parallel to the chosen-maternal transmission Mai has just completed in the same closing scene.

• Restated thesis: In conclusion, Pinnock dramatises the Del-Viv sibling relationship as the female line's two-route response to the same household pressure — Scene 2 Brooke recitation paired with the sound-system speech installing the two routes back-to-back, Scene 5 the protective imperative "Go back to school and sit that exam" registering costly divergence, Scene 8 "Black Studies" and Mai's notebook converging on the same closing horizon.

• Controlling concept: What Scene 2 installed as the two daughters' contrasting first speech-acts, Scene 5 pressured into divergent paths under household crisis, Scene 8 reconciled as complementary halves of the same female-line claiming — three stages of the same sibling argument, the second generation needing both routes to receive the inheritance the migration had tried to erase.

• Author's purpose: Writing inside the 1987 cultural moment that gave the diasporic second generation the formal-educational route Viv chooses, Pinnock honours both daughters equally and argues that the female line's claiming of the inheritance has had to operate in more than one register.

• Ultimate moral: The Del-Viv sibling relationship in Leave Taking is the play's quietest structural argument — the diasporic inheritance can be received in more than one form, and the closing image, with Del's hand on Enid's palm and Viv's "Black Studies" still in the air, is the female line's two routes meeting on the same horizon at last.

C10 — Viv

Question

How does Pinnock present Viv in Leave Taking?

Write about:

- how Pinnock presents Viv at different points in the play

- how Pinnock uses Viv to convey her ideas.

[30 marks + 4 AO4 = 34 marks]

Frame. Viv is Del's foil and Pinnock's second route to claiming the inheritance — the studious sixteen-year-old GCSE student whose Scene 2 Rupert Brooke recitation installs the host country's canon inside her voice, whose Scene 5 "I'm never there… I need another language to express myself" exposes the curriculum's cost, and whose Scene 8 announcement that she will read Black Studies at university converts the assimilation route into a reclamation route. The plan tracks three stages — Scene 2 curriculum-self installed (Viv stands and recites "A dust whom England bore, shaped, made aware" on Enid's prompt and asks the audience-surrogate question "And what if I fail my exams? What'll she do then?"), Scene 5 the curriculum cost named (the "another language" speech and the off-stage missed exam), Scene 8 Black Studies announced (the formal-educational route reclaiming the inheritance Mai is transmitting body-to-body to Del in the same closing scene).

Technique gloss:

• Foil character — character whose contrast illuminates the protagonist (Viv to Del).

• Recitation as curriculum-self — Viv's Rupert Brooke recitation as the host country's syllabus installed inside the daughter's voice.

• Audience-surrogate questioning — Viv's "And what if I fail my exams?" and "How can we ever live up to that?" asking on the audience's behalf.

• Confessional first-person — Viv's "I'm never there… I need another language to express myself" as the curriculum cost named in her own voice.

• Off-stage event — the missed English Literature exam in Scene 5 as the institution kept off-stage.

• Structural convergence — Viv's Black Studies announcement meeting Del's body-to-body reception on the same closing horizon.

• Hook (historical fact): The first British academic Centre for the Study of Race and Ethnicity emerged inside the 1980s, and the Black British literary movement (Caryl Phillips's The Final Passage, 1985; Joan Riley's The Unbelonging, 1985; the Black British women writers Pinnock herself would join) was already beginning to contest the GCSE English Literature canon that examined Caribbean-British students on a syllabus carrying no trace of their inheritance — the same canon Viv recites from in Scene 2 and reclaims as a discipline in Scene 8.

• Building sentence (historical context): Born 1961 in Islington to Jamaican parents who arrived in the Windrush years, Pinnock had passed through the comprehensive English Literature curriculum and the Royal Court Young Writers' Group before completing her own playwriting career — and her construction of Viv as the studious sixteen-year-old daughter carries the closest autobiographical proximity in the play's three-generation household, the curriculum-self the playwright herself converted into the reclamation Viv announces in Scene 8.

• Thesis: Across her Scene 2 standing recitation of Rupert Brooke's "A dust whom England bore, shaped, made aware" on Enid's prompt and her audience-surrogate question "And what if I fail my exams? What'll she do then?", her Scene 5 confessional "no matter how hard I search for myself in them books, I'm never there… I need another language to express myself", and her Scene 8 announcement that she will read Black Studies at university, Pinnock presents Viv as the second route to claiming the diasporic inheritance — formal-educational where Del's is body-to-body, and the route that converts the curriculum from a syllabus to a reclamation.

Scene focus: Enid's living room; the pastor's visit; Enid prompts Viv to "tell him who you are" and Viv stands and recites Rupert Brooke; Brod arrives, the Gullyman parable surfaces, and Viv's audience-surrogate question "Kick us out? Where would me and Del go?" registers the second-generation precarity.

• Topic sentence: Pinnock first installs Viv as the household's curriculum-self — the studious sixteen-year-old who stands at her mother's prompt and recites the host country's literary canon as the audible form of the assimilation route the family has been betting on.

• Technique + evidence: Pinnock deploys recitation as curriculum-self inside audience-surrogate questioning — the stage direction "(stands, recites) 'A dust whom England bore, shaped, made aware'" followed by Viv's "How can we ever live up to that? We don't deserve it, Brod" and "Kick us out? Where would me and Del go?" — combining recitation as curriculum-self (Rupert Brooke's First-World-War poem installed inside the British-born daughter's voice as the syllabus's most loyalist line, "A dust whom England bore, shaped, made aware" converting the daughter into the host country's product) with audience-surrogate questioning (Viv's two questions converting the household's silent anxiety into the questions the audience needs the play to ask).

• Close analysis: The stage direction "(stands, recites)" turns Viv's body into the host country's curriculum-self before she has spoken a line of her own — the formal upright posture of the school recitation imported into Enid's living room — while "How can we ever live up to that?" and "Kick us out? Where would me and Del go?" register the cost the curriculum-self has been compressing inside the same daughter, the recitation answered by the questions the canon does not address.

• Effect on audience 1: The audience hears Viv reciting Brooke on her mother's prompt and registers Pinnock's argument that the second-generation daughter has been raised inside a canon-curriculum the host country has not yet made room for her inside — the daughter standing to perform a poem about being "shaped, made aware" by an England whose 1981 BNA had just stripped her mother's automatic citizenship.

• Effect on audience 2: The audience watches Viv carry the household's hopes into the room and recognises her as the family's bet on the formal-educational route — the daughter whose recitation is the audible form of the assimilation Enid has staked everything on, even as Viv's own questions register the precarity of the wager.

• Author's purpose: Pinnock argues here that the second-generation daughter has been raised inside a canon-curriculum that the host country has loaded onto her body before her own inheritance has been made available to her — and that the play's work will be giving the same daughter the language to reclaim the inheritance the recitation has been compressing.

• Connective: Scene 5 will let Viv name in her own voice what the Brooke recitation had not yet been able to acknowledge — the cost of being examined on a canon that has not made room for her inheritance.

Scene focus: Mai's living room; Del has moved in, in the early stages of pregnancy; Viv arrives with the saved university money Enid forced on her in Scene 4 and tells Del she has walked out of her English Literature exam; her confessional speech names the curriculum-self's cost; Del orders her back to the exam.

• Topic sentence: Where Scene 2 had installed the curriculum-self inside the family room, Scene 5 stages its cost named in the daughter's own voice — Viv finally articulating what the Brooke recitation had compressed, the assimilation route exposed as a syllabus that has examined her without making room for what she carries.

• Technique + evidence: Pinnock deploys confessional first-person inside the off-stage event — Viv's "Oh, I knew all the answers. Pat me on the head and they all come tumbling out, say exactly what the examiners want to hear. But no matter how hard I search for myself in them books, I'm never there… Things I feel they haven't got words for. I need another language to express myself" set against the off-stage English Literature exam being sat without her and Del's "Go back to school and sit that exam" — combining confessional first-person (Viv's "I knew all the answers" shifting into "I'm never there" and "I need another language") with the off-stage event (the institution kept off-stage like every other antagonistic British space).

• Close analysis: Viv's verb "search" registers a daughter who has been actively looking inside the canon for an inheritance the canon does not contain, while "I'm never there" converts the host country's syllabus into a literal absence — examination on a literature that does not include her; "I need another language" names the formal-educational deficit her route has been compressing, the curriculum-self of Scene 2 finally articulated as a route the daughter has outgrown.

• Effect on audience 1: The audience hears Viv name in her own voice what the Brooke recitation had not yet been able to acknowledge — the curriculum-self examined on a canon in which she cannot find herself — and registers the play's argument that the assimilation route is far more precarious than the family's investment in it admits.

• Effect on audience 2: The audience watches Viv walk out of the exam and is then watched by Del refusing the saved university money and ordering her back, and recognises the curriculum-self's cost as the structural condition the second-generation daughter has been carrying — the canon producing a daughter who can pass the exam but cannot find her inheritance inside the books she is examined on.

• Author's purpose: Pinnock argues here that the formal-educational route is the family's contracted route into the host country's institutions and that the institutions themselves have produced exactly the dissonance Viv articulates — the daughter has done everything the syllabus asked and the syllabus has still failed to reach the inheritance she carries.

• Connective: Scene 8 will give Viv the second register — the academic discipline that does carry the inheritance, the reclamation route the canon's absences had been producing across the 1980s.

Scene focus: Mai's cleaned bedsit; Mai puts Del through the "Clairvoyance and Spiritual Aptitude" exam and hands over the notebook; Viv arrives announcing she is leaving for university to read Black Studies; the formal-educational route reclaims the inheritance the curriculum had refused.

• Topic sentence: Where Scene 5 had named the curriculum-self's cost, Scene 8 confirms its eventual reclamation — Viv announcing she will read Black Studies at university, the assimilation route the Brooke recitation had installed converting into a reclamation route inside the same closing scene that hands Mai's notebook to Del.

• Technique + evidence: Pinnock deploys structural convergence inside Viv's declarative announcement — Viv leaving for university to read Black Studies set against Del's reception of Mai's notebook ("You passed with flying colours. You're a natural"; "Is time. You ready"; "I don't have a daughter to pass them on to") in the same Scene 8 cleaned bedsit — combining structural convergence (the formal-educational route converging with the body-to-body route inside one closing image) with the declarative naming of "Black Studies" (the discipline the canon's 1980s absences had produced as the academic answer to the same syllabus Viv walked out of in Scene 5).

• Close analysis: Viv's announced "Black Studies" names the discipline as the formal-educational version of the inheritance Mai is transmitting through the notebook — the same cultural memory entering Viv's life through the academy and Del's life through the bedsit — and the Scene 2 "A dust whom England bore, shaped, made aware" recitation finds its structural answer in a daughter who has chosen to study the inheritance the dust was made of, the canon-curriculum at last converted into the reclamation the daughter herself directs.

• Effect on audience 1: The audience hears Viv's announcement and registers the play's most quietly hopeful image — the curriculum-self who had been "never there" inside Brooke and the English Literature exam now reading a discipline in which she can find what the canon had refused her, the formal-educational route reclaimed for the inheritance rather than weaponised against it.

• Effect on audience 2: The audience watches the two daughters' routes resolve on the same closing horizon — Viv leaving for the academy with the discipline she has chosen, Del receiving the notebook from the chosen mother — and recognises the play's structural argument that the diasporic inheritance can be received in more than one form.

• Author's purpose: Pinnock argues here that the formal-educational route can be reclaimed when the academy makes room for the diasporic canon — and that Viv's "Black Studies" is her clearest argument that the second-generation daughter can convert the host country's curriculum into the discipline the inheritance had been waiting for.

• Connective: Stuart Hall's Cultural Identity and Diaspora (1990) names diasporic identity as "a matter of 'becoming' as well as of 'being'"; Viv's Scene 8 announcement is exactly that becoming, the curriculum-self of Scene 2 converted into the discipline the daughter herself will study inside the 1980s academy.

• Restated thesis: In conclusion, Pinnock constructs Viv as Del's foil and the play's second route to claiming the diasporic inheritance — Scene 2 curriculum-self installed through the Brooke recitation and the audience-surrogate questions, Scene 5 curriculum cost named in "I'm never there… I need another language to express myself", Scene 8 Black Studies announced as the reclamation route the canon's absences had been producing.

• Controlling concept: What Scene 2 installed as the family's bet on assimilation through education, Scene 5 exposed as a route the institution had never made room for, Scene 8 reclaimed as a discipline the daughter could direct herself — the inheritance entering Viv's life through the academy as it enters Del's through the bedsit.

• Author's purpose: Writing closer to Viv than to any other character in the play, Pinnock honours the studious second-generation daughter whose route the canon had not made room for, and whose eventual claiming of Black Studies is the formal-educational version of the inheritance she was always already carrying.

• Ultimate moral: The closing image — Viv leaving for the academy and Del reading her own mother's palm with Mai's notebook beside her — is the play's argument that the diasporic inheritance can be received in more than one register, and that the canon-curriculum can be converted into the discipline the daughter herself directs.

T1 — Identity, Britishness and Caribbean inheritance

Question

How does Pinnock present ideas about identity in Leave Taking?

Write about:

- what different characters say and do

- how Pinnock presents ideas about identity.

[30 marks + 4 AO4 = 34 marks]

Frame. Identity is the play's defining theme — Del's Scene 2 sound-system speech installing the second-generation's claim to Black British culture, Viv's Scene 5 "I'm never there" exposing the canon-curriculum that refused her, and the Scene 8 cleaned bedsit converting identity from a category to a practice the daughter performs. The plan tracks three stages — Scene 2 Black-British register installed inside Enid's living room, Scene 5 Viv's "black ants" reading and her rejection of the Euro-centric curriculum, Scene 8 inheritance received and Black Studies announced.

Pedagogical depth to thread. The Caribbean-British double-consciousness reading + the Obeah-as-alternative-epistemology reading (see top of document). Identity in the diaspora is claimed by excavating the cultural inheritance the host country taught the second generation to refuse. Thread into BP2 or the conclusion.

Technique gloss:

• Patois vs south-east London speech — generational sound-divide as the diasporic identity question staged in phonemes.

• Direct address inside accumulating imagery — Del's police-vans / managers / bowing-and-scraping triad (Scene 2).

• Sound-system motif — Del's "bass… like one big pulsing heart" installing Black British youth culture inside the household.

• Extended simile — Mai's "words run across the page like black ants" and Viv's "search for myself in them books, I'm never there".

• Cyclical structure — Mai's bedsit returned to in Scene 8 as the room of identity finally claimed.

• Cultural-inheritance ritual — Obeah as identity received through inherited practice rather than through host-country acceptance.

• Hook (historical fact): Winsome Pinnock, born 1961 to Jamaican parents in Islington, wrote Leave Taking in 1987 inside the cultural emergence of Black British identity as a distinct political and aesthetic category — and her staging of two second-generation daughters across eight scenes converts that emergence into the territory the diasporic family has had to invent inside one North London living room.

• Building sentence (historical context): Pinnock anchors the identity question inside the 1981 cluster that had named it publicly — the New Cross fire of 18 January, the Brixton uprising of 10–12 April, the 1981 British Nationality Act stripping the 1948 Act's automatic citizenship — and inside the 1948–1971 Windrush arrival window that had brought Del and Viv's mother to a Britain whose 1962 Commonwealth Immigrants Act and 1971 Immigration Act would later retrospectively void the right to remain.

• Thesis: Across Scene 2 in which Del's sound-system speech ("The bass is mad. You wanna see it pounding the walls, like one big pulsing heart") installs the second generation's claim to a specifically Black British culture, Scene 5 in which Viv's "no matter how hard I search for myself in them books, I'm never there… I need another language to express myself" exposes the canon that has had no room for her, and Scene 8 in which Del's reception of Mai's handwritten book and Viv's announcement of Black Studies stage identity as inheritance claimed in two complementary registers, Pinnock presents identity as the territory the diasporic second generation has had to invent for itself.

Scene focus: Enid's North London living room; Del returns in her work uniform having stayed at the sound system and lost her job; her indictment of the manager and of Enid's "beloved England"; the hospital-party speech that ends in the slap.

• Topic sentence: Pinnock first presents identity as something the British-born daughter has been forced to claim against the assimilationist position her Windrush mother has been required to hold, the second generation's voice installed inside the very living room the credo had been intended to defend.

• Technique + evidence: Pinnock deploys direct address inside accumulating imagery — Del's "But what you give us that we can use out there? You don't see the police vans hunting us down, or the managers who treat us like we're the lowest of the low. You're too busy bowing and scraping to your beloved England" — combining the second-person "you" that converts the mother into the speech's defendant with the police-vans / managers / bowing-and-scraping triad that names institutional racism, workplace racism and assimilationist posture in three concrete images.

• Close analysis: The participles "hunting" and "bowing and scraping" turn institutions into agents and Enid into a posture, the grammar enacting Del's argument that the host country has produced her mother's deference rather than rewarded it.

• Effect on audience 1: The audience hears the second-generation register installed in the same scene as the manager's racism Del has just escaped ("He talks to me as if I can't speak English"), the daughter speaking the host country's English at the very mother whose patois the host country had taught her to refine away.

• Effect on audience 2: The audience registers Del's sound-system speech ("I been following that sound system for years. The bass is mad… When that bass gets inside you and flings you round the room you can't do nothing to stop it") as the positive content of the same identity claim — Black British youth culture installed as the daughter's actual belonging.

• Author's purpose: Pinnock argues here that the second generation's identity is being built on a register the host country has not authorised — police-vans diagnosis on one side, sound-system culture on the other — and that the daughter's anger is a form of seeing the assimilationist mother cannot yet afford.

• Connective: That register, however, is still being defined against the inheritance the daughter has not yet been handed — Scene 5 will stage Viv inside the school the host country built, naming the absence the canon has imposed.

Scene focus: Mai's bedsit, a few weeks later; Del has moved in with Mai, pregnant; Viv arrives with Enid's saved university money having walked out of her English Literature exam; she names the canon that has refused her.

• Topic sentence: Where Scene 2 had installed the daughter's voice against assimilation, Scene 5 names the school the assimilation was meant to win — the studious second-generation daughter walking out of an English Literature exam because the canon has refused to make room for her.

• Technique + evidence: Pinnock deploys extended simile inside diagnostic confession — Viv's "Oh, I knew all the answers. Pat me on the head and they all come tumbling out, say exactly what the examiners want to hear. But no matter how hard I search for myself in them books, I'm never there… Things I feel they haven't got words for. I need another language to express myself" set alongside Mai's earlier "words run across the page like black ants" reading of Del — combining the "search for myself in them books, I'm never there" indictment of the canon with the doubled image of letters refusing both daughters in two distinct ways.

• Close analysis: The colloquial "I'm never there" converts identity into a question of representation rather than belief, while "I need another language to express myself" registers that the host country's English has been the very instrument of the absence — the canon Viv aced has produced her invisibility.

• Effect on audience 1: The audience watches the model-student daughter who had recited Brooke's "A dust whom England bore, shaped, made aware" in Scene 2 now refuse the bargain, naming the price the curriculum had been quietly extracting.

• Effect on audience 2: The audience registers Mai's "black ants" reading as the alternative epistemology already present in the room — the Caribbean practitioner's hand naming what the British classroom had labelled "slow", identity diagnosed in vocabulary the host country had criminalised.

• Author's purpose: Pinnock argues here that the diasporic second generation's identity has been constructed inside an absence — the canon, the syllabus, the examination room — and that the daughter's "another language" is the play's name for the inheritance the host country has been refusing to count.

• Connective: That refusal sets up Scene 8, where both daughters reach the inheritance in two complementary forms — the body-to-body practice Mai hands to Del, and the formal-educational discipline Viv chooses for herself.

Scene focus: Mai's cleaned bedsit; Del receives the handwritten book ("I don't have a daughter to pass them on to"); Enid arrives as Del's first client and announces Viv is "Black Studies" at university; the closing palm-reading.

• Topic sentence: Where Scene 5 had named the absence, Scene 8 stages the answer — identity received through inherited practice and claimed through a chosen discipline, the second generation's two routes converging on the same horizon.

• Technique + evidence: Pinnock deploys cyclical structure inside doubled inheritance-naming — the same Mai's bedsit that had opened the play "Very messy" now cleaned by Del ("First time this place's been cleaned in years"), with Mai handing Del her notebook ("I don't have a daughter to pass them on to") and Enid reporting Viv's university choice ("Black Studies. You ever hear of such a thing?") — combining the cyclical setting (the unchanged room measuring the changed daughter) with the doubled inheritance-naming (the Obeah notebook and the academic discipline arriving as complementary forms of the same reclamation).

• Close analysis: The unchanged room measures the changed daughter — what looked like a fading practice in Scene 1 has, by Scene 8, gained the practitioner it required — while Enid's bewildered "You ever hear of such a thing?" registers that the second-generation daughters have invented routes the Windrush generation never had the vocabulary to anticipate.

• Effect on audience 1: The audience watches Del's hand close on a Caribbean spiritual notebook the British state had criminalised, and registers Pinnock's argument that the inheritance survives by being received rather than preserved.

• Effect on audience 2: The audience receives the closing palm-reading — Enid finally permitted to confess "How you going teach you children that they don't exist?" and "I would chop off my hand if it would help you" — as the moment identity arrives as a verb the daughter performs rather than a category the host country awards.

• Author's purpose: Pinnock makes her clearest argument about identity here — that the diasporic second generation can claim what the host country taught them to refuse, and that the claiming is registered in two complementary registers: the body-to-body Caribbean practice Mai passes hand-to-hand, and the academic discipline (Black Studies) the canon's absence had finally produced.

• Connective: Obeah was criminalised in Jamaica under the Obeah Act of 1898 and remained illegal there when the play premiered; Del's receipt of Mai's notebook and Viv's reclamation of Black Studies are the play's twin answers to a host country that had spent two centuries refusing the inheritance the daughters now claim.

• Restated thesis: In conclusion, Pinnock dramatises identity through three stages — Scene 2 second-generation register installed against assimilation, Scene 5 canon's absence exposed and "another language" demanded, Scene 8 inheritance received and Black Studies announced — the diasporic second generation inventing the territory the host country had not made for it.

• Controlling concept: What Scene 2 staged as the daughter's voice against the assimilationist credo, Scene 5 named as the absence the canon had imposed, Scene 8 answered as inheritance claimed in two complementary forms — three stages of the same identity argument, the third territory built inside the rooms the older generation had been keeping.

• Author's purpose: Writing inside the 1987 cultural emergence of Black British identity, Pinnock argues that identity in the diaspora is something invented rather than inherited unchanged — and that the inventing is the work of the second generation operating across the bedsit and the academy at once.

• Ultimate moral: Identity in Leave Taking is a practice Del begins rather than a category she occupies — the closing palm-reading inside Mai's cleaned bedsit, with Viv's Black Studies announcement still ringing in the room, names the second generation's claim to what the host country had taught them to refuse.

T2 — Belonging and displacement

Question

How does Pinnock present ideas about belonging in Leave Taking?

Write about:

- what different characters say and do

- how Pinnock presents ideas about belonging and displacement.

[30 marks + 4 AO4 = 34 marks]

Frame. Belonging in Leave Taking is the play's most spec-anchored theme — the question of where the diasporic family stands when neither the host country nor the homeland has made room for them, and how the second generation invents belonging when the older generation could not inherit it whole. The plan tracks three stages — Scene 2 displacement diagnosed inside the household (Brod's nationality letter, Del's "No wonder Dad left you"), Scene 6 Mai's "It mash up you life" naming the migration cost, Scene 8 belonging built rather than awarded inside the cleaned bedsit.

Pedagogical depth to thread. The Caribbean-British double-consciousness reading. Belonging in the diaspora has to be invented rather than received because the host country has refused it and the homeland has become unreachable. Thread into BP2 or BP3.

Technique gloss:

• Autobiographical declarative inside ironic reversal — Brod's Empire-Day-to-nationality-letter turn (Scene 2).

• Cyclical structure — Mai's bedsit at Scene 1 "messy" and Scene 8 cleaned, the room where displacement begins and belonging is built.

• Off-stage space — workplace, school, Home Office, Jamaica all kept off-stage to register their inaccessibility.

• Patois aphorism — Mai's diagnostic compression "It mash up you life" (Scene 6).

• Cultural-inheritance ritual — the practice as the third territory belonging is built inside.

• Stage business — Del's cleaning of Mai's bedsit ("First time this place's been cleaned in years") as the wordless construction of belonging.

• Hook (historical fact): Winsome Pinnock, writing Leave Taking in 1987 inside the cumulative legislative chain (1948 BNA, 1962 CIA, 1971 IA, 1981 BNA) that had progressively converted the British-subject passports Caribbean migrants had arrived on into contested documents, made belonging the structural question her title poses, her opening setting installs, and her closing image answers.

• Building sentence (historical context): Born in Islington in 1961 to Jamaican parents who had crossed the Atlantic in the Windrush years, Pinnock anchors her belonging question inside the 1981 cluster — Brixton uprising, Toxteth riots, New Cross fire — that had named publicly what 1980s Caribbean-British families were living, the same conditions whose furthest extreme would later produce the 2018 Windrush deportation scandal.

• Thesis: Across Scene 2 in which Brod's ambushed-citizenship speech ("Then them send me letter say if me don't get me nationality paper in order they going kick me outta the country") and Del's bitter "Yes, England loves you, all right. No wonder Dad left you" install displacement as the household's structural condition, Scene 6 in which Mai's "It not easy to turn you back on one country and start fresh in foreign. It mash up you life" names the cost of the leave-taking, and Scene 8 in which the cleaned bedsit, the handwritten notebook and the palm-reading stage belonging built rather than awarded, Pinnock dramatises belonging as the territory the diasporic second generation has had to construct because neither the host country nor the homeland had made it available.

Scene focus: Enid's North London living room; Brod's "nationality paper" speech reveals the Home Office threat; the £50 "nationality secure" exchange; Del's homecoming and her demolition of Enid's "England been good to me" credo.

• Topic sentence: Pinnock first presents belonging as something the legislative chain had already converted into a contingency, the diasporic household built inside a host country that has been quietly revoking the right to remain.

Things happen you wouldn't imagine" — combining the patois compression (the homeland-grammar carrying the diagnosis the host country has no vocabulary for) with the consultation form (the Caribbean spiritual practice doing the institutional work the post-war NHS pathologised) inside the same scene.
