• Effect on audience 1: The audience hears the diagnosis in the migrant's own register — the cost of belonging spoken at last in a vocabulary the host country had refused to authorise, the Caribbean practitioner producing the analysis the British state had abandoned to her.

• Author's purpose: Writing inside the 1987 cultural moment that named Black British identity as a distinct political and aesthetic category, Pinnock argues that belonging in the diaspora is something constructed rather than awarded — and that the construction is the work of the second generation inside the rooms the older generation has been keeping.

T3 — The mother-daughter relationship

Question

How does Pinnock present the relationship between mothers and daughters in Leave Taking?

Write about:

- how Pinnock presents the mother-daughter relationship at different points in the play

- how Pinnock uses the mother-daughter relationship to convey her ideas.

[30 marks + 4 AO4 = 34 marks]

Frame. The mother-daughter line is the structural device through which Pinnock carries the diasporic inheritance — Enid and Del across the eight scenes, Enid and her dying mother (Mooma) in Jamaica off-stage, Mai and Del as chosen mother and chosen daughter. The plan tracks three stages — Scene 2 authority-and-sacrifice clash in Enid's living room ending in the slap, Scene 4 inheritance redirected through grieving nostalgia ("chop chop chop"; "I want… I want to go home"), Scene 8 broken-speech confession and palm-reading as inheritance received.

Technique gloss:

• Female-line structure — Enid / Mai / Del / Viv as three generations of Caribbean-British womanhood with Mooma off-stage as the fourth.

• Patois inside sacrifice-speech — Enid's "I work two jobs seven days a week" (Scene 2) authority delivered through itemised cost.

• Stage direction as turning point — (Enid slaps Del. Pause. For a moment it looks as though Del might hit her back.)

• Asyndetic list — Enid's "Old time woman could cook. Roast breadfruit. Callaloo" (Scene 4).

• Onomatopoeic repetition + unfinished sentence — Mooma's "chop chop chop" and Enid's "I want… I want to go home" (Scene 4).

• Broken speech inside cultural-inheritance ritual — Enid's Scene 8 confession ("Nobody see you, nobody hear you") delivered under Del's reading hand.

• Hook (historical fact): Winsome Pinnock, daughter of Jamaican parents who arrived in the Windrush years, wrote Leave Taking in 1987 inside a Royal Court Young Writers' Group whose North London catchment had filled with mother-daughter households the British stage had never centred — and her construction of biological (Enid–Del) and chosen (Mai–Del) mother-daughter pairs converts that historical absence into a sequence of carefully staged confrontations.

• Building sentence (historical context): Pinnock anchors the mother-daughter axis inside the 1948–1971 Windrush arrival window opened by the 1948 British Nationality Act and the male Windrush figures' breakage by workplace racism, housing discrimination through the "colour bar" and SUS-law policing — the cumulative conditions that left the diasporic inheritance to travel almost entirely through the female line.

• Thesis: Across Enid's Scene 2 sacrifice-speech ("I work two jobs seven days a week to put food in you belly and roof over you head") and the slap that ends the row, her Scene 4 grieving recall of Mooma's "chop chop chop" farewell-refusal and the redirected inheritance she presses on Viv, and her Scene 8 broken-speech confession ("Nobody see you, nobody hear you … I would chop off my hand if it would help you") at Mai's cleaned bedsit under Del's reading hand, Pinnock dramatises the mother-daughter relationship as the structural device through which the female line carries the inheritance migration tried to break.

Scene focus: Enid's North London living room; Del returns in her work uniform having lost her job at the sound system; Enid catches her in the lie; the row escalates through the "I hate it here" / "You ungrateful…" exchange to the hospital-party speech and the slap.

• Topic sentence: Pinnock first presents the mother-daughter relationship as authority enforced through itemised sacrifice, the Windrush mother delivering her discipline in the only register the host country has left her — the receipt of what she has already paid.

• Technique + evidence: Pinnock deploys patois inside sacrifice-speech accumulation — Enid's "Long as you living under my roof you will work. I don't want people saying we lazy" and "I work two jobs seven days a week to put food in you belly and roof over you head. I wear one dress, one pair a shoes with hole in it so that you can dress like those children who have fathers. People laugh at me, but they never laugh at you" — combining the patois grammar ("in you belly", "a shoes with hole in it") with the itemised parallelism (two jobs, seven days, one dress, one pair of shoes) that converts maternal authority into a balance sheet.

• Close analysis: The grammatical parallelism of "two jobs" / "seven days" / "one dress" / "one pair a shoes" delivers authority as audited cost — the host country's two-job week and worn-out shoes built into the discipline — while the slap stage direction (Enid slaps Del. Pause. For a moment it looks as though Del might hit her back.) registers the moment authority and content collapse into the same gesture.

• Effect on audience 1: The audience hears Enid's discipline arriving in the receipt of her own labour — the Windrush mother's authority indistinguishable from the sacrifices the host country has extracted, the maternal voice and the immigrant-labour voice fused inside the same speech.

• Effect on audience 2: The audience watches the slap land as the moment the speech-economy fails — Del's "Yes, England loves you, all right. No wonder Dad left you" exceeds what Enid's itemised sacrifice can absorb, and the mother's hand replaces the mother's argument.

• Author's purpose: Pinnock argues here that the diasporic mother-daughter relationship is structured around inherited sacrifice the daughter has not been asked to ratify — the receipt the older generation has paid is being presented as the bill the younger generation must honour, and the slap is what happens when the daughter refuses the terms.

• Connective: Scene 4 will name what Enid has not told her daughters about the receipt — the homeland mother who never said goodbye, and the inheritance the leave-taking forced Enid to redirect through Viv's university place.

Scene focus: Enid sits drinking in the dark on the sofa after Cynthia's telephone call has confirmed Mooma's death; Viv listens; Enid recalls her Jamaican childhood, the moment she left for England, and forces the saved university money on Viv; the scene closes on "I want… I want to go home."

• Topic sentence: Where Scene 2 had installed the mother-daughter relationship as authority through sacrifice, Scene 4 reveals what the older generation has been carrying inside that authority — mourning a homeland mother she could not return to bury, the inheritance redirected from Jamaica to a university place in London.

• Technique + evidence: Pinnock deploys asyndetic list inside onomatopoeic repetition — Enid's "Old time woman could cook. Roast breadfruit. Callaloo" set against her account of leaving Mooma ("'Mooma,' I say, 'I gone now.' She never turn round, jus' carry on working, chop chop chop, play deaf. In the end I had to give up, walk away. (Slight pause.) It wasn't easy to leave") closing on "I want… I want to go home" — combining asyndeton (three Jamaican dishes set down without conjunctions, each item left alone like an unfinished sentence) with the triple "chop" (the cutlass-stroke sounding the refusal that the daughter could not get a goodbye through) and the unfinished-sentence syntax of the closing wish.

• Close analysis: The asyndetic refusal to join the dishes with "and" leaves each Jamaican food standing alone like an unfinished sentence, the triple "chop" gives the homeland mother's labour a sound and the daughter's leave-taking a percussion, and the closing "I want… I want to go home" registers a homeland the legislative chain has placed permanently beyond return.

• Effect on audience 1: The audience hears the play's title-image realised — "leave taking" is not a one-time act in 1959 but a sound that has been repeating in Enid's head for eighteen years, the homeland reduced to the noise of refused farewell.

• Effect on audience 2: The audience watches Enid press the saved building-society money on Viv — the saved Caribbean inheritance redirected into a London university place — and registers Pinnock's argument that the diasporic mother-daughter inheritance has had to be redirected because the original homeland inheritance the daughter left behind cannot be returned to.

• Author's purpose: Pinnock argues here that the mother-daughter inheritance in a Windrush household is the inheritance the migration has redirected — from cutlass and callaloo to a university place, from a homeland that cannot be returned to a host country that will not make room — and that the redirection is the cost the female line has been paying.

• Connective: Scene 8 will gather the unfinished sentence of Scene 4 into the bigger broken-speech confession at Mai's cleaned bedsit, where the daughter Enid had given up on finally builds the room inside which the older generation's silenced truths can be spoken aloud.

Scene focus: Mai's cleaned bedsit; Del has received the handwritten notebook; Enid arrives as Del's first client; the eighteen-year silence breaks ("Nobody see you, nobody hear you"; "I would chop off my hand if it would help you"); Del reads her mother's palm.

• Topic sentence: Where Scene 4 had revealed the redirection of the inheritance, Scene 8 stages its possible repair — the trained daughter finally able to make a room inside which the mother's silenced truths can be spoken, and the palm-reading converting the mother-daughter relationship into the play's closing image.

• Technique + evidence: Pinnock deploys broken speech inside cultural-inheritance ritual — Enid's "(Pause. It isn't easy for Enid to say what she has to say.) Nobody see you, nobody hear you … All the time you screaming, you screaming inside but nobody come" leading to "How you going teach you children that they don't exist?…You say I don't see how them treat you out there. I see it. I see it and it make me want to tear the place down. I would chop off my hand if it would help you" — combining the anaphoric doubled negation ("nobody see you, nobody hear you"; "I see it. I see it") with the closing wish ("someone to gather… gather me up and touch my cheek like I was a prize, not a curse, and stroke my hair like Mooma never could") so the confession arrives in clauses too painful to complete.

• Close analysis: The doubled "I see it" repairs the doubled "nobody see you" — eighteen years of host-country invisibility answered by a Windrush mother finally testifying that she has been watching her daughter's reality all along — while the closing "like Mooma never could" converts the Scene 4 "chop chop chop" into a four-generation wound the daughter's now-cleaned attention can finally hold.

• Effect on audience 1: The audience hears the Windrush mother permitted, by her trained daughter, to articulate what eighteen years of host-country suppression had kept unsaid — the speech delivered inside Mai's cleaned bedsit rather than Enid's living room, the cultural-inheritance space rather than the family-assimilation space.

• Effect on audience 2: The audience receives Del's palm-reading of Enid as the play's quietest reversal — the daughter who in Scene 2 had answered her mother's slap with "That's the last time" now holds the same mother's hand inside Mai's room, the mother-daughter line reaching the only repair the play has been preparing.

• Author's purpose: Pinnock argues here at two audiences at once — to the first-generation Windrush mothers, that the silences must be broken before the inheritance can be received; to the second-generation daughters, that the inheritance must be received before the silences can be broken.

• Connective: Leave Taking premiered at the Liverpool Playhouse Studio on 11 November 1987 inside the cultural emergence of Black British identity in the wake of the New Cross fire (1981) and the Brixton uprising (1981); the play's mother-daughter structure dramatises the female-line inheritance the 2018 Windrush deportation scandal would later force the wider British public to see.

• Restated thesis: In conclusion, Pinnock dramatises the mother-daughter relationship as the structural device through which the female line carries the diasporic inheritance — Scene 2 authority-and-sacrifice clash ending in the slap, Scene 4 grieving nostalgia and inheritance redirected, Scene 8 broken-speech confession and palm-reading — the doubled coming-of-age confirming that healing requires both halves of the household to move.

• Controlling concept: What Scene 2 installed as authority delivered through itemised sacrifice, Scene 4 named as the unfinished mourning of a homeland that could not be returned to, Scene 8 began to repair through the trained daughter's reception — three stages of the same mother-daughter inheritance, the female line carrying what the migration tried to break.

• Author's purpose: Writing inside the Royal Court Young Writers' Group and drawing on the household founded by her own Jamaican parents, Pinnock honours the diasporic mother-daughter line as the structural device through which the inheritance has had to travel, and through which it can finally be received.

• Ultimate moral: Healing across the diasporic mother-daughter line requires the trained daughter to make a room — Mai's cleaned bedsit, Del's reading hand — inside which the mother's silenced truths can finally be spoken aloud.

T4 — Cultural inheritance

Question

How does Pinnock present ideas about cultural inheritance in Leave Taking?

Write about:

- what different characters do with the inheritance

- how Pinnock presents cultural inheritance.

[30 marks + 4 AO4 = 34 marks]

Frame. Cultural inheritance is Pinnock's core theme at its most structural — the patois that the older generation carries, the Obeah practice that Mai keeps, the handwritten notebook that travels from one practitioner's hand to the next, the alternative system of knowing that the host country could neither acknowledge nor replace. The plan tracks three stages — Scene 1 inheritance precariously alive in the "Very messy" bedsit, Scene 5 the inheritance doing institutional work the British state had abandoned (Mai's "black ants" reading of Del), Scene 8 inheritance transferred body-to-body through the notebook and palm-reading.

Pedagogical depth to thread. The Obeah-as-alternative-epistemology reading + the Caribbean-British double-consciousness reading (see top of document). Cultural inheritance is the alternative knowledge system the host country tried to erase. Thread into BP1 or BP3.

Technique gloss:

• Naturalistic stage direction — Mai's "Very messy" bedsit (Scene 1) and the cock crows sound dignifying the inheritance.

• Patois aphorism — Mai's "Mai see right through to your soul" (Scene 1) installing the practice as cosmology.

• Extended simile — Mai's "words run across the page like black ants" (Scene 5).

• Recurring object — Mai's handwritten notebook of Obeah practice as inheritance in material form.

• Cyclical structure — Mai's bedsit at Scene 1 "Very messy" and Scene 8 cleaned, the room of inheritance.

• Stage direction — "MAI hands DEL the notebook" in Scene 8 as the choreography of the transfer.

• Hook (historical fact): Winsome Pinnock, daughter of Jamaican parents who arrived in the Windrush years, wrote Leave Taking in 1987 inheriting a Caribbean cultural landscape in which Obeah had been criminalised under the Jamaican Obeah Act of 1898 (imprisonment with hard labour for practitioners) — and her staging of the inheritance inside a Deptford bedsit converts more than a century of colonial suppression into the play's structural argument for transmission.

• Building sentence (historical context): Trained at the Royal Court Young Writers' Group, Pinnock anchors the inheritance question inside the 1948–1971 Windrush arrival window that brought patois, Obeah and food memory into North London households, and the SUS-laws / 1981 Brixton uprising decade that produced the diasporic cultural studies movement (Stuart Hall's CCCS work, the first British Centres for the Study of Race and Ethnicity emerging in the 1980s).

• Thesis: Across Scene 1 in which the "Very messy" bedsit, the cock crows sound and Mai's "Mai see right through to your soul" self-naming install the cultural inheritance as precariously alive, Scene 5 in which Mai's "words run across the page like black ants" clairvoyant reading of Del dramatises the inheritance doing the institutional labour the British state had abandoned, and Scene 8 in which the handwritten notebook ("I don't have a daughter to pass them on to") and the closing palm-reading complete the body-to-body transfer to the second generation, Pinnock dramatises cultural inheritance as an alternative knowledge system the diasporic family has had to keep clandestinely against the host country's amnesia.

Scene focus: Mai's Deptford bedsit; the room is "Very messy", the table covered in "papers, playing cards scattered all over, a glass of water and the remains of a half-burnt white candle"; Mai older and drinking stout; a cock crows; Mai introduces herself; Del dismisses the practice as "mumbo-jumbo shit".

• Topic sentence: Pinnock opens the play with the cultural inheritance present in the room rather than spoken about — the practitioner's working equipment and patois grammar registering the inheritance as a complete system of knowing before any client has reached the chair.

• Technique + evidence: Pinnock deploys patois aphorism inside naturalistic stage direction — Mai's "Unno scared a me? Is so young people does scared a obeah woman. Can't keep any secret from Mai. Mai see right through to your soul" spoken inside the "Very messy" bedsit ("papers, playing cards scattered all over, a glass of water and the remains of a half-burnt white candle") — combining the patois self-naming (the third-person "Mai see" converting the practitioner into the seeing-instrument itself) with the naturalistic stage direction (the half-burnt candle and scattered cards staging Obeah as accumulated working knowledge, not as theatrical prop).

• Close analysis: The third-person "Mai see right through to your soul" registers the practitioner as the instrument of the practice — the patois grammar refusing the host country's "I see" — while the "half-burnt" candle and cock crows place the room inside a working continuity, the homeland sound and the practice's daily equipment dignified by the same naturalism Pinnock gives the pastor's visit.

• Effect on audience 1: The audience hears Mai's self-naming as the older Caribbean woman's claim on a vocabulary the host country had criminalised — "obeah woman" spoken without apology, the word the law had punished installed as the name of the practitioner.

• Effect on audience 2: The audience registers the precariousness — an older woman, a thin client list, a messy room, the inheritance held by a single ageing practitioner — and recognises that the play's question will be whether the inheritance finds its next carrier in time.

• Author's purpose: Pinnock argues here that Caribbean spiritual practice is alternative epistemology — a complete system of seeing the host country had attempted to erase by classification — and that Mai's self-introduction is the cosmology naming itself before any client has reached the chair.

• Connective: Scene 5 will stage the system at work — the practitioner reading the British-born daughter in vocabulary the British school never reached for.

Scene focus: Mai's bedsit a few weeks later; Del has moved in with Mai; Mai takes Del's hand and conducts a clairvoyant reading; Mai names the dyslexia Del's school had labelled as "slow".

• Topic sentence: Where Scene 1 had installed the practitioner as the instrument of seeing, Scene 5 stages the practice doing the institutional work the British state had failed to provide — the inheritance proved by what it can name.

• Technique + evidence: Pinnock deploys extended simile inside the consultation form — Mai's "You have a liking for books, but when you read words run across the page like black ants. The teachers say you slow, so you give up and run with a crowd who make you feel like you belong. At least that's what you used to think, but just recently you have had a change of heart" — combining the "black ants" simile (the visual concrete imagery for letters that move and swarm, naming dyslexia in vocabulary the post-war British school never owned) with the diagnostic chain (the liking for books, the perceptual difficulty, the school's mislabel, the substitute community, the recent turn) that builds a portrait the daughter recognises.

• Close analysis: The verb "run" gives the words agency rather than the failing reader, while "black ants" compresses smallness, swarm and breaking-apart into one image, the host country's classification "slow" exposed as misreading; the "change of heart" clause then turns the diagnosis into the start of an apprenticeship rather than a label.

• Effect on audience 1: The audience watches Caribbean spiritual practice produce the diagnosis post-war British education had refused to provide — Mai's hand on Del's hand reading what no teacher's marker had reached.

• Effect on audience 2: The audience registers Mai's gift as the older Caribbean woman holding a complete system of knowing the host country had reclassified as superstition, the inheritance doing institutional labour the host country had abandoned to it.

• Author's purpose: Pinnock argues here that recovery of Mai's practice is recovery of cosmology — the room in which the British-born daughter can finally be seen is the room the colonial state had spent two centuries trying to close.

• Connective: Scene 7's physiognomic recognition ("wide and full between the eyes"; "frontal lobe big where them meet at the nose root"; "And now I see it plain plain") will confirm Mai's reading of Del as the next practitioner, and Scene 8 will stage the inheritance handed over.

Scene focus: Mai's cleaned bedsit ("First time this place's been cleaned in years"); Mai puts Del through her "Clairvoyance and Spiritual Aptitude" exam ("You passed with flying colours.
