• Topic sentence: Where Scene 5 had named Del's gift, Scene 8 stages the inheritance's full transfer through the form the diaspora had kept clandestinely — the handwritten notebook passing from Mai's hand to Del's, the inheritance arriving in its body-to-body form.

• Technique + evidence: Pinnock deploys recurring object inside cyclical structure — "MAI hands DEL the notebook. … I don't have a daughter to pass them on to. And my son... well, he isn't interested" — combining recurring object (the notebook as cultural memory in material form, body-to-body transmission of what enslavement, colonial criminalisation and migration had each tried to erase) with cyclical structure (the unchanged room measuring the changed daughter, the inheritance returning to the same address now ready to be received).

• Close analysis: The handwritten quality of the notebook is the play's quietest argument — the inheritance has reached Del not through print culture but through one practitioner's hand to another, the same hand-to-hand transmission that carried the practice through the Obeah Act, the Middle Passage and the Windrush migration — and "I don't have a daughter to pass them on to" names the gap the practice has been waiting for the host country to leave open.

• Effect on audience 1: The audience feels the closing palm-reading as the inheritance arriving in its earned form — the cultural knowledge transferred body-to-body, the room cleaned, the practitioner trained, the first client a Windrush mother whose silenced truths the practice can finally hold.

• Effect on audience 2: The audience recognises that the inheritance has survived not by being preserved but by being received — and registers Pinnock's argument that transmission, not protection, is the inheritance's only available form of life.

• Author's purpose: Pinnock argues here that cultural inheritance survives through body-to-body transmission rather than through institutional protection, and that the diaspora's task has been keeping the practice alive long enough for the next generation to receive it — chosen-family transmission filling the gap biological transmission has been forced to leave.

• Connective: Paul Gilroy's The Black Atlantic (1993) describes the inheritance of African religious practice carried through the Middle Passage and the diaspora as the structural form of Black-Atlantic identity — and Mai's handwritten notebook is exactly that inheritance staged in 1987 in dramatic miniature.

• Restated thesis: In conclusion, Pinnock dramatises cultural inheritance through three stages — Scene 1 inheritance installed as cosmology in the "Very messy" bedsit, Scene 5 inheritance doing the institutional labour the British state had abandoned in the "black ants" reading, Scene 8 inheritance transferred body-to-body through the handwritten notebook and palm-reading.

• Controlling concept: What Scene 1 installed as a working cosmology, Scene 5 demonstrated as alternative epistemology naming what the host country had refused to see, Scene 8 confirmed as completed transmission — three stages of the same inheritance, the diaspora's alternative knowledge system kept alive through body-to-body transmission against the host country's amnesia.

• Author's purpose: Writing inside a 1987 cultural moment that had inherited the colonial criminalisation of Afro-Caribbean spiritual practice, Pinnock makes the inheritance the play's central material — refusing both the exoticising lens of colonial fascination and the dismissive lens of rationalist contempt.

• Ultimate moral: Cultural inheritance in Leave Taking survives not because the diaspora protects it but because the diaspora transmits it — the closing palm-reading is one practitioner's body-to-body transfer staged as the form the inheritance has had to take to reach the next generation.

T5 — Coming-of-age and change

Question

How does Pinnock present ideas about coming of age in Leave Taking?

Write about:

- how Pinnock presents change in young characters

- how Pinnock uses coming-of-age to convey her ideas.

[30 marks + 4 AO4 = 34 marks]

Frame. Coming-of-age in Leave Taking is the doubled arc of two second-generation daughters — Del's body-to-body reception arc and Viv's formal-educational arc — and the play's argument that the diasporic second generation has had to invent its own coming-of-age inside a host country that refused to make room for it. The plan tracks three stages — Scene 2 the two daughters' initial registers installed in Enid's living room (Del's sound-system speech versus Viv's Brooke recitation), Scene 5 the divergence under household pressure (Del pregnant at Mai's bedsit; Viv's missed exam) and the protective "Go back to school and sit that exam", Scene 8 the converged coming-of-age in two complementary forms.

Technique gloss:

• Coming-of-age / Bildungsroman — the protagonist moves from innocence through experience to maturity; here doubled across two daughters.

• Foil-pairing — Del's body-to-body arc set against Viv's formal-educational arc.

• Sound-system motif — Del's "The bass is mad… like one big pulsing heart" installing Black British youth culture as the second-generation register.

• Intertextual quotation — Viv's recital of Rupert Brooke's "A dust whom England bore, shaped, made aware" (Scene 2) as the canon-self the curriculum has built.

• Off-stage event — Viv's walked-out exam (Scene 5) as the institutional refusal her arc has to absorb.

• Structural convergence — Scene 8's closing image as the two daughters' coming-of-age completed in two registers.

• Hook (historical fact): Winsome Pinnock, born 1961 in Islington to Jamaican parents, wrote Leave Taking in 1987 alongside Caryl Phillips's The Final Passage (1985) and Joan Riley's The Unbelonging (1985) — three Caribbean-diaspora writers whose mid-1980s output established the diasporic Bildungsroman as a recognisable Black British literary form, and her two on-stage daughters across eight scenes are its earliest dramatic embodiment.

• Building sentence (historical context): Trained at the Royal Court Young Writers' Group, Pinnock anchors her doubled coming-of-age inside the 1971 Caribbean Education in Britain Working Party finding that Caribbean-British students were placed in "educationally subnormal" schools at four times the white-peer rate, the same school-system pressure whose 1981 Brixton uprising and New Cross fire backdrop named her generation's conditions publicly.

• Thesis: Across Scene 2 in which Del's sound-system speech ("The bass is mad. You wanna see it pounding the walls, like one big pulsing heart") and Viv's recitation of Brooke's "A dust whom England bore" install the two daughters' contrasting first registers, Scene 5 in which Del pregnant at Mai's bedsit ("Go back to school and sit that exam") and Viv's walked-out English Literature exam stage the divergence under household pressure, and Scene 8 in which the handwritten notebook and the "Black Studies" announcement converge the two daughters' arcs on the same horizon, Pinnock dramatises coming-of-age as the doubled arc through which the diasporic second generation has had to invent the maturity the host country had not made room for.

Scene focus: Enid's North London living room; Brod is being teased about the pastor; Enid prompts Viv to recite Brooke; Del returns having stayed at the sound system and lost her café job; the row escalates through Del's "He talks to me as if I can't speak English" to the hospital-party speech.

• Topic sentence: Pinnock first installs the two daughters' coming-of-age arcs as two contrasting registers inside the same living room — the elder daughter speaking the Black British sound-system register, the younger reciting the canon-curriculum register the host country has built for her.

• Technique + evidence: Pinnock deploys foil-pairing inside the sound-system motif and the canon-recitation — Del's "All I did last night was dance. What's wrong with that? I like dancing. I been following that sound system for years. The bass is mad. You wanna see it pounding the walls, like one big pulsing heart. When that bass gets inside you and flings you round the room you can't do nothing to stop it" set against Viv's (stands, recites) "A dust whom England bore, shaped, made aware" — combining the sound-system motif (Black British youth culture installed as the daughter's actual belonging, the pulsing bass converting the room into the body) with the canon-recitation (Brooke's First-World-War nationalism rehearsed by the studious daughter for Enid's pastor) inside the same scene.

• Close analysis: The simile "like one big pulsing heart" converts the room into a body the elder daughter inhabits without translation, while Brooke's "A dust whom England bore" installs the younger daughter inside a canon that has shaped her self-vocabulary; the two coming-of-age arcs are operating in two registers before either has been named.

• Effect on audience 1: The audience feels the two arcs installed before either has developed — Del's body-to-body register (the dance, the bass, the manager's racism Del has just escaped: "He talks to me as if I can't speak English") and Viv's mind-to-canon register (the recital, the pastor, the family's exam-bet) — both already operating inside the same room.

• Effect on audience 2: The audience recognises that coming-of-age in this play is doubled rather than single — Del's anger and Viv's compliance are two different responses to identical household pressure, and the play's argument will be that both arcs are necessary for either to mature.

• Author's purpose: Pinnock argues here that the diasporic Bildungsroman has had to be doubled in 1980s Britain — that no single coming-of-age arc could carry the second generation's response to the host country's pressure, and that the two routes must converge for either to reach maturity.

• Connective: Scene 5 will strip both arcs to their divergent paths under crisis — Del pregnant at Mai's bedsit, Viv walking out of the very exam Brooke's recitation had prepared her for.

Scene focus: Mai's bedsit; Del visibly pregnant has moved in; Viv visits with Enid's saved university money having walked out of her English Literature exam; Del refuses the money and pushes Viv back to the exam.

• Topic sentence: Where Scene 2 had installed the two arcs as contrasting registers, Scene 5 stages their divergence under household pressure — Del pregnant and ousted from her biological mother's house, Viv missing the exam that was supposed to be the family's bet, the second generation's coming-of-age forced into a costly fork.

• Technique + evidence: Pinnock deploys protective imperative inside diagnostic confession — Del's "Go back to school and sit that exam" set against Viv's "Oh, I knew all the answers. Pat me on the head and they all come tumbling out, say exactly what the examiners want to hear. But no matter how hard I search for myself in them books, I'm never there… I need another language to express myself" — combining the elder daughter's protective imperative (Del refusing to let her own derailment derail Viv) with the younger daughter's diagnostic confession (the canon Viv aced has produced her invisibility) inside the same exchange.

• Close analysis: The imperative "Go back" converts Del's care into structural pressure — the elder daughter refusing to permit her younger sister to inherit her path — while "I'm never there" converts Viv's compliance into resistance, the model-student daughter naming the absence the curriculum had built into her self-vocabulary.

• Effect on audience 1: The audience feels the divergence as the cost of the household pressure — Del's body-to-body arc taking her out of the biological mother's house, Viv's formal-educational arc forced to absorb the missed exam, both arcs now under visible strain.

• Effect on audience 2: The audience recognises that the two routes have not been free choices — the household pressure has imposed the divergence, and the second generation's coming-of-age has had to operate inside conditions neither daughter chose; Mai's earlier "black ants" reading of Del registers the same school-system that had produced Del's dyslexia mis-diagnosis as the school Viv is now refusing.

• Author's purpose: Pinnock argues here that the diasporic coming-of-age has had to absorb costs no British coming-of-age has had to carry — Del's pregnancy, Viv's missed exam, both arcs forced into divergence by the conditions the host country and the household together produced.

• Connective: Mai's Scene 7 physiognomic recognition of Del ("wide and full between the eyes"; "And now I see it plain plain") and Scene 8's converged image will gather the divergence back into a single horizon both arcs can finally reach.

Scene focus: Mai's cleaned bedsit; Del receives the handwritten notebook ("I don't have a daughter to pass them on to"); Enid reports Viv's university choice ("Black Studies. You ever hear of such a thing?"); the two arcs converge on the same horizon.

• Topic sentence: Where Scene 5 had stripped the two arcs to divergent paths, Scene 8 stages their convergence — Del's body-to-body arc completing in the cleaned bedsit with the handwritten notebook and the closing palm-reading, Viv's formal-educational arc completing in the Black Studies announcement, the second generation's doubled coming-of-age reaching its earned form.

• Technique + evidence: Pinnock deploys structural convergence inside doubled inheritance-naming — Enid's "Black Studies. You ever hear of such a thing?" set alongside Mai's "You passed with flying colours. You're a natural" and the stage direction in which Mai hands Del the notebook — combining the structural convergence (the academic and the body-to-body forms of the inheritance arriving as complementary rather than competing) with the doubled inheritance-naming (Viv's discipline announced and Del's apprenticeship confirmed inside the same closing scene).

• Close analysis: The naming of "Black Studies" converts Viv's assimilation route into reclamation, the canon that had refused her replaced by a discipline she has chosen for herself; the handwritten notebook registers Del's body-to-body alternative, the inheritance arriving inside the room Del has cleaned — the second generation's coming-of-age arriving in two registers because no single register could carry it.

• Effect on audience 1: The audience feels the two arcs completing as the play's most quietly hopeful image — the second generation's doubled coming-of-age finally reaching the inheritance neither route alone could have carried.

• Effect on audience 2: The audience recognises the doubled coming-of-age as the play's structural answer — the diasporic Bildungsroman requires both daughters' arcs to converge before either can be said to have reached maturity; Enid's bewildered "You ever hear of such a thing?" registers that the daughters have invented routes the Windrush generation had no vocabulary to anticipate.

• Author's purpose: Pinnock argues here that the diasporic second generation cannot reach maturity through any single arc, and that the convergence of the two arcs is the form the Bildungsroman has had to take to survive in 1980s Britain.

• Connective: The first British academic Centre for the Study of Race and Ethnicity emerged in the 1980s; the Black British literary and cultural studies movement Pinnock joined gave Caribbean-British students the formal-educational route Viv announces, and the body-to-body cultural inheritance Mai transfers to Del was the alternative the formal canon had refused — both routes necessary for the coming-of-age the host country had not made room for.

• Restated thesis: In conclusion, Pinnock dramatises coming-of-age as the doubled arc of two second-generation daughters — Scene 2 contrasting first registers (Del's sound-system speech, Viv's Brooke recitation), Scene 5 divergence under household pressure (Del's pregnancy, Viv's walked-out exam), Scene 8 converged coming-of-age in two complementary forms (Mai's notebook, Viv's Black Studies).

• Controlling concept: What Scene 2 installed as two contrasting registers, Scene 5 stripped to divergent paths under crisis, Scene 8 confirmed as converged coming-of-age — three stages of the same diasporic Bildungsroman, the second generation reaching maturity only when both arcs reach the inheritance.

• Author's purpose: Writing inside the 1987 cultural moment in which the diasporic Bildungsroman was being established as a Black British literary form, Pinnock argues that coming-of-age in the diaspora has had to be doubled — and that the convergence is the form the genre had to take.

• Ultimate moral: Coming-of-age in Leave Taking is the doubled arc through which the diasporic second generation reaches the inheritance the host country had taught them to refuse — and the play's closing image is that doubled arc converged in one quiet scene.

T6 — Race and racism in 1980s Britain

Question

How does Pinnock present ideas about race and racism in Leave Taking?

Write about:

- how Pinnock presents race and racism at different points in the play

- how Pinnock uses race and racism to convey her ideas.

[30 marks + 4 AO4 = 34 marks]

Frame. Race and racism in Leave Taking are the structural condition the household has been built inside — the casual workplace abuse Del is subjected to ("He talks to me as if I can't speak English"), the breakage of Caribbean-British men by racist workplace abuse, racist graffiti and Home-Office citizenship-stripping (Gullyman, Viv's father, Brod), and the legislative chain that progressively eroded the Windrush generation's right to remain. Pinnock keeps the British institutions off-stage — the racist Smithfield workplace, the police station, the Home Office — and stages instead the private household their pressure produces. The plan tracks three stages — Scene 2 Gullyman's racist "'wogs out'" graffiti and Brod's Home Office threat, Scene 7 the truth about Viv's father broken by Smithfield's "'Show us yer tail, yer black monkey'", Scene 8 Viv's choice of Black Studies and the female line carrying what the racist host country could not break.

Technique gloss:

• Off-stage character — Gullyman, Viv's father, the racist Smithfield colleagues, the Home Office officer; the institutions of British racism kept off-stage so the household's interior bears their weight.

• Antithesis (close analysis) — Brod's "smiling boy" converting into the "hard man" under racist workplace pressure.

• Embedded racist quotation — the "'wogs out'" graffiti and the "'Show us yer tail, yer black monkey'" workplace slur quoted verbatim inside Brod's narration.

• Doubled male-female outcome — racist host country breaking the male Windrush line (Gullyman, Viv's father, Brod) while the female line is forced to carry the inheritance.

• Structural reclamation — Viv's Scene 8 announcement of Black Studies as the academic response to a canon that had refused her inheritance.

• Recurring object — Mai's handwritten book of Obeah practice as the inherited form the racist host country had criminalised but could not erase.

• Hook (historical fact): Winsome Pinnock, born 1961 in Islington to Jamaican parents, wrote Leave Taking in 1987 in the wake of the 1981 New Cross fire that killed thirteen young Black people in south-east London on 18 January and the Brixton uprising of 10–12 April that followed Operation Swamp — the same cluster of events that had forced 1980s British racism into public visibility and the same conditions her household interior absorbs.

• Building sentence (historical context): Trained at the Royal Court Young Writers' Group, Pinnock anchors the racism question inside the legislative chain of the 1962 Commonwealth Immigrants Act, the 1971 Immigration Act, and the 1981 British Nationality Act — restrictions that progressively voided Windrush citizenship — and inside the 1965 / 1968 / 1976 Race Relations Acts that nominally outlawed discrimination her household still bears.

• Thesis: Across Scene 2 in which Brod's Gullyman story ("Gullyman heart brock, him mind crack" after "'wogs out'") and the Home Office threat to Brod's "All my life I think of meself as a British subject" identity together register what 1980s Britain has cost the male Windrush figure, Scene 7 in which Brod's disclosure that "you father change from a smiling boy into a hard man" after Smithfield meat-market colleagues' "'Show us yer tail, yer black monkey'" names the racist conditions that broke the male Windrush marriage from outside, and Scene 8 in which Viv's announcement of Black Studies and Del's reception of Mai's handwritten book together stage the female line claiming what the racist host country could not break, Pinnock presents race and racism as the structural condition inside which the diasporic family has been forced to carry its inheritance.

Scene focus: Enid's North London living room; the pastor has visited; Brod is dressed scruffily; Brod tells Gullyman's story of buying a car and house, the "'wogs out'" graffiti, and his own Home Office letter threatening his right to remain.

• Topic sentence: In the play's second scene, Pinnock establishes 1980s British racism not through any on-stage institution but through its consequences in the body of the male Windrush visitor — Gullyman's off-stage breakdown after racist graffiti, and the Home Office threat to Brod's right to remain, together installing racism as the household's structural condition.

• Technique + evidence: Pinnock deploys the off-stage character function inside Brod's Scene 2 retelling — Brod's "One mornin Gullyman wake up to find him lovely car covered in shit an a message on him door read 'wogs out'. Gullyman heart brock, him mind crack, and now he can hardly talk broken English" set alongside Brod's own "Then them send me letter say if me don't get me nationality paper in order they going kick me outta the country. You mother, same thing" — combining off-stage character function (Gullyman as the extreme outcome the household never sees, the Home Office officer as the institution producing the threat) with embedded quotation of the racist graffiti ("'wogs out'" installed verbatim, the play refusing to soften the slur) inside the same Scene 2 exchange.

• Close analysis: The patois compound "heart brock, him mind crack" enacts the breakage as two coordinated verbs without the conjunction "and", the syntax mirroring the simultaneous breaking of the man's inner and outer life; the racist graffiti operates as the household's evidence that respectability (a car, a house, the corrected accent) has not bought safety inside 1980s Britain.

• Effect on audience 1: Thus the audience hears Brod's voice carrying the off-stage institutions of British racism into the household — the anonymous neighbour who left the graffiti, the Home Office that is now threatening Brod, the policing pressure that bracketed the play's 1980s setting.

• Effect on audience 2: Indeed, the audience recognises that Pinnock declines to stage the institutions themselves — the racist neighbour, the Home Office officer, the police — and registers that the off-stage staging is itself the indictment, the household's interior bearing the weight British institutions refuse to.

• Author's purpose: Pinnock may be using the Scene 2 off-stage staging to argue that 1980s British racism produced its damage at the level of the household rather than at the level of the institutions that produced it, and that the diasporic family is the place where the cumulative cost of British racism has had to be absorbed.

• Context (AO3): The 1981 New Cross fire, the 1981 Brixton uprising, and the legislative chain of the 1962 Commonwealth Immigrants Act, the 1971 Immigration Act, and the 1981 British Nationality Act together formed the 1980s Britain inside which Pinnock wrote — and the Home Office threat that surfaces in Scene 2 anticipates by thirty-one years the 2018 Windrush deportation scandal the Guardian's reporting forced into public view.

Scene focus: Mai's bedsit; Brod arrives drunk, complains about being denied rice and peas, and inside the disorder tells Del the buried truth about her father at Smithfield meat market.

• Topic sentence: Where Scene 2 had installed British racism as the off-stage condition, Scene 7 names its precise male outcome — the absent father at the centre of Del's childhood now revealed as a man broken not by his own failure but by the racist workplace and racist household-economy the host country imposed on him.

• Technique + evidence: Pinnock deploys antithesis inside the trickster function — Brod's Scene 7 disclosure "I never see a man eyes look so empty. At first him an' you mother share everything … It wasn't till we get job a Smithfield meat market that him start to change. Hear the other men, yah, 'Show us yer tail, yer black monkey.' Keep our mouth shut, keep our anger inside. But you father change from a smiling boy into a hard man" — combining antithesis (the "smiling boy" converting into the "hard man", the racist workplace operating as the agent of the change) with the trickster function (the play's hardest disclosure entrusted to its drunkest character, the diagnosis travelling inside the disorder of Brod's drunken visit) inside the same exchange.

• Close analysis: The repeated imperative "Keep our mouth shut, keep our anger inside" registers the racist workplace as a daily compression the male Windrush body was forced to absorb without release, and the antithetical pairing "smiling boy" into "hard man" converts the change into something Britain produced rather than something the father chose; the "empty" eyes diagnose the cumulative cost as interior rather than circumstantial.

• Effect on audience 1: Thus the audience feels the disclosure as a release of eighteen years of Del's misreading — the absent father at the centre of her childhood now explained as a man broken by Britain rather than as a man Enid drove away.

• Effect on audience 2: Indeed, the audience recognises Pinnock's diagnostic precision — "no one care what he want to do to a black woman" names the racist conditions under which the workplace's compression was redirected into the household, the diagnosis returned to the host country's indifference rather than to any individual cruelty.

• Author's purpose: Pinnock may be using Brod's disclosure to argue that 1980s British racism produced outcomes the diasporic family had no language for — the marriages it broke from outside, the men it hollowed out — and that the disclosure of those outcomes is the precondition for the second generation's understanding of the household it has been raised inside.

• Context (AO3): Smithfield meat market had a long-documented record of racist workplace abuse against Caribbean-British workers in the 1960s–70s; housing discrimination through the "colour bar" in private rentals and policing pressure that produced "sus" law arrests under the 1824 Vagrancy Act compounded the workplace racism, and the cumulative weight produced exactly the "smiling boy"-into-"hard man" outcome Brod names as Viv's father's diagnosis.

Scene focus: Mai's cleaned bedsit; Del receives the handwritten book; Viv announces she has chosen to study Black Studies at university; the closing palm-reading stages the female line claiming the inheritance the racist host country had tried to erase.

• Topic sentence: Where Scene 7 had named the male outcome of British racism, Scene 8 stages the female response — Viv's academic reclamation of Black Studies converging with Del's body-to-body reception of Mai's handwritten book, the female line claiming what the racist host country had broken the male line too far to carry.

• Technique + evidence: Pinnock deploys structural reclamation inside the cyclical structure — Viv's Scene 8 announcement of Black Studies at university framed alongside Del's receipt of the handwritten book in the same scene — combining structural reclamation (Viv's choice of Black Studies as the academic answer to a canon Pinnock herself has described as one in which she "could not find a trace of" her experience, the inheritance entering the academy through the daughter's choice rather than through the institution's permission) with cyclical structure (the same Mai's bedsit that opened the play "messy" now cleaned by Del, the inheritance returning to both daughters in two complementary forms) inside the same closing image.

• Close analysis: The naming of "Black Studies" in Viv's announcement is the formal-educational reclamation of the inheritance the racist canon had refused, while the handwritten book registers the body-to-body alternative — the same cultural inheritance the host country had criminalised under colonial Obeah law arriving in Del's hands inside a south-London bedsit.

• Effect on audience 1: Thus the audience feels the convergence as the play's quietly hopeful answer to the racist conditions Scene 2 and Scene 7 named — the female line claiming both the academic and the body-to-body forms of the inheritance the host country had refused.

• Effect on audience 2: Indeed, the audience recognises that Pinnock's response to British racism is structural rather than declarative — the play does not stage the racist institutions in order to denounce them; it stages the female line claiming what those institutions had tried to break.

• Author's purpose: Pinnock may be using Viv's Scene 8 announcement and Del's reception together to argue that the diasporic answer to British racism is the female line claiming the inheritance the host country had refused, and that the claiming happens both in the academy (Viv's Black Studies) and in the bedsit (Del's handwritten book).

• Context (AO3): The first British academic Centre for the Study of Race and Ethnicity emerged in the 1980s and the Black British literary movement Pinnock joined gave Caribbean-British students the formal-educational route Viv announces, while Stuart Hall's Cultural Identity and Diaspora (1990) named diasporic identity as "a matter of 'becoming' as well as of 'being'" — exactly the becoming Pinnock stages as the female line's reclamation.

• Restated thesis: In conclusion, Pinnock dramatises race and racism as the structural condition inside which the diasporic family has had to carry its inheritance — Scene 2 Gullyman's abuse and Brod's Home Office threat installing the off-stage institutions, Scene 7 Brod's disclosure naming the male outcome, Scene 8 Viv's Black Studies and Del's handwritten book staging the female line's reclamation.

• Controlling concept: What Scene 2 installed as the off-stage British institutions through "'wogs out'" and the Home Office letter, Scene 7 named as the male outcome of the "smiling boy" converting into the "hard man" under Smithfield's "'Show us yer tail, yer black monkey'", Scene 8 answered through the female line's claiming of both academy and inheritance — three stages of the same diagnosis, racism producing the conditions inside which the diasporic family has had to invent its own response.

• Author's purpose: Writing inside the 1987 cultural moment that the New Cross fire and the Brixton uprising had forced into public visibility, Pinnock argues that 1980s British racism produced damage at the level of the household, and that the diasporic answer has had to be the female line's structural reclamation of what the racist host country tried to erase.

• Ultimate moral: Race and racism in Leave Taking are not incidents the play denounces but the structural condition inside which the female line has had to claim the inheritance the racist host country could not break — and the closing palm-reading is that claiming staged as one quiet physical action.

T7 — Memory, Windrush legacy and leave-taking

Question

How does Pinnock present ideas about leaving and migration in Leave Taking?

Write about:

- how Pinnock presents leaving and migration at different points in the play

- how Pinnock uses leaving and migration to convey her ideas.

[30 marks + 4 AO4 = 34 marks]

Frame. Memory and leave-taking are the title's double action — the leaving Enid did in 1948–1971 when she travelled from Jamaica to Britain, and the leave-taking the play stages of an entire generation as Enid's mother dies off-stage in Jamaica without Enid present. Pinnock holds these two leavings against each other across the eight scenes — what was left, what was lost, what cannot be returned to, and what the female line carries forward as the only inheritance the migration could not break. The plan tracks three stages — Scene 3 the telephone announcing the grandmother's death in Jamaica, Scene 4 grieving asyndetic list of the homeland that cannot be returned to, Scene 8 the inheritance received as the leave-taking's only possible repair.

Technique gloss:

• Title-anchored controlling concept — Leave Taking as both the leaving Enid performed in the Windrush years and the parting from the homeland-mother the play stages.

• Off-stage event — the grandmother's death in Jamaica delivered by telephone, the homeland kept off-stage so the household's interior bears its weight.

• Asyndetic list (close analysis) — Enid's "Old time woman could cook. Roast breadfruit. Callaloo".

• Recurring object — Mai's handwritten book as the only inheritance the leave-taking did not erase.

• Cyclical structure — Mai's bedsit returned to in Scene 8 as the room of inheritance received.

• Doubled audience address — the play addressed simultaneously to the Windrush generation that left and to the second generation that was never asked.

• Hook (historical fact): Winsome Pinnock, daughter of Jamaican parents who had themselves arrived in the Windrush years after HMT Empire Windrush docked at Tilbury on 22 June 1948 with over a thousand Caribbean passengers, wrote Leave Taking in 1987 at the moment her second generation was beginning to count the cost of those leavings — the title naming both Enid's 1948–1971 leave-taking from Jamaica and the parting from the homeland-mother the play stages.

• Building sentence (historical context): Pinnock anchors her memory question inside the legislative chain (1948 BNA opening the route, 1962 Commonwealth Immigrants Act introducing employment vouchers, 1971 Immigration Act making "patriality" the test for unrestricted entry, 1981 BNA stripping the original rights) whose furthest extreme would produce the 2018 Windrush deportation scandal that proved her 1987 silences prescient.

• Thesis: Across Scene 3 in which the telephone delivers the off-stage announcement of Enid's mother's death in Jamaica and the leave-taking is named as a permanent loss, Scene 4 in which Enid's grieving asyndetic list "Old time woman could cook. Roast breadfruit. Callaloo" assembles the homeland in fragments because the homeland cannot now be returned to whole, and Scene 8 in which Del's reception of Mai's handwritten book and her palm-reading of Enid stage the only inheritance the migration could not erase, Pinnock dramatises memory and leave-taking as the controlling concept the play's title named — what the female line has had to carry forward because the homeland the older generation left has become unreachable.

Scene focus: Enid's living room; the telephone rings; the off-stage news of Enid's mother's death in Jamaica enters the household; the leave-taking the title named is registered as the permanent absence of return.

• Topic sentence: In the play's third scene, Pinnock installs the leave-taking as the household's structural condition — the off-stage homeland delivering, by telephone, the death the older generation has been unable to travel back to attend, the legislative chain made personal in a single ringing instrument.

• Technique + evidence: Pinnock deploys the off-stage event inside Enid's refusing question — Enid's "Then who kill her then? Me?" on the Scene 3 telephone, the homeland kept off-stage and the death of her mother in Jamaica arriving as the news Brod has to confirm twice ("Mooma dead.") — combining off-stage event (the homeland that the play declines to stage, the absence registering the migration's permanent cost) with rhetorical self-incrimination (the interrogative "Me?" converting eighteen years of unanswered absence into the daughter's first reflex of guilt, the leave-taking's interest finally due) inside the same scene.

• Close analysis: The telephone is the play's quietest argument about leave-taking — the homeland reduced to a voice through a wire, the mother's death delivered as information rather than as presence, the leaving Enid performed in the Windrush years now returning to her as the news she cannot answer in person; her immediate redirect to "Money send money... Send money for Mooma funeral" converts grief into the only currency the leave-taking still permits.

• Effect on audience 1: Thus the audience feels the off-stage homeland as the condition of Enid's grief — Jamaica acoustically present in the ringing instrument and physically unreachable across the legislative chain, the leave-taking the title named registered as the permanent absence the telephone confirms.

• Effect on audience 2: Indeed, the audience recognises that the play's central loss is delivered by an instrument rather than by a return — that Enid's leave-taking from Jamaica in the Windrush years has cost her the right to attend her own mother's death, and that the cost is the form of the legislative chain made personal.

• Author's purpose: Pinnock may be using the Scene 3 telephone to argue that the leave-taking the Windrush generation performed was permanent in ways the original arrivals could not have anticipated — that the homeland they left has been kept off-stage by the host country's restrictions, and that the cost of leave-taking is paid as the inability to return for the moments that matter most.

• Context (AO3): The 1962 Commonwealth Immigrants Act required Commonwealth citizens to obtain employment vouchers, the 1971 Immigration Act effectively closed the Windrush route by making "patriality" the test for unrestricted entry, and by the 1980s return travel for the Windrush generation had become legally and economically harder than it had been for the original arrivals — the legislative chain producing exactly the off-stage homeland Pinnock stages in Scene 3.

Scene focus: Enid grieving on the sofa with Viv after the telephone has carried the news of her mother's death in Jamaica; the saved university money disclosed; the homeland assembled in fragments of remembered food.

• Topic sentence: Where Scene 3 had installed the leave-taking as the off-stage absence of return, Scene 4 reveals what memory in the diaspora has been forced to look like — the homeland assembled in fragments of remembered food because the homeland cannot now be returned to whole, the inheritance redirected from Jamaica to a saved university place in London because the original homeland is no longer available to receive it.

• Technique + evidence: Pinnock deploys an asyndetic list inside the food-motif — Enid's "Old time woman could cook. Roast breadfruit. Callaloo" (Scene 4) framed by the disclosure that she has saved a large sum of money for Viv's university — combining asyndeton (the three Jamaican dishes set down without conjunctions, each item left alone like an unfinished sentence) with food-motif (each dish a material trace of the Jamaica the host country has not made room for, callaloo and breadfruit standing for the cultural memory the leave-taking has had to carry across in fragments) inside the same nostalgic eruption.

• Close analysis: The asyndetic refusal to join the items with "and" leaves each Jamaican dish standing alone like an unfinished sentence, and the unfinished syntax mirrors the unfinished mourning — a daughter who could not travel back for her own mother's funeral can only assemble the homeland in fragments of remembered food, the leave-taking having broken even the grammar of the homeland's articulation.

• Effect on audience 1: Thus the audience feels memory in the diaspora as something forced to operate in fragments — the homeland too far away to be assembled whole, the food-motif standing in for the cultural inheritance the leave-taking has reduced to what the older generation can carry in the body and on the tongue.

• Effect on audience 2: Indeed, the audience recognises the saved university money as the diasporic redirection of the homeland inheritance Enid herself cannot bring back — the leave-taking having converted what the older generation hoped to receive into what the older generation hopes to give, the inheritance redirected from Jamaica to London because Jamaica cannot now be returned to receive it.

• Author's purpose: Pinnock may be using the Scene 4 grieving to argue that memory in the diaspora is the form the leave-taking has imposed on the older generation — that the homeland survives as fragments because the legislative chain has refused the older generation the right to assemble it whole, and that the redirection of the inheritance from breadfruit to a university place is the only form the host country leaves available.

• Context (AO3): Stuart Hall, in Cultural Identity and Diaspora (1990), described diasporic identity as constituted "by recognising" and "continuously producing and reproducing" itself anew through "transformation and difference" — and Enid's Scene 4 fragments are exactly that diasporic memory at work, the homeland reproduced through what the leave-taking has made portable.

Scene focus: Mai's cleaned bedsit; Mai passes the handwritten book to Del; the closing palm-reading of Enid stages the inheritance the leave-taking could not erase.

• Topic sentence: Where Scene 4 had revealed memory as the homeland reduced to fragments, Scene 8 stages the leave-taking's only possible repair — the inheritance the migration could not break passing through the female line in the form Mai has been carrying, the handwritten book and the palm-reading standing for what survived because the female line refused to let it be lost.

• Technique + evidence: Pinnock deploys the recurring object inside the cyclical structure — Mai's "handwritten book" of Obeah practice transferred to Del in Scene 8, the closing palm-reading staging Enid as Del's first client, the same Mai's bedsit that opened the play "messy" now cleaned by Del — combining recurring object (the book as the leave-taking's only inheritance survived, body-to-body transmission carrying what enslavement, colonial criminalisation, and the Windrush leave-taking had each tried to erase) with cyclical structure (the unchanged room measuring the changed daughter, the inheritance the leave-taking attempted to leave behind returning in the female line that carried it forward) inside the same closing image.

• Close analysis: The handwritten quality of the book is the form the leave-taking permitted — the inheritance had to be carried in something portable, in something a single woman could take across the Atlantic in her hand, and the same handwriting now reaches Del in a cleaned south-London bedsit as the only form the migration could not erase.

• Effect on audience 1: Thus the audience feels the closing palm-reading as the leave-taking's quietest answer — the Jamaica that the legislative chain has kept off-stage now arriving as a practice the daughter has trained herself to perform, the leave-taking the title named answered in the only register the migration could not break.

• Effect on audience 2: Indeed, the audience recognises that the play's title contains both leavings — the leaving Enid performed in 1948–1971 and the leave-taking the audience itself is performing as the older generation passes — and that the inheritance the female line carries is the only form of the homeland the leave-taking left available.

• Author's purpose: Pinnock may be making her clearest argument about leave-taking and memory here — that what the diaspora has had to carry forward is not the homeland itself but the practices the homeland trained the female line to keep, and that the closing palm-reading is the leave-taking the title named accepted and answered in the same scene.

• Context (AO3): The 2018 Windrush deportation scandal — exposed by the Guardian's reporting in April 2018 — revealed that hundreds of Windrush-generation British citizens had been wrongfully detained or deported by the Home Office; the play's 2018 Bush Theatre revival opened weeks after the reporting, the leave-taking the title named registering anew as the host country's continuing refusal to honour the generation that left in 1948.

• Restated thesis: In conclusion, Pinnock dramatises memory and leave-taking as the controlling concept the play's title named — Scene 3 telephone announcing the off-stage homeland's death, Scene 4 grieving asyndetic list assembling the homeland in fragments, Scene 8 the inheritance received as the leave-taking's only possible repair.

• Controlling concept: What Scene 3 installed as the off-stage absence of return, Scene 4 named as the homeland reduced to fragments, Scene 8 answered as the inheritance the female line carried forward — three stages of the same leave-taking, the title's double action played out across the household the migration produced.

• Author's purpose: Writing in 1987 inside the cultural emergence of Black British identity, Pinnock argues that the cost of leave-taking is paid not by the generation that left but by the generation that was never asked, and that memory in the diaspora is the form the older generation has had to carry forward because the homeland itself has been kept off-stage by the host country's restrictions.

• Ultimate moral: Memory in the diaspora is the inheritance the female line refused to let the leave-taking erase — and the closing palm-reading is the homeland the migration left behind reassembled as the only practice the host country could not break.

T8 — Religion and spiritual inheritance

Question

How does Pinnock present ideas about religion and spiritual belief in Leave Taking?

Write about:

- how Pinnock presents religion and spiritual belief at different points in the play

- how Pinnock uses religion and spiritual belief to convey her ideas.

[30 marks + 4 AO4 = 34 marks]

Frame. Religion in Leave Taking is doubled — the host country's Christianity (the pastor's Scene 2 visit) set alongside the Caribbean diaspora's Obeah (Mai's practice across Scenes 1, 5–8) — and Pinnock treats both with the same naturalistic seriousness. The plan tracks three stages — Scene 1 weary practice in the messy bedsit, Scene 6 Enid's consultation with Mai, Scene 8 Del's reception of the handwritten book and the palm-reading.

Pedagogical depth to thread. The Obeah / Christian double-register reading (see top of document). Obeah is presented as alternative epistemology, not superstition; recovering Mai is recovering the Caribbean cosmology imperial Britain attempted to erase. Thread into BP1 or BP3.

Technique gloss:

• Naturalism with cultural-inheritance ritual — Mai's Obeah practice held inside naturalistic staging.

• Doubled religious staging — pastor's visit (Scene 2) and Mai's practice given equal naturalistic weight.

• Recurring object — Mai's "handwritten book" of Obeah practice.

• Sound motif — the rooster crowing inside Mai's bedsit.

• Stage direction — Mai's "messy" bedsit (Scene 1) and its cleaned state (Scene 8).

• Anagnorisis — Mai's Scene 7 physiognomic recognition ("wide and full between the eyes"; "frontal lobe big where them meet at the nose root"; "And now I see it plain plain").

• Hook (historical fact): Winsome Pinnock, daughter of Jamaican parents who arrived in the Windrush years, wrote Leave Taking in 1987 inheriting a Caribbean cultural landscape in which Obeah had been criminalised under the Jamaican Obeah Act of 1898 (imprisonment with hard labour for practitioners) — and her doubled religious staging of pastor and Obeah practitioner converts more than a century of colonial elevation of Christianity into a structural argument for cosmological equivalence.

• Building sentence (historical context): Trained at the Royal Court Young Writers' Group inside a North London household in which the older generation had consulted Obeah practitioners as 1980s NHS services pathologised Caribbean cultural beliefs, Pinnock anchors Mai's practice in the centuries of clandestine transmission through which African religious memory survived enslavement, colonial criminalisation and migration.

• Thesis: Across Mai's Scene 1 weary practice in the messy Deptford bedsit in which the inheritance looks precariously alive, Enid's Scene 6 desperate consultation with Mai in which the inheritance is the only therapeutic resource available to a Windrush mother whose suffering British institutions cannot recognise, and Del's Scene 8 reception of Mai's handwritten book and palm-reading of Enid in which the inheritance is finally received, Pinnock dramatises Afro-Caribbean spiritual inheritance as a religion that survives by being passed on rather than by being protected.

Scene focus: Mai's Deptford bedsit; the bedsit is "messy"; Mai is older, weary, drinking stout; she conducts a clairvoyant reading for Enid; a rooster crows inside the flat.

• Topic sentence: In the play's opening scene, Pinnock establishes Afro-Caribbean spiritual inheritance as a living practice — small, weary, but alive — set inside a room whose disorder registers the conditions under which the inheritance has had to survive in 1980s Britain.

• Technique + evidence: Pinnock deploys naturalistic stage direction inside sound motif — the "messy" opening state of Mai's bedsit, with Mai older and drinking stout, and a rooster crowing inside the flat — combining naturalistic stage direction (the bedsit's mess as the body of the practitioner, no exoticism imposed and no folklore added) with sound motif (the rooster crowing inside a south-London bedsit, the rural-Jamaica acoustic geography imported into the room) inside the same opening setting.

• Close analysis: The naturalistic register is the argument — Pinnock declines to stage the practice with heightened lighting, ritualistic music, or supernatural effects, treating the inheritance with the same dignity she gives any other religion in the play, while the rooster sound carries the homeland into the room with an uncanniness that registers the cost of the leave-taking.

• Effect on audience 1: Thus the audience feels the practice as a small working business rather than as a folkloric set-piece — the stout, the worn equipment, the thin client list all registering the religion's daily texture.

• Effect on audience 2: Indeed, the audience recognises that Pinnock is asking them to take the inheritance seriously on its own terms — the same seriousness the play will give the pastor's visit in the next scene.

• Author's purpose: Pinnock may be using the Scene 1 staging to argue that Afro-Caribbean spiritual inheritance deserves the dignity of naturalism rather than the diminishment of folklore — and that the room itself is the indictment of a host country that has not made space for the religion to flourish.

• Context (AO3): Obeah was criminalised under the Jamaican Obeah Act 1898 and remains illegal in Jamaica to this day; the practice survived through clandestine transmission across enslavement, colonial criminalisation, and migration, reaching 1980s Britain in the condition Pinnock stages in Mai's Deptford bedsit.

Scene focus: Mai's bedsit; Enid consults Mai about Del; Enid says "I have to be man and woman, shout so them hear me and when I hear meself I think, why that woman shouting so?"; Mai eventually delivers her imperative "I want you out of here by Wednesday evening" to Del.

• Topic sentence: Where Scene 1 had installed the practice as living inheritance, Scene 6 reveals its central function in 1980s Britain — Mai's bedsit as the only therapeutic space available to a Windrush mother whose suffering the host country's institutions cannot recognise.

• Technique + evidence: Pinnock deploys doubled religious staging inside rhetorical self-questioning — Enid's Scene 6 visit to Mai framed by the earlier Scene 2 pastor's visit, both encounters staged with the same naturalistic respect, and Enid's confession "I have to be man and woman, shout so them hear me and when I hear meself I think, why that woman shouting so?" — combining doubled religious staging (Christianity and Obeah given equal weight as religions consulted in family crisis, neither elevated above the other) with rhetorical self-questioning (Enid's compound subject "man and woman" split apart by the third-person self-reference "that woman", the speaker no longer able to recognise the voice doing the shouting) inside the same scene.

• Close analysis: The compound noun-phrase "man and woman" registers the female-line burden the migration produced — a Windrush woman holding the two gendered roles the male Windrush figures have been broken too far to carry — while the splitting of the self into "meself" and "that woman" enacts the cost of that holding inside the same sentence, and the disclosure happens inside Mai's room because no British institution has made room for it.

• Effect on audience 1: Thus the audience feels Enid's consultation as the play's quietest indictment — a Windrush mother whose suffering has no other room reaches for the religion the host country criminalised.

• Effect on audience 2: Indeed, the audience recognises that Mai's practice is providing what no other character or institution in the play can — a space where the older generation's experience is recognised as legitimate rather than pathologised.

• Author's purpose: Pinnock may be using Enid's Scene 6 consultation to argue that Afro-Caribbean spiritual inheritance has functioned in 1980s Britain as therapeutic resource as much as religious practice — providing the holding space the host country had refused to provide.

• Context (AO3): 1980s NHS services were culturally ill-equipped for Caribbean-British patients — patois-speaking patients were assessed without interpreters, cultural beliefs were frequently pathologised — and the institutional void produced exactly the conditions inside which Mai's practice became the only available form of care for Windrush women.

Scene focus: Mai's cleaned bedsit; Mai passes the "handwritten book" of Obeah practice to Del; Del reads Enid's palm.

• Topic sentence: Where Scene 6 had revealed the practice's therapeutic function, Scene 8 stages its full transmission — the inheritance no longer carried by Mai alone but received by the second generation, the religion surviving by being passed on rather than by being protected.

• Technique + evidence: Pinnock deploys the recurring object inside the cyclical structure — Mai's "handwritten book" of Obeah practice transferred to Del in the cleaned bedsit, the closing palm-reading staging Enid as Del's first client — combining recurring object (the book as cultural memory in material form, body-to-body transmission of what enslavement, colonial criminalisation, and migration had tried to erase) with cyclical structure (the same bedsit that opened the play "messy" now cleaned and ready to be the room of the next practitioner) inside the same closing image.

• Close analysis: The handwritten quality of the book is the play's argument that the religion travels by hand-to-hand transmission rather than by print culture — the same hand-to-hand transmission that carried the practice through the colonial criminalisation and across the Atlantic, and that now reaches Del in a cleaned south-London bedsit.

• Effect on audience 1: Thus the audience feels the closing palm-reading as the religion arriving in its next register — the inheritance no longer something Mai alone holds but something the second generation has begun to perform.

• Effect on audience 2: Indeed, the audience recognises that the religion's survival has depended not on protection but on transmission — and that Mai's quiet withdrawal at the close is the dignified form of trust the moment of transmission requires.

• Author's purpose: Pinnock may be making her clearest argument about Afro-Caribbean spiritual inheritance here — that it is a religion which survives by being received and passed on, and that its 1980s survival is the work of older practitioners willing to recognise the next generation and trust the transmission.

• Context (AO3): Paul Gilroy, in The Black Atlantic (1993), described the Black-Atlantic identity as the inheritance of African religious practice carried through the Middle Passage and the diaspora; Mai's handwritten book is one such inheritance staged in 1987 in dramatic miniature.

• Restated thesis: In conclusion, Pinnock dramatises Afro-Caribbean spiritual inheritance as a religion that survives by being passed on — Scene 1 weary practice in the messy bedsit, Scene 6 desperate consultation as the only therapeutic resource available, Scene 8 transmission of the handwritten book and the closing palm-reading.

• Controlling concept: What Scene 1 installed as living but precarious inheritance, Scene 6 named as the therapeutic resource the host country had refused to provide, Scene 8 confirmed as completed transmission — three stages of the same religion, treated with the same naturalistic respect Pinnock gives the host country's Christianity.

• Author's purpose: Writing inside a 1987 cultural moment that had inherited the colonial criminalisation of Afro-Caribbean spiritual practice, Pinnock makes Mai's Obeah the dignified religion the play centres — refusing both the exoticising lens of colonial fascination and the dismissive lens of rationalist contempt.

• Ultimate moral: A religion survives not because it is protected but because it is recognised, received, and passed on — and Mai's quiet transmission of the handwritten book to Del is the religion's survival staged as one small physical action.

T9 — Family, household and the secrets the host country imposed

Question

How does Pinnock present the family in Leave Taking?

Write about:

- how Pinnock presents the family at different points in the play

- how Pinnock uses the family to convey her ideas.

[30 marks + 4 AO4 = 34 marks]

Frame. The family in Leave Taking is a diasporic household built around silences — the absent father at the centre, Enid's eighteen-year silence about her own marriage and the homeland-mother in Jamaica, the secrets the host country has trained the household to keep — and Pinnock structures the play as the slow release of those silences. The plan tracks three stages — Scene 1 the household with its silences installed as architecture, Scene 7 Brod's drunken disclosure breaking the first silence, Scene 8 Enid's broken-speech confession releasing the last.

Technique gloss:

• Suppressed exposition — the silences installed in Scene 1, released across Scenes 7–8.

• Off-stage character — Viv's father, Enid's mother in Jamaica, Gullyman as the household's silenced background.

• Trickster of disclosure — Brod as the character who delivers the play's hardest truth.

• Broken speech — Enid's Scene 8 confession.

• Stage business — the kicked wall, the cleaning of the bedsit as the household's wordless beats.

• Cultural-inheritance ritual — the palm-reading as the room inside which family truths become speakable.

• Hook (historical fact): Winsome Pinnock, daughter of Jamaican parents who arrived in the Windrush years, wrote Leave Taking in 1987 inside a Britain whose Home Office had already begun the documentary erosion (later compounded in 2010 by the deliberate destruction of Windrush landing cards) that the Guardian's April 2018 reporting would expose as the Windrush deportation scandal — making her 1987 household silences read thirty-one years later as a structural prophecy.

• Building sentence (historical context): Trained at the Royal Court Young Writers' Group, Pinnock anchors her family around an absence — Del and Viv's father, broken by Britain — inside the same legislative chain (1962 CIA / 1971 IA / 1981 BNA) and SUS-laws / 1981 Brixton uprising decade whose cumulative pressure had broken the male Windrush figures around her own North London childhood.

• Thesis: Across Scene 1 in which the household's silences are installed as the texture of every confrontation, Scene 7 in which Brod's drunken disclosure delivers the buried truth about Viv's father, and Scene 8 in which Enid's broken-speech confession at Mai's cleaned bedsit finally cracks the eighteen-year silence, Pinnock dramatises the family as a household whose architecture has been the silences the host country imposed, and whose healing requires the slow release of those silences inside the inherited practice the older generation kept.

Scene focus: Enid's North London living room; Enid's assimilationist credo to Brod and the girls; the absent father, the unspoken marriage, the dying off-stage mother all already present in what is not being said.

• Topic sentence: In the play's opening household scene, Pinnock installs the family's silences before installing its conflict — the absent father, the unspoken marriage, the dying off-stage mother all already present in the household as what cannot yet be spoken.

• Technique + evidence: Pinnock deploys suppressed exposition inside Enid's patois assimilationist credo — Enid's Scene 2 "You come here, you try to fit in. Stick to the rules. England been good to me. I proud a my English girls" spoken at home while the absent father, the unspoken marriage, and the unreachable Jamaica all hover around the line — combining suppressed exposition (the family's central truths held off-stage and out of speech, the household built on what is not being said) with the asyndetic imperative chain "You come here, you try to fit in. Stick to the rules" (the host-country survival-grammar Enid has internalised, the missing conjunctions registering the line as a list of conditions rather than a coherent argument, and the substitution of "English girls" for the names of her own daughters closing the family around its silences as the form of its continued existence) inside the same opening.

• Close analysis: The silences are part of the household's architecture rather than gaps in it — the play has been built around a hole at the centre of the family, and the hole is what the next seven scenes will spend their time slowly filling.

• Effect on audience 1: Thus the audience feels the absence of Del and Viv's father as the family's quiet centre — the unfilled chair around which every Scene 2 line of dialogue arranges itself.

• Effect on audience 2: Indeed, the audience recognises that the household has been built on silences the host country has helped to install — the broken father not named, the racism that broke him not described, the Caribbean inheritance not invoked.

• Author's purpose: Pinnock may be using the Scene 2 silences to argue that the diasporic family in 1980s Britain has been structured around the truths the host country has trained it to keep, and that the play's argument will be the slow release of those truths.

• Context (AO3): Caribbean migration to Britain produced households in which the male Windrush figures' breakage was, for many families, a private fact rarely spoken about — the social stigma combined with the host country's silence about the conditions that produced the breakage created exactly the suppressed-exposition shape Pinnock stages in Scene 2.

Scene focus: Mai's bedsit; Brod arrives drunk, complaining about being denied rice and peas; inside the disorder he tells Del the truth about Viv's father.

• Topic sentence: Where Scene 1 had installed the silences as the family's architecture, Scene 7 stages their first crack — the household's hardest truth entrusted to the play's drunkest character, the trickster of disclosure delivering inside disorder what no other register has been able to carry.

• Technique + evidence: Pinnock deploys the trickster function inside antithesis — Brod's Scene 7 disclosure to Del that "I never see a man eyes look so empty. At first him an' you mother share everything … you father change from a smiling boy into a hard man. … If they won't treat him like a human being outside, him make sure she treat him like a king in him own house" — combining trickster function (the truth delivered by a drunken visitor whose disorder is the only register inside which the play's hardest disclosure can travel) with antithesis (the "smiling boy" converting into the "hard man"; the racist "outside" of Smithfield converting into the tyrannical "own house" inside, two parallel reversals naming the household-economy the racist workplace produced) inside the same exchange.

• Close analysis: The verb "change" registers the alteration as something done to the father by the host country rather than as a defect he carried in; the "empty" eyes diagnose the cumulative cost as interior rather than circumstantial, and Brod's coda "no one care what he want to do to a black woman" names the racist host country's indifference as the licensing condition for the household's daily harm.

• Effect on audience 1: Thus the audience feels the disclosure as a release of eighteen years of Del's misreading her family — the absent father at the centre of her childhood now explained as a man broken by Britain rather than as a man Enid drove away.

• Effect on audience 2: Indeed, the audience recognises that the household has entrusted its hardest truth to its drunkest character, and registers Pinnock's argument that disclosure inside disorder is the only register inside which suppressed family history can finally travel.

• Author's purpose: Pinnock may be using Brod's disclosure to argue that the diasporic family's silences have been kept by the older generation out of protection rather than malice — and that the daughters of the household have grown up with explanations they had never been given.

• Context (AO3): The 1981 Brixton uprising and the New Cross fire of January 1981 named publicly what 1980s Black-British men in Britain were already living — the housing discrimination, the policing pressure, the workplace racism that produced outcomes which broke marriages and households — and Brod's disclosure about Viv's father is one such outcome made domestic.

Scene focus: Mai's cleaned bedsit; Enid arrives as Del's first client; the eighteen-year silence about her own marriage and her own mother in Jamaica finally cracks.

• Topic sentence: Where Scene 7 had cracked the silence about Viv's father, Scene 8 cracks the silence about Enid's own marriage and her own mother — the older generation finally able to speak the suppressed truths inside the room the trained daughter has built.

• Technique + evidence: Pinnock deploys broken speech inside anaphoric repetition — Enid's halting confession "Nobody see you, nobody hear you … All the time you screaming, you screaming inside but nobody come" spoken to Del at Mai's cleaned bedsit during the closing palm-reading — combining broken speech (the eighteen-year silence breaking inside a sentence that refuses to assemble whole) with anaphoric repetition (the threefold "nobody" installing the diasporic woman's invisibility as the sentence's structural feature, the doubled "screaming" converting the noiseless surface into an interior register the host country has not heard) inside the same closing exchange.

• Close analysis: The fragmentation is the form of the truth's difficulty — a woman who has not spoken these sentences for eighteen years cannot now speak them whole — while the anaphora makes the host-country indifference into the sentence's repeated subject; the setting transfers the family's disclosure into Mai's cultural-inheritance space, registering that the truth can only be received in a room the older generation built and the younger generation has cleaned.

• Effect on audience 1: Thus the audience hears the Scene 8 fragmentation as the sound of a Windrush mother finally permitted, by her trained daughter, to articulate what eighteen years of host-country suppression had kept unsaid.

• Effect on audience 2: Indeed, the audience receives Del's palm-reading of Enid as the play's quietest reversal — the daughter who once mocked Mai's practice now using it as the room inside which her mother can speak the family's silenced history.

• Author's purpose: Pinnock may be using Enid's confession to argue that family healing in the diasporic household requires the older generation's confession and the younger generation's reception in the same room — and that the silences the host country trained the family to keep can only be broken when the inherited practice has provided the holding space.

• Context (AO3): Leave Taking premiered at the Liverpool Playhouse Studio on 11 November 1987 and was revived at the Bush Theatre in 2018 with Sarah Niles as Enid — the revival opening weeks after the Guardian's reporting on the Windrush deportation scandal, Enid's broken-speech confession registering anew as the diasporic version of the public truth-telling the scandal forced.

• Restated thesis: In conclusion, Pinnock dramatises the family as a diasporic household built around silences the host country imposed — Scene 1 silences installed as the household's architecture, Scene 7 Brod's drunken disclosure cracking the first silence, Scene 8 Enid's broken-speech confession releasing the last.

• Controlling concept: What Scene 1 installed as the household's silences, Scene 7 cracked through the trickster's drunken disclosure, Scene 8 released through the older generation's broken-speech confession — three stages of the same family truth-telling, the silences the host country had trained the household to keep finally speakable inside the inherited practice.

• Author's purpose: Writing in 1987 about the diasporic family's silences and watching her play return to the Bush Theatre in 2018 inside the Windrush deportation scandal, Pinnock argues that family healing in the diaspora requires both the older generation's confession and the younger generation's reception in the same room.

• Ultimate moral: The family in Leave Taking is not the household its silences had pretended to be but the household its disclosures invent — and Mai's cleaned bedsit, with Del's reading hand on Enid's palm, is the diasporic family the play has spent eight scenes building.

T10 — Generational change and cross-generational inheritance

Question

How does Pinnock present the way inheritance passes between generations in Leave Taking?

- how Pinnock presents the older generation

- how Pinnock presents the younger generation

- how Pinnock uses cross-generational inheritance to convey her ideas.

Frame. Generational change in Leave Taking runs at the cross-generational-inheritance angle — NOT the 2025 older-younger conflict angle, which the ExactAngleRepeatPenalty rules out. The play's argument is for transmission rather than collision: what passes between the Windrush generation and the British-born second generation is the inheritance Pinnock has built the eight scenes to track. The plan follows three stages of the same transmission — Scene 1 the older generation's inheritance in three forms (patois, Obeah, the rooster-sound) installed alongside the younger generation's three refusals (silence, theft, dismissal), Scene 5 Mai's "black ants" recruitment as the older generation actively shaping the next, Scene 8 the inheritance transmitted in two complementary forms.

Technique gloss:

• Cross-generational transmission — three forms (patois, Obeah, education) passing in three registers.

• Anagnorisis — Mai's Scene 7 physiognomic recognition ("wide and full between the eyes"; "frontal lobe big where them meet at the nose root"; "And now I see it plain plain") as the older generation recognising the next.

• Recurring object — the handwritten book as transmission in material form.

• Doubled coming-of-age — both generations changing across the eight scenes.

• Cyclical structure — Mai's bedsit at open and close as the room of transmission.

• Stage business — Del's cleaning of the bedsit as the younger generation's preparation to receive.

• Hook (historical fact): Winsome Pinnock, born 1961 to Jamaican parents in Islington, wrote Leave Taking in 1987 inheriting a Caribbean cultural landscape in which African religious memory had survived enslavement, the colonial Obeah Act 1898 and migration — and her staging of cross-generational inheritance between an older Caribbean-born practitioner and a British-born second-generation daughter continues that same Atlantic transmission inside a Deptford bedsit.

• Building sentence (historical context): Trained at the Royal Court Young Writers' Group, Pinnock anchors the inheritance question inside the cumulative legislative chain that had progressively voided the Windrush generation's right to remain (the 1948 British Nationality Act, the 1962 Commonwealth Immigrants Act, the 1971 Immigration Act and the 1981 British Nationality Act) and the 1980s academic emergence of Black Studies in British universities her play's closing scene anticipates.

• Thesis: Across Scene 1 in which the older generation's three inheritance-forms (patois, Obeah, the rooster-sound) install alongside the younger generation's three refusals, Scene 5 in which Mai's "black ants" reading names Del's dyslexia and recruits her into the practice the host country had refused to recognise, and Scene 8 in which the handwritten book passes from Mai ("I don't have a daughter to pass them on to") and Mai's imperative "Is time. You ready" completes the transmission alongside Viv's Black Studies announcement, Pinnock dramatises cross-generational inheritance as the active work both generations have had to do for the diasporic line to continue.

• Topic sentence: In the play's opening, Pinnock places the older generation's three inheritance-forms — patois (Enid's register), Obeah practice (Mai's working life), the rooster's homeland-sound — inside the same room as the younger generation's three first refusals: silence, theft, dismissal.

• Effect on audience 1: Thus the audience hears the inheritance in operation — patois on one side, silence on the other, the rooster's sound in between — before any character has named the transmission.

• Effect on audience 2: Indeed, the audience recognises that cross-generational transmission has already begun even where it looks refused — Del's body's recognition of which object is worth taking is the first signal of the inheritance she will eventually claim.

• Author's purpose: Pinnock may be using Scene 1 to argue that cross-generational inheritance in the diaspora cannot be measured by what the younger generation says about it — the body's recognition precedes the speech's articulation, and the transmission is already in operation before either side has named it.

• Context (AO3): Pinnock said in 2018 that she wrote the play in part to depict "the black British experience as distinct from African American culture" — and the cross-generational inheritance of patois, Obeah, and food across the play's three generations is the dramatic embodiment of that distinct Caribbean-British inheritance.

Scene focus: Mai's bedsit; pregnant Del has moved in; Mai reads aloud what she sees in Del's eyes and names her dyslexia; the older practitioner recruits the younger daughter into the inheritance the host country had pathologised.

• Topic sentence: Where Scene 1 had installed the three inheritance-forms and three refusals as parallel registers, Scene 5 stages the older generation actively shaping the transmission — Mai not merely keeping the inheritance but using it to name what the host country's institutions had refused to recognise in the younger generation.
